1-1.q: Which inventory costing method is allowed under US GAAP but Prohibited under IFRS?
o: FIFO
Weighted Average
LIFO
Specific identification
1-1.e: LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) is allowed under US GAAP but Prohibited under IFRS. Under IFRS, only FIFO and Weighted Average are permitted. This is one of the most tested GAAP vs IFRS differences.

1-1.q: Under IFRS, Pp&E can be measured after acquisition using:
o: Cost model only
Fair value model only
Either the cost model or the revaluation model
Historical cost minus impairment only
1-1.e: Under IFRS, companies can choose the Cost Model (cost minus accumulated depreciation) or the Revaluation Model (revalue to fair value periodically). US GAAP only permits the cost model — no upward revaluation of Pp&E.

1-1.q: Development costs under IFRS are:
o: Always expensed immediately
Capitalized when technical feasibility is established
Never recorded
Expensed only if the project fails
1-1.e: Under IFRS, development costs are Capitalized once technical and commercial feasibility is established. Under US GAAP, all R&D costs (research And development) are expensed as incurred.

1-1.q: US GAAP is Best described as:
o: Principles-based — broad concepts requiring judgment
Rules-based — detailed specific rules and bright lines
Identical to IFRS in all material respects
Only applicable to public companies
1-1.e: US GAAP is Rules-Based — it contains detailed, specific rules and bright lines for many transactions. IFRS is Principles-Based — it provides broader concepts that require more professional judgment in application.

1-1.q: Under IFRS, if inventory is written down below cost and later recovers value:
o: The write-down is permanent — cannot be reversed
The write-down can be Reversed up to the original cost
A new asset must be created
The recovery is reported in OCI only
1-1.e: IFRS allows Reversal of inventory write-downs if Nrv later recovers — but only up to the original cost. US GAAP (ASC 330) does Not allow reversal of inventory write-downs — they are permanent once recorded.

1-1.q: Which statement is True for Both US GAAP and IFRS?
o: LIFO inventory is permitted
Pp&E can be revalued upward
Accrual basis is required for external reporting
Development costs are always expensed
1-1.e: Both US GAAP and IFRS require the Accrual Basis for external financial reporting. The other options describe differences: LIFO = GAAP only; Pp&E revaluation = IFRS only; development cost capitalization = IFRS only.

1-1.q: Revenue recognition under ASC 606 (GAAP) and IFRS 15 is:
o: Completely different — opposite approaches
Substantially converged — both use the 5-step model
Only applicable to product sales, not services
Based on cash receipts under both standards
1-1.e: ASC 606 and IFRS 15 were a Joint Project between FASB and IASB, resulting in Substantially Converged standards. Both use the same 5-step revenue recognition model, making this one of the most aligned areas between GAAP and IFRS.

1-1.q: Under US GAAP, extraordinary items are:
o: Required to be shown separately after income from continuing operations
Still permitted for truly unusual items
Eliminated — no longer presented separately
Shown in OCI
1-1.e: US GAAP Eliminated extraordinary items (ASU 2015-01). Items previously classified as extraordinary are now classified and presented within continuing operations. IFRS never had the concept of extraordinary items.

1-1.q: The IASB (International Accounting Standards Board) issues:
o: US GAAP
IFRS
FASB standards
SEC regulations
1-1.e: IASB issues IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards), used in 140+ countries. FASB (Financial Accounting Standards Board) issues US GAAP, used in the United States.

1-1.q: Under IFRS, the revaluation model for Pp&E allows assets to be reported at:
o: Historical cost minus accumulated depreciation
Fair value at the reporting date
The higher of cost or fair value
Original cost only
1-1.e: Under the IFRS revaluation model, Pp&E is carried at Fair Value at the date of revaluation, less subsequent accumulated depreciation and impairment. Any increase goes to revaluation surplus in OCI (equity). US GAAP does not permit this upward revaluation.

1-1.q: Under IFRS, development costs Must be capitalized when:
o: The project is first conceived
Technical and commercial feasibility has been Established and the entity intends and is able to complete the asset
The asset is placed in service
Management approves the project budget
1-1.e: IFRS Ias 38: development costs are Capitalized once 6 criteria are met — technical feasibility, intention to complete, ability to use/sell, probable future economic benefits, adequate resources, ability to measure expenditure reliably. Under US GAAP, All research And development costs are Expensed as incurred (except for software development costs under specific rules). This is a major IFRS/GAAP difference.

1-1.q: LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) inventory method is:
o: Required under IFRS for all companies
Permitted under both IFRS and US GAAP
Prohibited Under IFRS — allowed only under US GAAP
Required under US GAAP for all companies
1-1.e: LIFO: Prohibited under IFRS (Ias 2). Permitted under US GAAP. This is one of the most tested IFRS/GAAP differences. Implication: a US company reporting under GAAP can use LIFO (which during inflation reduces taxable income). An IFRS company cannot. If a GAAP company using LIFO converts to IFRS, it must restate inventory using FIFO or weighted average.

1-1.q: Under IFRS, upward Revaluation of Pp&E (Property, Plant & Equipment) is:
o: Prohibited — same as US GAAP
Permitted under Ias 16 — excess over historical cost is recorded in OCI as a revaluation surplus
Required for all Pp&E annually
Recorded directly in net income
1-1.e: IFRS Ias 16 Revaluation Model: companies may revalue Pp&E to Fair Value. Increases above historical cost → recorded in OCI (revaluation surplus in equity). Decreases → expense to income first; any excess reduces revaluation surplus. US GAAP: No upward revaluation of Pp&E — assets carried at historical cost less accumulated depreciation. Once impaired under GAAP, no reversal is permitted.

1-1.q: The reversal of inventory write-downs is:
o: Prohibited under both IFRS and US GAAP
Permitted under IFRS (Ias 2) if the net realizable value subsequently recovers; Prohibited under US GAAP
Required under IFRS but prohibited under GAAP
Permitted under GAAP but prohibited under IFRS
1-1.e: Inventory Write-Down Reversal: IFRS Ias 2 Allows reversal if Nrv subsequently recovers — reversed to the Lower of original cost or new Nrv, recognized as income. US GAAP (ASC 330): write-downs create a New Cost Basis — reversals are Prohibited even if value recovers. Key exam point: IFRS more 'current value' oriented; GAAP more conservative (once written down, stays down).

1-1.q: IFRS is Best described as being:
o: Rules-based with detailed specific guidance for every situation
Principles-Based — setting broad principles and requiring professional judgment for application
Identical to US GAAP in all material respects
Less comprehensive than US GAAP
1-1.e: IFRS = Principles-Based: provides broad conceptual principles, requiring professional judgment in application. US GAAP = Rules-Based: highly detailed, specific guidance and bright-line tests for many situations. Implications: IFRS requires more judgment; GAAP provides more prescriptive guidance reducing comparability differences but creating complex rules. The CMA exam expects knowledge of both frameworks and their philosophical difference.

1-1.q: Under IFRS, interest costs on borrowings to construct a qualifying asset:
o: Are always expensed immediately
Must Be Capitalized as part of the asset cost — same as US GAAP
Are capitalized only if management elects to do so
Are disclosed in notes but never capitalized
1-1.e: Borrowing Costs: Ias 23 Requires capitalization of borrowing costs directly attributable to acquiring, constructing, or producing a qualifying asset — same policy as US GAAP (ASC 835-20). A qualifying asset takes a substantial period to prepare for use. Capitalization begins when: expenditures are incurred, borrowing costs are incurred, and preparation activities are underway. Ceases when substantially complete.

1-1.q: Under US GAAP, goodwill arising from a business combination is:
o: Amortized over its useful life, up to 40 years
Amortized over 15 years for tax purposes only — no book amortization for public companies
Tested For Impairment Annually (or when indicators exist) — not amortized (for public companies)
Written off immediately in the period of acquisition
1-1.e: US GAAP Goodwill (ASC 350): Public Companies test goodwill for Impairment Annually — not amortized. Private Companies may elect to amortize over 10 years (private company alternative). IFRS (IFRS 3): Also prohibits amortization and requires annual impairment testing. Note: both GAAP and IFRS prohibit goodwill amortization for public companies, but US GAAP previously required amortization before Sfas 142 (2002).

1-1.q: Which of the following is an area where IFRS and US GAAP have Converged (reached similar treatment)?
o: Inventory methods (LIFO treatment)
Pp&E revaluation
Business Combinations — both use the Acquisition Method, recognizing 100% of the fair value of assets acquired and liabilities assumed
Development cost capitalization
1-1.e: Converged Areas: Business Combinations (IFRS 3 / ASC 805) — both require the Acquisition Method (purchase price allocation at fair value, goodwill recognition). Areas still Diverged: LIFO (GAAP allows, IFRS prohibits), Pp&E revaluation (IFRS allows, GAAP prohibits), inventory write-down reversal (IFRS allows, GAAP prohibits), development costs (IFRS capitalizes when criteria met, GAAP expenses). Convergence has reduced but not eliminated differences.

1-1.q: Under IFRS, the impairment of long-lived assets uses which recovery model?
o: Cost model only — no recovery once impaired
Recoverable Amount model (higher of fair value less costs to sell vs. value in use) — and Reversals Are Permitted for assets other than goodwill
Historical cost less accumulated depreciation only
Net realizable value model identical to GAAP
1-1.e: IFRS Impairment (Ias 36): Recoverable Amount = higher of (1) Fair Value Less Costs of Disposal, and (2) Value in Use (PV of future cash flows). Impairment = Carrying Amount − Recoverable Amount. Reversals Are Permitted (except goodwill) if recoverable amount subsequently increases — reversed to the lower of revised carrying amount or original cost less depreciation. US GAAP: impairment losses for most long-lived assets Cannot be reversed — a key difference.

1-1.q: A company following IFRS presents its balance sheet. Under IFRS, assets are Typically listed in which order?
o: Most liquid first (cash, then receivables, then inventory, etc.) — same as US GAAP
Least Liquid First — non-current assets presented before current assets (common IFRS presentation, opposite of US GAAP)
Alphabetical order
By size from largest to smallest
1-1.e: IFRS Balance Sheet Order: IFRS does Not mandate a specific order — companies may present in order of Liquidity Or Reverse Liquidity. In practice, many IFRS companies present Non-Current assets first (plant, equipment, intangibles) then current assets — opposite of US GAAP convention (which presents most-liquid first: cash, Ar, inventory, then long-term). This presentation difference is frequently tested on the CMA exam.

1-2.q: The Primary external users of financial statements are:
o: Management and the board of directors
Investors and creditors
Employees and regulators only
The general public
1-2.e: The Primary external users are Investors (equity holders) and Creditors (lenders). The conceptual framework is designed primarily to serve their decision-making needs — investment, credit, and capital allocation decisions.

1-2.q: The two Fundamental qualitative characteristics of useful financial information are:
o: Timeliness and understandability
Comparability and verifiability
Relevance and faithful representation
Materiality and consistency
1-2.e: Relevance (making a difference in decisions) and Faithful Representation (complete, neutral, free from error) are the two Fundamental qualitative characteristics. The others (comparability, verifiability, timeliness, understandability) are Enhancing characteristics.

1-2.q: Information is Material when:
o: It relates to a large dollar amount
Omitting or misstating it could influence the decisions of users
It is disclosed in the notes
It relates to continuing operations
1-2.e: Materiality is a component of Relevance. Information is material if Omitting Or Misstating it could Influence Decisions of users. Materiality is entity-specific — it depends on the size and nature of the item relative to the entity.

1-2.q: The Objective of financial reporting is to provide information useful for:
o: Tax compliance only
Investment and credit decisions, and assessing future cash flows
Internal management decisions
Setting regulatory policy
1-2.e: The primary objective of financial reporting is to provide information useful for Investment And Credit Decisions and to help users assess the entity's ability to generate future Cash Flows. It is focused on external decision-making.

1-2.q: Faithful Representation in financial reporting means information is:
o: Always conservative
Complete, neutral, and free from material error
Based on the latest market values
Supported by management estimates only
1-2.e: Faithful representation requires information to be: Complete (all necessary information included), Neutral (no bias toward a particular outcome), and Free From Material Error (not necessarily perfect, but accurate enough). It is one of the two fundamental qualitative characteristics.

1-2.q: The Primary users of general-purpose financial statements according to the IASB and FASB conceptual frameworks are:
o: Employees and suppliers
Government agencies and regulators
Existing And Potential Investors And Creditors — those who cannot demand special-purpose reports directly from the company
Management and the board of directors
1-2.e: Primary Users: the conceptual frameworks of both IASB and FASB identify existing and potential Investors and Creditors (lenders) as the primary users of general-purpose financial statements. Management has direct access to internal information; government can demand special reports; employees typically have less power. Financial statements are designed primarily to serve investors and creditors making resource allocation decisions.

1-2.q: Relevance as a fundamental qualitative characteristic of financial information requires that information:
o: Be verifiable by an independent party
Be consistent from period to period
Be Capable Of Making A Difference in users' decisions — having predictive value, confirmatory value, or both
Be presented in a format users find easy to understand
1-2.e: Relevance: financial information is relevant if it is Capable Of Making A Difference in users' decisions. Two components: (1) Predictive Value — helps users forecast future outcomes; (2) Confirmatory Value — helps users confirm or correct prior evaluations. Both can be present simultaneously (current earnings are relevant for predicting future earnings And confirming past forecasts). Materiality is an entity-specific aspect of relevance.

1-2.q: Faithful Representation requires financial information to be:
o: Relevant, timely, and comparable
Complete, Neutral, and Free From Error — accurately depicting the economic phenomena it purports to represent
Verified by an external auditor
Presented in the most favorable light for the company
1-2.e: Faithful Representation: the three components are: (1) Complete — all necessary information is included (including descriptions and explanations); (2) Neutral — without bias in selection or presentation; (3) Free From Error — no errors in describing the phenomenon, although estimates may involve uncertainty. Faithful representation replaced 'reliability' in the 2010 conceptual framework update. Prudence (conservatism) was re-introduced as part of neutrality.

1-2.q: Comparability as an enhancing qualitative characteristic means:
o: All companies must use identical accounting methods
Information must be consistent with the prior year only
Information IS More Useful When IT Can Be Compared with similar information about other entities or across periods — enabling identification of similarities and differences
Financial statements must be presented in a standard format mandated by regulators
1-2.e: Comparability: users can identify and understand Similarities And Differences between entities or periods. Requires: consistent accounting policies (period-to-period), disclosure of policy changes, uniform application of principles. Comparability ≠ uniformity (different methods may be equally comparable if disclosed). Facilitates: industry analysis, period-to-period trend analysis, benchmarking against peers.

1-2.q: The Materiality concept means that:
o: All information must be disclosed regardless of size
Only information about large dollar amounts needs to be disclosed
Information IS Material If Omitting Or Misstating IT could reasonably be expected to influence decisions of primary users
Only items above 5% of net income are material
1-2.e: Materiality: entity-specific aspect of Relevance — there is no bright-line percentage. Materiality is based on whether omitting, misstating, or obscuring information could reasonably be expected to influence the decisions of primary users. Factors: size (dollar amount relative to key financial statement totals) And nature (e.g., fraud may be material even if small). Management, auditors, and regulators use judgment — no universal threshold.

1-2.q: Timeliness as an enhancing qualitative characteristic means:
o: Financial statements must be published within 30 days of period-end
Information must cover exactly 12-month periods
Information IS Available In Time to be capable of influencing decisions — older information loses its relevance even if still accurate
Reports must be prepared simultaneously for all users
1-2.e: Timeliness: information must be available Before IT Loses Its Capacity to influence decisions. The more time that passes since the information was generated, the less timely and less useful it becomes. Trade-off with faithfulness: very timely information may be less complete/accurate (preliminary estimates). Quarterly reporting for public companies is the balance between timeliness and completeness.

1-2.q: The Cost Constraint in financial reporting means:
o: Companies may only disclose information that costs nothing to prepare
Financial Information Should Only Be Provided when the Benefits of that information outweigh the Costs of providing it
All disclosures are free and therefore should be maximized
Standards setters always require disclosure regardless of cost
1-2.e: Cost Constraint (formerly 'cost-benefit'): the pervasive constraint in financial reporting. Even if information is relevant and faithfully represented, it need not be provided if Costs of preparation and disclosure exceed Benefits to users. Costs include: gathering, processing, verifying, disseminating, and potential competitive harm from disclosure. Benefits: improved decision-making by primary users. Standards setters weigh this when requiring disclosures.

1-2.q: Understandability as an enhancing qualitative characteristic assumes that:
o: Financial information should be simplified so anyone can understand it without expertise
Complex transactions may be excluded from financial statements if they are too difficult to explain
Information IS Classified, Characterized, And Presented Clearly for users who have Reasonable Financial Knowledge and are willing to study the information
Financial statements must be written in plain language without technical terms
1-2.e: Understandability: information must be classified and presented clearly for users who have Reasonable Knowledge of business and economics and who are Willing To Study the information. This does not mean eliminating complex but relevant information — complex items should be Explained, not omitted. The standard assumes a reasonably sophisticated user, not the average member of the public.

1-2.q: Verifiability as an enhancing qualitative characteristic means:
o: Only auditors can verify financial information
Different Independent Observers could reach Consensus that the information faithfully represents the economic phenomena it purports to depict
Financial statements must be verified by a government agency
Only historical cost information can be verified
1-2.e: Verifiability: independent observers with different viewpoints could reach substantial Agreement that the information represents what it purports to represent. Direct verification: check a bank statement balance. Indirect verification: verify inventory valuation by checking inputs and methodology of the valuation model. Note: fair value estimates may be harder to verify than historical cost — this is why Verifiability is an Enhancing (not fundamental) characteristic.

1-2.q: The Objective of general-purpose financial reporting is primarily to:
o: Demonstrate management's stewardship of resources and maximize reported earnings
Comply with tax and regulatory requirements
Provide Financial Information About The Reporting Entity that is Useful to existing and potential Investors and Creditors in making decisions about providing resources to the entity
Present a complete picture of all business activities to all possible users
1-2.e: Objective (Conceptual Framework Chapter 1): provide Financial Information that is useful to Primary Users (investors and creditors) in making decisions about: (1) providing resources — buying/selling equity, lending/not lending; (2) exercising their rights — voting, monitoring management. Secondary: assess management's stewardship (accountability). Not: demonstrate legal compliance, satisfy all possible users, or maximize reported performance.

1-2.q: The Going Concern assumption in financial reporting means that:
o: The company will remain profitable indefinitely
The financial statements assume the entity Will Continue Operating for the Foreseeable Future — assets are valued on a use basis, not liquidation basis
Assets must be stated at their current market value
The company has no significant risks or uncertainties
1-2.e: Going Concern: the foundational assumption that the entity will continue operating for the foreseeable future — long enough to fulfill its objectives and commitments. Implications: assets valued at historical cost or use-value (not liquidation value), liabilities classified as current/non-current. If going concern is in doubt: assets may need to be written down to Nrv, liabilities reclassified as current, disclosures added. Auditors evaluate going concern each period.

1-2.q: Accrual Basis accounting, as required for external financial reporting, means that:
o: Revenue is recognized when cash is received and expenses when cash is paid
Revenues And Expenses are recognized when Earned Or Incurred — not necessarily when cash changes hands
Only cash transactions are recorded in financial statements
Monthly bank statements are the primary source for financial reporting
1-2.e: Accrual Basis: revenue recognized when Earned (performance obligation satisfied), expenses recognized when Incurred (benefit consumed), regardless of cash timing. Required under both GAAP and IFRS for external reporting. Alternative: Cash Basis (revenue when received, expense when paid) — used for some small businesses and tax reporting, but Not acceptable for GAAP/IFRS financial statements. Accrual basis provides a more complete picture of economic activity.

1-2.q: The Economic Entity Assumption means:
o: All related companies must be consolidated
A single set of financial statements covers all entities in a country
The Activities Of The Business Are Separate from the activities of its owner(s) — even for sole proprietorships
The entity uses only one accounting method throughout its life
1-2.e: Economic Entity Assumption: the business is a Separate Accounting Unit from its owners. The owner's personal assets and liabilities are Not included in the business financial statements. Applies to: sole proprietorships (legally one entity, but separate for accounting), partnerships, corporations. Basis for: consolidated financial statements (group treated as one economic entity), segment reporting (treat each segment separately for reporting purposes).

1-2.q: Primary Qualitative Characteristics that make financial information useful include which two Fundamental characteristics?
o: Comparability and Verifiability
Timeliness and Understandability
Relevance and Faithful Representation — the two fundamental characteristics in both IASB and FASB frameworks
Materiality and Cost-Benefit
1-2.e: Fundamental Characteristics (both IASB and FASB): (1) Relevance — capable of making a difference in decisions (predictive + confirmatory value, materiality); (2) Faithful Representation — complete, neutral, free from error. Enhancing characteristics (improve usefulness but are secondary): Comparability, Verifiability, Timeliness, Understandability. The enhancing characteristics cannot make irrelevant or unfaithfully represented information useful.

1-2.q: Creditors who are Primary Users of financial statements are most interested in:
o: Management's compensation plan and long-term incentives
Details of the company's research and development projects
The company's Ability To Generate Cash Flows sufficient to repay debt and meet interest obligations
The company's share price performance relative to competitors
1-2.e: Creditors (lenders) focus on: (1) Liquidity — can the company pay short-term obligations? (current ratio, quick ratio); (2) Solvency — can it repay long-term debt? (debt-to-equity, interest coverage); (3) Cash Flow Generation — is cash flow sufficient to service debt? (operating cash flow); (4) Collateral — what assets back the loan? Different from investors who focus on profitability and growth. This explains why both the balance sheet (solvency) and Scf (cash generation) are particularly important to creditors.

1-3.q: The Balance Sheet reports financial position as of:
o: A period of time (e.g., year ended Dec 31)
A specific point in time (e.g., as of Dec 31)
The average of the period
When cash is received
1-3.e: The Balance Sheet (Statement of Financial Position) reports assets, liabilities, and equity at a Specific Point In Time — e.g., 'As of December 31, 20XX.' The IS, Scf, and Sce cover a Period of time.

1-3.q: Net Income from the Income Statement flows to which part of the Balance Sheet?
o: Paid-in capital
Retained earnings
Long-term liabilities
Accumulated OCI
1-3.e: Net Income flows from the Income Statement to Retained Earnings in the equity section of the Balance Sheet. Retained Earnings = Beginning Re + Net Income − Dividends.

1-3.q: If a company has Total Assets = $500,000 and Total Liabilities = $300,000, Total Equity =
1-3.e: Assets = Liabilities + Equity. $500,000 = $300,000 + Equity. Equity = $500,000 − $300,000 = $200,000.

1-3.q: Which financial statement shows changes in all equity components including OCI?
o: Balance Sheet
Income Statement
Statement of Cash Flows
Statement of Changes in Equity
1-3.e: The Statement Of Changes In Equity shows all changes in equity components: common stock, Apic, retained earnings, treasury stock, and accumulated OCI. It bridges the beginning and ending equity balances on the Balance Sheet.

1-3.q: The ending cash balance on the Statement of Cash Flows should equal:
o: Net income for the period
Cash on the Balance Sheet at period end
Total current assets
Cash paid for financing activities
1-3.e: The Ending Cash Balance on the Scf must equal the Cash balance reported on the Balance Sheet at the same date. This is a key reconciliation — the Scf explains how cash changed from beginning to ending balance.

1-3.q: Gains and Losses differ from Revenues and Expenses because gains/losses arise from:
o: Primary business operations
Peripheral or incidental activities
Investing activities only
Non-operating items only
1-3.e: Gains and Losses arise from Peripheral or Incidental activities (e.g., sale of equipment, lawsuit settlement). Revenues and Expenses arise from the entity's Primary Business Operations (e.g., sales, COGS, S&A).

1-3.q: The Accounting Equation is Best stated as:
o: Revenue − Expenses = Net Income
Assets = Liabilities + Shareholders' Equity
Cash = Assets − Liabilities
Net Income = Assets + Liabilities
1-3.e: The fundamental Accounting Equation: Assets = Liabilities + Shareholders' Equity. This equation Always balances — every transaction affects at least two accounts maintaining this equality.

1-3.q: The Balance Sheet is described as a 'Point In Time' statement because:
o: It takes a long time to prepare
It measures performance over a fiscal year
IT Shows The Financial Position (assets, liabilities, equity) as of a Specific Date — not over a period
It balances to zero
1-3.e: Balance Sheet: presents financial position As Of a specific date (e.g., December 31, 2024). It is a Snapshot — what the company owns, owes, and the owners' residual interest at that moment. Contrast with Period statements (Income Statement, Scf, Statement of Equity) which cover a From/To period (e.g., year ended December 31, 2024). The date caption distinguishes it from period-based statements.

1-3.q: Net income from the Income Statement Flows to which other financial statement?
o: Statement of Cash Flows — as the starting point for operating activities
Statement Of Owners' Equity — increasing retained earnings, and then to the Balance Sheet
Balance Sheet — directly as a separate line item
Both the Scf and Balance Sheet independently without going through equity
1-3.e: Net Income Flow: Income Statement (NI) → Statement Of Owners' Equity (increases Retained Earnings) → Balance Sheet (Retained Earnings is a component of equity). The Scf also starts with Net Income (indirect method) but this is an adjustment for cash flow purposes, not a direct balance sheet flow. The ultimate destination of net income on the balance sheet is through the Retained Earnings account.

1-3.q: The ending balance of Cash on the Statement of Cash Flows Must equal:
o: Cash and marketable securities on the balance sheet
Net income plus depreciation
The Cash (And Cash Equivalents) Balance On The Balance Sheet at the same date — this is a critical articulation between the two statements
Total current assets divided by current liabilities
1-3.e: Scf ↔ Balance Sheet Articulation: the closing cash balance on the Statement of Cash Flows = the Cash and Cash Equivalents balance on the Balance Sheet at the same date. This is a fundamental check on the accuracy of the Scf. Cash equivalents = short-term, highly liquid investments convertible to known cash amounts, with original maturities of 3 months or less (T-bills, money market funds).

1-3.q: The Accounting Equation (Assets = Liabilities + Equity) is Always true because:
o: It is defined by law and enforced by regulators
It is an assumption that may be violated in practice
Every Transaction Affects At Least Two Accounts such that both sides always remain equal — the equation is mathematically self-balancing
It only applies to publicly traded companies
1-3.e: Double-Entry Bookkeeping: every transaction affects Two Or More accounts equally — the accounting equation is Always in balance. Buy equipment for cash: Assets increase (equipment), Assets decrease (cash) — net asset effect = zero, but equation still balances. Take a loan: Assets increase (cash), Liabilities increase (loan) — equation adds equal amounts to both sides. The equation cannot be violated if double-entry is applied correctly.

1-3.q: The Statement Of Cash Flows provides information that Cannot be obtained from the Income Statement because:
o: The Scf contains more detail about revenues and expenses
The Income Statement is only for internal use
The Scf Shows Actual Cash Inflows And Outflows — a company can report net income while experiencing negative cash flows due to accrual accounting differences
The Scf is prepared on a cash basis and the IS on an accrual basis, so they are interchangeable
1-3.e: Scf vs Income Statement: the Income Statement uses Accrual Basis — revenue when earned, expense when incurred. A company can be profitable on an accrual basis but cash-poor if: receivables are growing (revenue earned but not collected), inventory is building (cash spent on goods not yet sold), or large capital expenditures are made. The Scf reveals the Actual Cash position — critical for assessing liquidity and viability.

1-3.q: Revenues in the accounting framework Increase Equity because:
o: They represent cash received from customers
Revenue is always immediately paid out as dividends
Revenues Increase Net Assets (assets increase or liabilities decrease) — the net asset increase flows to retained earnings, increasing owners' equity
They reduce the company's liabilities
1-3.e: Accounting Equation Dynamics: Revenue → increases assets (cash/Ar) or decreases liabilities (deferred revenue) → increases Net Assets → increases Retained Earnings → increases Equity. The income statement links to equity through retained earnings: Revenues increase NI → NI increases Retained Earnings → Retained Earnings increases Total Equity. This is why the income statement and balance sheet are interrelated.

1-3.q: The Statement Of Owners' Equity reconciles which beginning and ending balances?
o: Beginning and ending retained earnings only
Beginning and ending cash balances
The Beginning And Ending Balances Of All Equity Accounts — paid-in capital, retained earnings, Aoci, treasury stock — showing all changes during the period
Beginning and ending asset and liability balances
1-3.e: Statement Of Owners' Equity: shows the Rollforward of All equity components during the period. Changes include: +Net Income, -Dividends, +/- OCI, +Stock issuances, -Treasury stock purchases. It Reconciles: Beginning Balance + All Changes = Ending Balance for each equity component. The ending equity balance must tie to the Balance Sheet equity total — another key articulation.

1-3.q: Expenses in the accounting framework Decrease Equity because:
o: They always represent cash outflows from the business
They are deducted from revenue to calculate net income
Expenses Decrease Net Assets (assets decrease or liabilities increase) → decrease retained earnings → decrease equity
Expenses represent owner withdrawals
1-3.e: Accounting Equation Dynamics: Expenses → decrease assets (cash, prepaid) or increase liabilities (accrued expense) → decrease Net Assets → decrease Retained Earnings → decrease Equity. This linkage explains why: paying rent reduces cash (asset) → reduces net assets → reduces equity. Even if not yet paid, accruing rent increases liabilities → reduces net assets → reduces equity. The income statement and balance sheet are continuously linked through this mechanism.

1-3.q: Which financial statement covers a Period of time (has 'from/to' date captions)?
o: Balance Sheet only
Balance Sheet and Notes to Financial Statements
Income Statement, Statement Of Cash Flows, and Statement Of Owners' Equity — all three cover activity during a period
Only the Statement of Cash Flows
1-3.e: Period vs Point-In-Time: Period Statements (cover activity during a time period, e.g., 'For the Year Ended December 31, 2024'): Income Statement, Statement of Cash Flows, Statement of Owners' Equity. Point-In-Time (balance as of a date, e.g., 'As of December 31, 2024'): Balance Sheet / Statement of Financial Position. This distinction is fundamental for understanding what each statement conveys.

1-3.q: In the accounting equation, Liabilities are best described as:
o: Amounts owed to the company's shareholders
Future economic benefits owned by the company
Present Obligations to transfer economic resources as a result of Past Events — amounts owed to parties other than owners
The residual interest in assets after deducting liabilities
1-3.e: Liabilities (conceptual definition): present Obligations of the entity to transfer economic resources to others (creditors, suppliers, employees, tax authorities) arising from Past Transactions or Events. Examples: accounts payable (received goods, obligated to pay), bonds payable (received cash, obligated to repay), deferred revenue (received cash, obligated to perform). Not equity (owners' claims) and Not future plans (purchasing equipment next year creates no present obligation).

1-3.q: The Purpose of the Statement of Cash Flows is to provide information about:
o: The company's profitability over the period
The company's assets and liabilities at period end
The Company'S Cash Receipts And Payments during the period — classified as operating, investing, and financing — explaining the change in the cash balance
How the company calculates its tax obligation
1-3.e: Scf Purpose: shows how the company Generated and Used Cash during the period. Three sections: (1) Operating — day-to-day business activities; (2) Investing — buying/selling long-term assets and investments; (3) Financing — borrowing, repaying debt, issuing stock, paying dividends. Explains the change from beginning to ending cash balance. Critical for liquidity assessment — a company can be profitable but unable to pay bills if cash flows are negative.

1-3.q: Gains in the accounting framework differ from Revenues because gains:
o: Are larger amounts than revenues
Are recognized on a cash basis while revenues use accrual basis
Arise From Peripheral Or Incidental Activities — not the primary business operations — such as selling a fixed asset above book value
Are not reported on the income statement
1-3.e: Revenues vs Gains: Revenues arise from the entity's Primary Business Activities (selling products, providing services — the core operations). Gains arise from Peripheral or Incidental transactions — selling a delivery truck, selling an investment, insurance recovery for a loss. Both increase equity through the income statement, but their source differs. Disclosure matters: separately identified on the income statement to reveal the quality of earnings.

1-3.q: The Notes To Financial Statements are:
o: Optional supplementary information that companies may provide
Only required by publicly traded companies
An Integral Part Of The Financial Statements — required and providing context, detail, and explanations necessary for a complete understanding of the face amounts
Prepared separately from the financial statements by management's choice
1-3.e: Notes To Financial Statements: Required and Integral — the financial statements are Not complete without the notes. Notes provide: (1) Accounting Policies — basis of preparation, significant policies; (2) Detail supporting face amounts (Ar aging, debt maturity schedules); (3) Contingencies; (4) Subsequent Events; (5) Related Party transactions; (6) Segment information. A reader who relies only on face amounts without reading notes has an incomplete understanding.

1-4.q: An item is Recognized in financial statements when it:
o: Is approved by the board of directors
Meets the definition of an element And can be measured reliably
Is disclosed in the notes to the FS
Is approved by the auditor
1-4.e: Recognition requires meeting the Definition of a financial statement element And having a Reliable Measurement. Merely disclosing in the notes is not the same as recognition — recognition means recording on the face of the statements.

1-4.q: Inventory is written down to Nrv because of the:
o: Historical cost principle
Matching principle
Lower of Cost or Nrv (Lcnrv) rule and conservatism
Revenue recognition principle
1-4.e: Inventory is written down to Nrv (Net Realizable Value) when Nrv falls below cost. This applies the Lcnrv rule, which reflects Conservatism — do not overstate assets. Assets should not be carried at more than their recoverable value.

1-4.q: The Going Concern assumption means:
o: The entity will be liquidated within one year
The entity will continue operations for the foreseeable future
All assets are measured at liquidation value
The entity is profitable
1-4.e: Going Concern assumes the entity will Continue Operations for the foreseeable future. If going concern is in doubt, assets must be revalued to liquidation values and disclosures are required. Most financial statements are prepared assuming going concern.

1-4.q: The Cost-Benefit constraint in financial reporting means:
o: Costs must always be reported before benefits
The benefits of providing information must exceed the cost of providing it
Only low-cost information needs disclosure
Expensive assets get special treatment
1-4.e: The Cost-Benefit constraint (also called cost constraint): the benefits of providing financial information must Exceed The Cost of providing it. This is why not all conceivable information is disclosed — only that which is worth the cost to prepare and use.

1-4.q: Fair value is Best defined as:
o: The amount paid for an asset at acquisition
The book value of an asset
The price in an orderly transaction between market participants
The replacement cost of an asset
1-4.e: Fair value = the price that would be received to Sell An Asset or paid to transfer a Liability in an Orderly Transaction between Market Participants at the measurement date. Fair value is market-based, not entity-specific.

1-4.q: The Historical Cost measurement basis records assets at:
o: The current market value at each balance sheet date
The present value of future cash flows the asset will generate
The Amount Paid (or fair value of consideration given) At The Time Of Acquisition — not updated for subsequent market changes
The replacement cost of the asset at reporting date
1-4.e: Historical Cost: assets recorded at the Price Paid at acquisition and generally Not updated for subsequent changes in value (except for impairment write-downs). Most assets in GAAP financial statements use historical cost basis: Pp&E, most intangibles, inventory (at cost then Lcnrv). Advantages: objective, verifiable, reliable. Disadvantage: may not reflect current economic reality. Contrast with: Fair Value (mark to market), Nrv (net realizable value for inventory).

1-4.q: Fair Value as a measurement basis is Most commonly used for which category of assets?
o: Property, Plant & Equipment (at historical cost less depreciation)
Inventory (at lower of cost or Nrv)
Financial Instruments — trading securities, available-for-sale securities, and derivatives are measured at fair value with changes recognized in income or OCI
Accounts receivable (at net realizable value)
1-4.e: Fair Value Measurement: most commonly applied to Financial Instruments. Under GAAP: Trading Securities → fair value, changes to Net Income; Available-For-Sale Securities → fair value, changes to OCI; Derivatives → fair value, changes to income (or OCI for designated hedges). Pp&E at historical cost (GAAP) or revalued amount (IFRS option). Inventory at Lcnrv. Ar at Nrv. Fair value is ASC 820's definition: exit price in an orderly transaction between market participants.

1-4.q: The Going Concern assumption affects financial statement preparation because:
o: It requires all assets to be stated at liquidation values
Assets Are Valued On A Use Basis and liabilities classified as current/non-current based on their expected settlement — inappropriate if the entity will liquidate
It eliminates the need for depreciation
It requires management to guarantee the company will be profitable
1-4.e: Going Concern Impact: if going concern is assumed, assets are valued at amounts useful in Operations (historical cost, Nrv, etc.) — Not liquidation values. If going concern is in doubt: (1) Assets may need to be written down to liquidation/distress values; (2) Long-term liabilities may need to be reclassified as Current (if covenants are violated or the company may not survive to pay them); (3) Additional Disclosures required. This is a fundamental assumption that changes the entire accounting model.

1-4.q: Net Realizable Value (Nrv) as a measurement basis is Primarily used for:
o: Long-term investments in equity securities
Property, plant and equipment
Inventory — written down to Nrv when Nrv < cost (Lower of Cost or Net Realizable Value rule)
Bonds payable
1-4.e: Nrv = Estimated Selling Price less Estimated Costs To Complete And Sell. Used for: (1) Inventory — if Nrv < cost, write down to Nrv (GAAP: Lower of Cost or Nrv; IFRS: same concept). (2) Accounts Receivable — Ar is stated at Nrv (net of allowance for doubtful accounts) = the amount expected to be collected. (3) Contract assets. Nrv is a specific measurement basis for assets whose economic benefit will be realized through Sale rather than Use.

1-4.q: The Monetary Unit Assumption in financial reporting means:
o: All companies must report in US dollars
Financial information is quantifiable in Money Terms and reported in a common monetary unit — and ignores the effects of inflation on that monetary unit
Transactions must be settled in cash only
Exchange rates must be fixed for reporting purposes
1-4.e: Monetary Unit Assumption: (1) Quantifiability — only transactions and events expressible in Money Terms are recorded (valuable employee skills, business relationships, brand culture are not recognized as assets because they can't be reliably measured in money); (2) Stable Monetary Unit — assumes the dollar is a stable measure (ignores inflation). Implication: a building purchased in 1980 for $1M is still shown at historical cost (or cost less depreciation) — inflation adjustments are not made to maintain a stable measurement unit.

1-4.q: The Periodicity Assumption in financial reporting requires that:
o: Financial statements must cover exactly 12-month periods
The accounting period must align with the calendar year
Financial Information Can And Should Be Reported For Discrete Time Periods — even though the entity continues as a going concern indefinitely
All transactions must be recorded on a daily basis
1-4.e: Periodicity (Time Period Assumption): for decision-making purposes, economic activity is Divided Into Discrete Time Periods — annual, quarterly, monthly. This creates the need for: Accruals (recognizing revenue/expense before cash), Deferrals (postponing recognition to future periods), Allocations (spreading costs over periods — depreciation). Without periodicity, financial statements would only be prepared at the end of the entity's life.

1-4.q: Recognition of an item in financial statements requires that:
o: The item has been approved by the board of directors
The Item Meets The Definition Of An Element (asset, liability, revenue, expense) And can be Measured Reliably with a Probable future benefit or obligation
The item has been audited by external auditors
The item has resulted in a cash transaction
1-4.e: Recognition Criteria: to recognize an item on the face of the financial statements (not just in notes): (1) Meets the Definition of an element (asset = future economic benefit controlled by the entity from a past event); (2) Probable future economic benefit/sacrifice; (3) Reliable Measurement. If items can't be reliably measured, they may be Disclosed in notes without recognition on the face. Example: self-developed brand value may not be recognized (can't reliably measure) but may be disclosed.

1-4.q: The Asset element in financial statements is defined as:
o: Any physical property owned by the entity
Probable Future Economic Benefits Controlled by the entity as a result of Past Transactions or Events
Any payment made by the entity
Resources purchased with borrowed funds
1-4.e: Asset Definition (GAAP): a probable future economic benefit Obtained or Controlled by the entity as a result of past transactions or events. Three elements: (1) Future Economic Benefit (cash flow potential — through use or sale); (2) Controlled by the entity (doesn't need to be legally owned — a lease can create a right-of-use asset); (3) Past Transaction (the acquiring event has occurred). A purchase order for future delivery → Not yet an asset (no past transaction yet).

1-4.q: The Expense element in financial statements is defined as:
o: Cash payments made during the period
Outflows Or Uses Of Assets or incurring of Liabilities as a result of delivering goods or services — decreasing equity
Dividends paid to shareholders
All costs of production, including unsold inventory
1-4.e: Expense Definition: outflows or other uses of assets Or incurrings of liabilities arising from the entity's Ongoing Operations (delivering goods, providing services) that Decrease Equity (Retained Earnings). Distinct from: Losses (peripheral transactions), Distributions to owners (dividends — also decrease equity but are not expenses). Timing: recognized when the Asset IS Consumed or Liability IS Incurred, not when cash is paid (accrual basis).

1-4.q: Under the Accrual Basis, when should revenue from selling goods on credit (accounts receivable) be recognized?
o: When the cash is collected from the customer
When the customer places the order
When The Performance Obligation IS Satisfied — typically when goods are delivered and control passes to the customer, regardless of when cash is received
When the invoice is mailed to the customer
1-4.e: Accrual Basis Revenue Recognition: recognize revenue when Earned — when the performance obligation is satisfied (goods delivered, control transferred). The timing of Cash Collection is irrelevant to when revenue is recognized. If cash comes Before delivery → Deferred Revenue (liability). If delivery comes Before cash → Account Receivable (asset). This is the fundamental difference between accrual and cash basis accounting.

1-4.q: The Cost Constraint means that requiring a specific disclosure is justified only if:
o: The amount is material to the financial statements
The disclosure has been required by a previous standard
The Benefits To Users of the information exceed the Costs to the preparer of providing it
The disclosure is required by tax law
1-4.e: Cost Constraint: a pervasive constraint on financial reporting. Even useful, relevant information may not be required if Costs of preparation/disclosure outweigh Benefits to users. Costs: data gathering, systems, preparation time, competitive harm from disclosure, litigation risk. Benefits: improved user decisions, lower cost of capital, market efficiency. Standard setters explicitly consider this when adding disclosure requirements — but are not required to justify every standard based on a formal cost-benefit analysis.

1-4.q: Under US GAAP, when current period financial statements are Restated to correct an error from a prior period, the correction is recorded as:
o: A current period expense or revenue
An extraordinary item on the income statement
An Adjustment To The Beginning Retained Earnings of the earliest period presented — retrospective correction
A cumulative catch-up adjustment in the current period's operating expenses
1-4.e: Prior Period Error Correction (ASC 250): errors from prior periods are Restated Retrospectively — all prior period financial statements presented are corrected to show what they would have shown had the error not occurred. The cumulative effect on all prior periods appears as an adjustment to the Beginning Retained Earnings of the earliest comparative period shown. Contrast with Accounting Policy Changes (also retrospective in most cases) and Estimates changes (prospective).

1-4.q: Contingent Liabilities under US GAAP are Accrued (recognized on the balance sheet) only when:
o: Any possible loss exists, regardless of probability
Both conditions are met: the loss is Probable and the amount can be Reasonably Estimated
The loss has been determined by a court judgment
Management decides to record the liability
1-4.e: Contingent Liability Recognition (ASC 450): accrue when Both: (1) Probable — the loss is likely to occur based on available information (more likely than not); (2) Reasonably Estimable — can quantify the loss. Reasonably Possible (not probable): disclose in Notes only — no accrual. Remote: generally no accrual or disclosure required. Record the Best Estimate; if no single estimate is better, record the Minimum of the range.

1-4.q: The Revenue element is defined as:
o: Cash received from customers during the period
Inflows Of Assets or Settlement Of Liabilities from Delivering Goods Or Providing Services that constitute the entity's Ongoing Major Or Central Operations
All increases in equity during the period
Gains from sale of long-term assets
1-4.e: Revenue Definition: inflows of assets (usually cash or Ar) or settlements of liabilities arising from the entity's Primary, Ongoing operations — delivering products or providing services. Distinct from Gains (peripheral/incidental transactions). Revenue classification matters: revenue is part of gross profit; gains are separate. Example: for a car manufacturer, car sales = Revenue; selling a factory building = Gain. Both increase equity but classified differently.

1-4.q: An Estimate Change in accounting (e.g., revising the useful life of equipment) is accounted for:
o: Retrospectively by restating all prior period financial statements
As an error correction with a cumulative adjustment to beginning retained earnings
Prospectively — applied to the Current And Future Periods only, without restating prior periods
As an extraordinary item on the income statement
1-4.e: Accounting Estimate Changes (ASC 250): prospective treatment — no restatement of prior periods. The revised estimate is applied in the Current Period and Forward. Rationale: estimates are inherently uncertain; revising an estimate is not an error. Example: extend useful life of equipment from 5 to 8 years → recalculate depreciation prospectively using remaining book value over remaining useful life. No income statement 'catch-up' for prior periods.

1-5.q: A contingent liability is Recorded (accrued) when the loss is:
o: Reasonably possible and estimable
Probable and reasonably estimable
Remote and estimable
Possible but not estimable
1-5.e: A contingent liability is Accrued (recorded) when the loss is: (1) Probable (likely to occur) And (2) Reasonably Estimable (can be measured). If only probable but not estimable, or if reasonably possible, disclose in notes but do not record.

1-5.q: Treasury Stock is presented on the Balance Sheet as:
o: An asset — the company owns its own shares
A reduction from total shareholders' equity
Other comprehensive income
A current liability
1-5.e: Treasury Stock = shares repurchased by the company. It is presented as a Deduction from Shareholders' Equity (at cost under the cost method). It is Not an asset — a company cannot own itself.

1-5.q: Additional Paid-in Capital (Apic) represents:
o: The par value of shares issued
The amount received Above par value when shares are issued
Retained earnings from prior periods
Dividends paid to shareholders
1-5.e: Apic = the amount received from shareholders Above par value when stock is issued. Example: Issue 1,000 shares with $1 par value at $15 per share: Common Stock = $1,000 (par), Apic = $14,000 (excess above par).

1-5.q: Long-term debt is measured at:
o: Face (par) value only
Fair value at each reporting date
Amortized cost — present value of future payments
The higher of cost or market
1-5.e: Long-term debt (bonds, notes payable) is measured at Amortized Cost — the present value of future cash payments (principal + interest), adjusted for unamortized premium or discount. Not remeasured to fair value each period (unless FV option elected).

1-5.q: Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (Aoci) includes:
o: Net income from current operations
Unrealized gains/losses on AFS securities, foreign currency translation, pension adjustments
Cash dividends received
Revenue not yet earned
1-5.e: Aoci accumulates items that bypass the income statement but affect equity: unrealized gains/losses on Available-For-Sale securities, foreign currency Translation adjustments, pension liability adjustments, and effective portion of cash flow hedges.

1-5.q: A contingent liability that is Reasonably Possible (not probable) should be:
o: Accrued on the balance sheet as a liability
Disclosed In The Notes To Financial Statements but Not recorded on the face of the balance sheet
Ignored — no action required
Recorded only in the internal management accounts
1-5.e: Reasonably Possible contingent liability: Not accrued on the balance sheet (recognition requires Probable + Estimable) but Disclosed In Notes. Notes should describe: nature of contingency, estimate of possible loss (or range), or statement that estimate cannot be made. Remote contingencies generally require no disclosure. This three-tier approach (accrue/disclose/ignore) is essential CMA exam knowledge.

1-5.q: Treasury Stock (shares repurchased by the company) is shown on the balance sheet as:
o: An asset — the company owns shares in itself
A contra-revenue — deducted from sales
A Contra-Equity Account — deducted from stockholders' equity, reducing total equity
An investment in financial assets
1-5.e: Treasury Stock: repurchased shares are Not assets (a company cannot own itself). They are Contra-Equity — shown as a Deduction in the stockholders' equity section. Methods: Cost Method (record at cost paid, debit Treasury Stock) — most common; Par Value Method. Treasury stock reduces: shares outstanding, total equity, and earnings per share denominator. Dividends are not paid on treasury shares. Reissuance: credit Treasury Stock at cost, any difference to Apic (or Retained Earnings if Apic is insufficient).

1-5.q: Bonds Payable are measured at:
o: Face (par) value always
Fair value with changes to net income
Current market price
Amortized Cost — face value adjusted for unamortized premium or discount using the effective interest method
1-5.e: Bonds Payable Measurement: carried at Amortized Cost = face value ± unamortized premium/discount. Premium: bond issued above face (market rate < stated rate) → deduct from face to get carrying value; amortized toward face value over life. Discount: bond issued below face (market rate > stated rate) → add to face to get carrying value; amortized toward face value over life. Effective interest method: interest expense = beginning carrying value × market rate. Carrying value at maturity = face value.

1-5.q: Held-To-Maturity (HTM) debt securities are valued at:
o: Fair value with unrealized gains/losses in OCI
Fair value with unrealized gains/losses in net income
Net Realizable Value — estimated proceeds if sold today
Amortized Cost — classified as HTM because the company has the Positive Intent And Ability to hold to maturity
1-5.e: HTM Securities: carried at Amortized Cost — not marked to fair value. Rationale: if management will hold to maturity, interim market value changes are irrelevant — the bond will repay face value at maturity regardless. Strict Classification Rules: company must have Both (1) positive intent And (2) ability to hold to maturity. Selling before maturity 'taints' the portfolio and forces reclassification of all HTM securities. Only debt securities qualify for HTM (not equity).

1-5.q: Available-For-Sale (AFS) debt securities are valued at:
o: Amortized cost (like HTM securities)
Historical cost
Fair Value — with unrealized gains and losses reported in OCI (Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income), not in current earnings
Fair value — with unrealized gains and losses reported in net income (like trading securities)
1-5.e: AFS Securities: Fair Value on the balance sheet; unrealized FV changes → OCI (not Net Income). When sold, the cumulative unrealized gain/loss is Reclassified from OCI to realized gain/loss in Net Income (reclassification adjustment). This 'parking in OCI' prevents management from manipulating earnings by selectively selling winners while holding losers. Contrast: Trading securities → FV changes go directly to Net Income immediately.

1-5.q: Trading Securities are classified as:
o: Non-current assets held to generate long-term returns
Current assets valued at amortized cost
Current Assets valued at Fair Value — with unrealized gains and losses recognized in Net Income immediately
Non-current assets valued at fair value through OCI
1-5.e: Trading Securities: bought and held Principally for short-term profit from market price changes. Classified as Current Assets. Measured at Fair Value with All unrealized gains/losses → Net Income each period. Management cannot 'hold and hope' — FV changes are immediately recognized in earnings. Most actively managed investment portfolios (not held-to-maturity or AFS) are trading. Note: under GAAP, almost all equity securities with determinable FV are trading-like (FV through NI) unless elected otherwise.

1-5.q: Under ASC 606, Revenue from a contract with a customer is recognized:
o: When the contract is signed
When cash is received
When (Or As) the entity Satisfies A Performance Obligation by transferring a promised good or service to the customer
Evenly over the contract term regardless of performance
1-5.e: ASC 606 Core Principle: recognize revenue to depict the Transfer Of Promised Goods Or Services in an amount that reflects the Consideration the entity expects to receive. 5-step model: (1) Identify the contract; (2) Identify performance obligations; (3) Determine transaction price; (4) Allocate transaction price to POs; (5) Recognize revenue when (or as) each Po is satisfied (control transferred). This is the foundation of modern revenue recognition under both GAAP and IFRS 15.

1-5.q: A Loss Contingency that is Remote should be treated as:
o: Accrued as a liability
Disclosed in the financial statement notes
Generally Requires No Accrual Or Disclosure — remote contingencies are typically ignored for financial reporting purposes
Disclosed only if the amount is material
1-5.e: Remote Contingency: Generally requires No Accrual and No Disclosure. The loss is very unlikely to occur. Exceptions: Guarantees — even remote, should typically be disclosed; Guarantees of debt may require fair value recognition under GAAP regardless of remote probability. Three-tier system: Probable + Estimable → Accrue; Reasonably Possible → Disclose Only; Remote → Generally Ignore.

1-5.q: Under US GAAP, Internally Generated intangible assets (like a self-developed brand) are generally:
o: Capitalized at their estimated fair market value
Capitalized at the direct costs incurred in their development
Not Recognized as assets on the balance sheet — expensed as incurred (except for certain software development costs and acquired in-process R&D)
Recorded in OCI as unrealized gains
1-5.e: Internally Generated Intangibles (US GAAP): generally Cannot be recognized as assets. Research costs → Expensed. Development costs → Expensed under GAAP (IFRS capitalizes when feasibility criteria met). Brand names, mastheads, customer lists, internally generated goodwill → Not recognized. Only Acquired intangibles (in a business combination or standalone purchase) are recognized. Rationale: internally generated value is too difficult to measure reliably and separate from general business development costs.

1-5.q: Pension Obligations are classified on the balance sheet as:
o: An off-balance sheet item disclosed only in notes
A component of stockholders' equity
A Non-Current Liability (underfunded pension) or Non-Current Asset (overfunded pension) — the funded status of the plan is recognized on the balance sheet
A current liability only when contributions are due
1-5.e: Pension Accounting (ASC 715): the Funded Status of the pension plan = Plan Assets − Projected Benefit Obligation (Pbo). If Underfunded (Pbo > Assets) → Net Pension Liability on the balance sheet. If Overfunded (Assets > Pbo) → Net Pension Asset. This recognition of funded status was a major change from prior practice (Fas 87, now ASC 715). Unrealized gains/losses and prior service costs are recorded in OCI until amortized to pension expense.

1-5.q: The Lower Of Cost Or Net Realizable Value (Lcnrv) rule for inventory means that inventory is written down when:
o: The market price of the inventory rises above cost
Inventory'S Net Realizable Value Falls Below Its Cost — the carrying value is reduced to Nrv, recognizing a loss in the current period
The company cannot sell the inventory within 30 days
Management decides a write-down is prudent
1-5.e: Lcnrv Rule (ASC 330): inventory is carried at the Lower of (1) Cost (FIFO, weighted avg, specific Id) or (2) Net Realizable Value (estimated selling price less estimated costs to complete and sell). If Nrv < Cost → Write Down to Nrv, recognize loss in current period income. Under GAAP: once written down, the new lower cost becomes the new cost basis — Cannot be written back up if Nrv recovers. Under IFRS: write-down reversals are permitted.

1-5.q: Intangible Assets with Indefinite useful lives (like trademarks renewed indefinitely) are:
o: Amortized over 40 years
Amortized over 20 years per GAAP requirements
Not Amortized — but tested for Impairment Annually (or when indicators exist)
Expensed immediately when acquired
1-5.e: Indefinite-Lived Intangibles (ASC 350): if an intangible asset has No Foreseeable Limit to the period over which it will generate cash flows, it has an Indefinite Useful Life. Examples: trademarks with perpetual renewal rights, certain broadcast licenses. Treatment: Not amortized; tested for Impairment at least annually. If fair value < carrying amount → recognize impairment loss. Contrast: Finite-lived intangibles (patents, customer lists) → Amortized over useful life.

1-5.q: Deferred Tax Liabilities arise when:
o: The company has paid more taxes than the accounting expense
Taxable Income IS Less Than Accounting Income in the current period due to Temporary Differences — the company will owe more taxes in the future
The company has a net operating loss carryforward
Tax rates are expected to decrease in the future
1-5.e: Deferred Tax Liability (DTL): when Book Income > Taxable Income in the current period due to Temporary Differences (differences that will reverse in future periods). The company has lower taxes Now but will pay higher taxes Later. Example: accelerated depreciation for tax (lower current taxable income) vs. straight-line for books (higher current book income) → DTL. Not a Permanent Difference (tax credit, non-deductible expense) which never reverses.

1-5.q: Accounts Receivable are reported on the balance sheet at:
o: The gross (face) amount of all invoices outstanding
The historical cost of goods sold on credit
Net Realizable Value — gross Ar minus the Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (estimated uncollectibles)
Fair value at each reporting date
1-5.e: Ar Balance Sheet Value = Net Realizable Value = Gross Ar − Allowance for Doubtful Accounts (Ada). The Ada reflects Estimated uncollectibles — recognized in the period of the sale (matching principle). The Ada is a Contra-Asset account (debit balance normal for asset, Ada has credit balance). Bad debt expense = the current period's addition to the allowance. Direct write-off (no allowance) is Not GAAP because it violates matching.

1-5.q: Equity Securities (stocks) with a readily determinable fair value are measured under US GAAP (ASC 321) at:
o: Historical cost
Amortized cost
Lower of cost or fair value
Fair Value — with All unrealized gains and losses recognized in Net Income (not OCI) in the period they arise
1-5.e: ASC 321 (Equity Securities, post-2016): All equity securities with determinable FV are measured at Fair Value with changes in FV recognized in Net Income — no longer AFS treatment with OCI for equities. Exception: equity method investments (>20% ownership). Practical expedient: equity securities Without determinable FV can use cost minus impairment. This change eliminated the AFS (OCI) classification for most equity securities, making FV changes flow directly to earnings.

1-6.q: Current Assets are listed on the Balance Sheet in order of:
o: Alphabetical order
Liquidity — most liquid first
Size — largest first
Date of acquisition
1-6.e: Current Assets are listed in order of Liquidity (most liquid first): Cash → Marketable Securities → Ar → Inventory → Prepaid Expenses. This ordering reflects how quickly each asset can be converted to cash.

1-6.q: Deferred Revenue is classified on the Balance Sheet as:
o: An asset — the company has the cash
A liability — the company owes goods or services
Equity — additional paid-in capital
Other comprehensive income
1-6.e: Deferred Revenue (unearned revenue) is a Liability. The company has received cash but has Not Yet Performed the service or delivered the product. It represents an obligation to deliver future goods/services.

1-6.q: The Current Portion of Long-Term Debt is classified as:
o: Non-current liability
Current liability
Long-term debt that stays non-current
Equity
1-6.e: The portion of long-term debt due Within One Year is reclassified as a Current Liability. This properly reflects that this portion requires cash payment in the near term and affects liquidity analysis.

1-6.q: Working Capital = $400,000. Current Liabilities = $150,000. Current Assets =
1-6.e: Working Capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. $400,000 = Ca − $150,000. Ca = $400,000 + $150,000 = $550,000.

1-6.q: Prepaid Insurance is classified on the Balance Sheet as:
o: A liability — insurance not yet used
A current asset — a future economic benefit
An expense immediately
Non-current asset always
1-6.e: Prepaid Insurance is a Current Asset — the company has paid for future insurance coverage that hasn't been used yet. It represents a Future Economic Benefit (the right to coverage). As time passes, it is expensed (Insurance Expense).

1-6.q: Non-current assets include all of the following Except:
o: Pp&E (net of depreciation)
Goodwill
Accounts Receivable
Equity method investments
1-6.e: Accounts Receivable is typically a Current Asset (expected to be collected within one year). Pp&E, goodwill, and equity method investments are all Non-Current assets because they are held for longer-term benefit.

1-6.q: A company has Current Assets of $600,000 and Current Liabilities of $400,000. Current Ratio =
1-6.e: Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities = $600,000 / $400,000 = 1.5. A ratio > 1 means the company has more current assets than current liabilities — generally a sign of adequate short-term liquidity.

1-6.q: Current Assets are defined as assets that are expected to be:
o: Used in the production of goods within 1 year
Converted To Cash, Sold, Or Consumed within One Year or the Operating Cycle — whichever is Longer
Recorded at their original purchase price
Tangible assets only
1-6.e: Current Asset Classification: converted to cash, sold, or used within One Year Or The Operating Cycle, whichever is Longer. The operating cycle = time from purchasing inventory → selling → collecting cash. For most companies < 1 year, so 1 year applies. For tobacco, liquor, or lumber companies, operating cycle > 1 year — then Oc applies. Order on balance sheet: most liquid first (Cash, Short-term investments, Ar, Inventory, Prepaid expenses).

1-6.q: Working Capital is calculated as:
o: Total Assets minus Total Liabilities
Net income plus depreciation
Current Assets Minus Current Liabilities — measures the company's ability to pay short-term obligations with short-term resources
Cash and cash equivalents only
1-6.e: Working Capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. Positive working capital = more Ca than Cl → generally good liquidity. Negative working capital = more Cl than Ca → potential liquidity problem. Key metric for: creditors (can the company pay short-term debts?), analysts (operational efficiency), covenant compliance (many loan covenants include minimum working capital requirements). Related: Current Ratio = Ca/Cl; Quick Ratio = (Cash + St Investments + Ar)/Cl.

1-6.q: Deferred Revenue on the balance sheet is classified as:
o: An asset — it represents money the company has earned
A component of stockholders' equity
A Liability — the company has received Cash but has Not Yet Performed the service or delivered the goods — it owes a future service
A contra-revenue account deducted from sales
1-6.e: Deferred Revenue = Liability: the company received cash Before completing the performance obligation. Examples: subscription payments received in advance, gift card sales, annual software license fees paid upfront. As the company Delivers the goods/services, Deferred Revenue (liability) decreases and Revenue (income) increases. If the company liquidates without performing, it would owe refunds to customers — hence it is a genuine Liability.

1-6.q: Prepaid Expenses appear on the balance sheet as:
o: A current liability — the company owes future services
A component of equity
A Current Asset — the company Has Paid for a future benefit that has not yet been consumed (e.g., prepaid insurance, prepaid rent)
An intangible asset
1-6.e: Prepaid Expenses = Current Asset: the company has paid cash for a benefit it has not yet received. Examples: prepaid insurance (paid 12-month premium; each month, 1/12 is expensed and the asset decreases), prepaid rent, prepaid subscriptions. As the benefit is consumed (time passes, services are used), the Prepaid Asset decreases and Expense is recognized. Prepaid expenses are the 'mirror image' of deferred revenue.

1-6.q: On a classified balance sheet, Long-Term Debt due within the next 12 months should be:
o: Kept in the non-current section since it was originally a long-term liability
Ignored until it actually becomes payable
Reclassified To Current Liabilities — the current maturity of long-term debt is a current obligation
Disclosed only in notes, not reclassified on the face
1-6.e: Current Maturity Of Long-Term Debt: the portion of long-term debt due within the next 12 months (or operating cycle) must be Reclassified from long-term to Current Liabilities. This ensures the balance sheet accurately reflects the company's near-term cash obligations. Example: a $10M bond maturing in 18 months → $0 currently due → remains long-term. A $10M bond maturing in 8 months → Entire $10M moves to current.

1-6.q: The Equity section of a balance sheet includes which components?
o: Cash, Retained Earnings, and Long-term Debt
Common Stock (par value), Additional Paid-In Capital, Retained Earnings, Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income, and Treasury Stock
Total Assets minus Long-term Liabilities only
Revenue, Expenses, and Dividends
1-6.e: Stockholders' Equity Components: (1) Common Stock — par value of issued shares; (2) Additional Paid-In Capital (Apic) — proceeds above par; (3) Retained Earnings — cumulative net income minus dividends; (4) Accumulated OCI (Aoci) — cumulative unrealized gains/losses on AFS securities, pension adjustments, foreign currency translation; (5) Treasury Stock — cost of repurchased shares (contra-equity, debit balance). Total = Common + Apic + Re + Aoci − Treasury Stock.

1-6.q: Goodwill appears on the balance sheet Only when:
o: The company develops a strong brand reputation
The company has above-average profitability
IT IS Acquired In A Business Combination — the excess of the Purchase Price over the Fair Value of identifiable net assets acquired
The company has loyal customer relationships
1-6.e: Goodwill Recognition: goodwill = Purchase Price − Fair Value of Identifiable Net Assets (Assets − Liabilities). Only recognized when Acquired externally in a business combination. Internally Generated goodwill (company's own reputation, brand) Cannot be recognized under GAAP or IFRS. Goodwill represents: customer relationships, assembled workforce, synergies, brand — things paid for but not separately identifiable as assets.

1-6.q: Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (Aoci) in the equity section represents:
o: Cumulative undistributed profits retained by the company
Dividends declared but not yet paid
Cumulative Unrealized Gains And Losses that are excluded from Net Income — including AFS security FV changes, pension adjustments, and foreign currency translation adjustments
The additional capital raised above par value on stock issuances
1-6.e: Aoci: the accumulation of items that Bypass the income statement (OCI items). Examples: unrealized G/L on AFS securities, unrealized G/L on cash flow hedges, pension prior service cost and actuarial G/L, foreign currency translation adjustment. Aoci increases/decreases each period by the current period's OCI items. When OCI items are reclassified to income (realized), Aoci decreases. Aoci is part of Total Comprehensive Income but not Net Income.

1-6.q: On a classified balance sheet, Intangible Assets are presented:
o: Under Current Assets if the benefit will be consumed within 1 year
As a deduction from equity
As Non-Current Assets — long-term assets without physical substance, such as patents, trademarks, copyrights, and goodwill
As a component of Property, Plant & Equipment
1-6.e: Intangible Assets: Non-Current assets with no physical substance. Separately identified on the balance sheet (or in a note). Examples: patents (amortized over useful life), trademarks, customer lists, software, goodwill. Finite-lived intangibles: amortized. Indefinite-lived (goodwill, certain trademarks): not amortized but impairment tested. Distinct from Tangible long-term assets (Pp&E — land, buildings, equipment).

1-6.q: The Current Ratio is calculated as:
o: Net Income ÷ Total Assets
Total Assets ÷ Total Liabilities
Current Assets ÷ Current Liabilities — measures short-term liquidity
(Cash + Ar) ÷ Current Liabilities
1-6.e: Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities. Measures ability to pay short-term obligations with short-term resources. Rule of thumb: ratio > 1 = more Ca than Cl = generally adequate liquidity. Very high ratio may indicate excess inventory or poor cash management. Quick Ratio = (Cash + Short-term Investments + Net Ar) / Cl — more conservative, excludes inventory (which may not be quickly convertible to cash). Both are key balance sheet analysis metrics.

1-6.q: Accrued Liabilities on the balance sheet represent:
o: Amounts paid in advance by customers
Long-term obligations to bondholders
Expenses That Have Been Incurred but Not Yet Paid In Cash — recognized under the accrual basis of accounting
Deferred tax obligations
1-6.e: Accrued Liabilities (Accrued Expenses): expenses Incurred but Unpaid at period-end. Examples: accrued wages (employees worked but payday hasn't arrived), accrued interest (time has passed but interest isn't due yet), accrued rent, accrued income taxes. Entry: Debit Expense, Credit Accrued Liability. When paid: Debit Accrued Liability, Credit Cash. Essential to the Matching Principle — recognize expense when incurred, not when paid.

1-6.q: Notes Payable Due In 18 Months would be classified on the balance sheet as:
o: Current Liability — because it will be due in the future
A component of stockholders' equity
Long-Term (Non-Current) Liability — it is not due within the next 12 months (or operating cycle if longer)
Off-balance sheet — disclosed in notes only
1-6.e: 18-Month Note = Non-Current Liability. The current/non-current classification is based on When The Obligation IS Due: due within 12 months (or operating cycle) → Current. Due after 12 months → Non-Current (long-term). The 18-month note matures beyond 1 year, so it is a long-term liability on today's balance sheet. In 6 months, when it is due in 12 months, it will be reclassified to current.
