1-6.q: Long-Term Investments on the balance sheet represent assets that:
o: Are expected to be sold within 1 year
Are physical long-lived assets used in operations
The Company Intends To Hold For More Than One Year — including equity method investments, held-to-maturity securities, and long-term notes receivable
Are always recorded at fair value
1-6.e: Long-Term Investments (Non-current): investments management does not intend to convert to cash within 1 year. Examples: Equity Method investments (20-50% ownership — significant influence), HTM bonds (held to maturity), long-term notes receivable, investments in real estate not used in operations, sinking fund assets. Distinct from: Short-Term Investments (current assets — trading and AFS securities management intends to sell within 1 year).

1-7.q: Other Comprehensive Income (OCI) includes unrealized gains/losses on Available-for-Sale (AFS) securities. This means these gains/losses:
o: Are included in net income on the income statement
Bypass the income statement but are reported in OCI and affect equity
Are never reported until the securities are sold
Are reported only in the notes
1-7.e: AFS security unrealized gains/losses Bypass the income statement and are reported directly in OCI (Other Comprehensive Income). They are accumulated in Aoci (equity) until the security is sold, at which point they are reclassified to net income.

1-7.q: Trading securities report unrealized gains/losses:
o: In OCI (bypasses income statement)
In net income on the income statement
Only when sold
In retained earnings directly
1-7.e: Trading securities are reported at fair value with unrealized gains/losses flowing Through Net Income each period. This is different from AFS securities, where unrealized changes go to OCI.

1-7.q: Comprehensive Income =
o: Net Income only
Net Income + Other Comprehensive Income
Gross Profit + OCI
Net Income − Dividends
1-7.e: Comprehensive Income = Net Income + Other Comprehensive Income (OCI). It captures All changes in equity during a period Except owner transactions (investments and dividends). OCI items include AFS unrealized G/L, foreign currency translation, pension adjustments.

1-7.q: Discontinued operations are presented on the income statement:
o: As part of operating income
Within cost of goods sold
Separately, After income from continuing operations, Net of tax
Only in the notes — not on the face
1-7.e: Discontinued operations are presented Separately at the bottom of the income statement, After income from continuing operations. They are shown Net Of Tax. This separate presentation allows users to better assess the results of Continuing operations.

1-7.q: In a multi-step income statement, Gross Profit is calculated as:
o: Net Sales − All Operating Expenses
Net Sales − Cost of Goods Sold
Operating Income − Non-operating items
Net Income + Tax Expense
1-7.e: Gross Profit = Net Sales Revenue − Cost Of Goods Sold. It represents profit before operating expenses (Sga, R&D). It is the first key subtotal in a multi-step income statement.

1-7.q: Foreign currency translation adjustments are reported in:
o: Net income on the income statement
Cost of goods sold
OCI — not net income
Retained earnings directly
1-7.e: Foreign currency Translation adjustments (translating foreign subsidiary FS into the parent's reporting currency) are reported in OCI, not in net income. They are accumulated in Aoci until the foreign subsidiary is sold.

1-7.q: Pension Liability adjustments (actuarial gains/losses) are reported in:
o: Operating expenses
OCI — Other Comprehensive Income
Net income directly
Notes only
1-7.e: Pension actuarial gains/losses and prior service costs are reported in OCI (accumulated in Aoci). They may be subsequently amortized into net income via the corridor method or immediately recognized in OCI under IFRS.

1-7.q: Comprehensive Income is equal to:
o: Net income plus revenues minus expenses
Only items recorded on the income statement
Net Income Plus All Other Comprehensive Income (OCI) Items — the total change in equity during a period excluding transactions with owners
Operating income plus non-operating items
1-7.e: Comprehensive Income = Net Income + Other Comprehensive Income. It captures All Changes In Equity during a period Except transactions with owners (issuing stock, dividends). OCI items: unrealized G/L on AFS securities, pension actuarial G/L and prior service cost, foreign currency translation adjustments, unrealized G/L on qualifying cash flow hedges. Aoci (Accumulated OCI) on the balance sheet = cumulative total of past OCI items.

1-7.q: Discontinued Operations on the income statement are reported:
o: As part of continuing operations within operating income
Only in footnotes — not on the face of the income statement
Net Of Tax as a Separate Line Item below income from continuing operations — showing only the net effect of the discontinued component
Above gross profit as a separately identified revenue stream
1-7.e: Discontinued Operations (ASC 205-20): reported Separately from continuing operations on the income statement: (1) Income from continuing operations (shown first); (2) Discontinued operations — shown Net Of Tax as one line item (or two: results of operations + gain/loss on disposal). Rationale: investors should be able to see what the Continuing business earns vs. the discontinued segment, for better predictive value.

1-7.q: Unrealized Gains on Available-For-Sale (AFS) securities flow to:
o: Net income — recognized immediately in the income statement
Cost basis — no recognition until sold
Other Comprehensive Income (OCI) — and accumulate in Aoci on the balance sheet until the security is sold
The operating section of the income statement
1-7.e: AFS Unrealized Gains/Losses → OCI (not Net Income). Each period: mark to fair value, record change as OCI. When Sold: remove from Aoci and recognize as Realized gain/loss in Net Income (reclassification adjustment). Rationale: management did not Intend to sell (unlike trading); requiring income recognition of temporary fluctuations would distort earnings with volatility from price swings that may reverse. Contrast: Trading securities → All FV changes → Net Income immediately.

1-7.q: The Multi-Step income statement format is preferred because it separately discloses:
o: Cash flows from operating activities
Gross Profit, Operating Income, And Net Income — allowing analysis of core business profitability at multiple levels
Only total revenues and total expenses
Tax expense before all other items
1-7.e: Multi-Step Income Statement: (1) Net Revenue; (2) Cost of Goods Sold; (3) = Gross Profit; (4) Operating Expenses (Sga, R&D, depreciation); (5) = Operating Income; (6) Non-operating items (interest income/expense, gains/losses); (7) = Income Before Tax; (8) Income Tax Expense; (9) = Net Income; (10) OCI → (11) = Comprehensive Income. Each step has analytical value: Gross Margin = pricing power; Operating Margin = core operational efficiency; Net Margin = overall profitability.

1-7.q: Earnings Per Share (EPS) is required to be disclosed on the income statement for:
o: All companies including private companies
Only companies with complex capital structures
All Publicly Traded Companies — both basic and diluted EPS must be reported on the face of the income statement
Only companies that have issued stock options
1-7.e: EPS Requirement: GAAP requires publicly traded companies to present both Basic and Diluted EPS on the Face of the income statement. Private companies are Not required to present EPS. Basic EPS = Net Income available to common shareholders / Weighted average common shares outstanding. Diluted EPS = Net Income adjusted / Weighted avg shares + dilutive potential shares (options, convertibles). Both are required; diluted EPS is typically lower (more shares in denominator).

1-7.q: Operating Income on the multi-step income statement excludes:
o: Cost of goods sold
Selling, general and administrative expenses
Interest Expense And Income Tax Expense — these are non-operating items that appear below operating income
Depreciation and amortization
1-7.e: Operating Income (EBIT — Earnings Before Interest and Taxes): revenues minus All Operating expenses (COGS, Sga, R&D, D&A) but Before interest and taxes. Interest is a Financing cost (not an operating cost of delivering goods/services). Income taxes are determined after all other items. Separating operating from non-operating items allows: assessment of core business performance independent of capital structure (interest) and tax strategies.

1-7.q: An Extraordinary Item on the income statement (under legacy GAAP):
o: Is still required as a separate category under current US GAAP
Was Eliminated by ASU 2015-01 — no longer a separate category in US GAAP
Is defined as any unusual gain or loss regardless of amount
Includes discontinued operations and changes in accounting principle
1-7.e: Extraordinary Items: Eliminated under US GAAP by ASU 2015-01 (effective 2016). Previously: events that were Both unusual in nature And infrequent in occurrence were reported net-of-tax as extraordinary. Now: unusual and/or infrequent items are presented as part of income from continuing operations (separately disclosed if material but not 'extraordinary'). IFRS never had an extraordinary item classification. This simplification removed a category that was inconsistently applied.

1-7.q: Gross Profit on the income statement is calculated as:
o: Net Income minus operating expenses
Revenue minus all operating, non-operating, and tax expenses
Net Revenue Minus Cost Of Goods Sold (COGS) — the profit from production/purchasing before operating overhead
Operating income plus interest income
1-7.e: Gross Profit = Net Revenue − Cost of Goods Sold. Gross Profit Margin = Gross Profit / Revenue. Key analytical metric: measures the efficiency of production/sourcing and pricing power. High gross margin: product commands premium pricing or has low production costs. Declining gross margin: price pressure, rising input costs, unfavorable product mix. COGS includes: direct materials, direct labor, manufacturing overhead — Not Sga or R&D.

1-7.q: Foreign Currency Translation Adjustments (from consolidating foreign subsidiaries) are reported as:
o: Part of operating income on the income statement
A separate item in net income
An OCI Item — accumulated in Aoci on the balance sheet; reclassified to income only when the foreign subsidiary is disposed of
A realized gain/loss in the period of translation
1-7.e: Foreign Currency Translation Adjustment (Cta): when consolidating a foreign subsidiary, assets/liabilities are translated at the Current Exchange Rate, while revenues/expenses at the Average rate. The difference is the Cta — recorded in OCI/Aoci. It is Not reclassified to income each period — only when the subsidiary is Sold or otherwise disposed of (because the temporary fluctuations in exchange rates may reverse). This is one OCI item that may Never reclassify (if the subsidiary is kept indefinitely).

1-7.q: Income From Continuing Operations on the income statement:
o: Includes the results of discontinued business segments
Represents The Results From The Company'S Ongoing Business Activities — reported before discontinued operations
Is always the same as net income
Excludes all non-operating items
1-7.e: Income From Continuing Operations: the 'above the line' income — all revenue, COGS, operating expenses, interest, and taxes from the Ongoing business that management expects to continue. Reported Before discontinued operations. This is the most relevant predictor of Future Earnings since discontinued segments no longer contribute. Financial analysts often focus on this figure when forecasting future performance, not total net income which is distorted by one-time discontinued items.

1-7.q: Gains on the income statement typically appear:
o: As part of Revenue at the top of the income statement
As a component of Cost of Goods Sold
In The Non-Operating Section — below operating income, since gains arise from peripheral activities not related to the primary business
As a direct credit to retained earnings, bypassing the income statement
1-7.e: Gains: arise from Peripheral or Incidental transactions — not the company's primary business. Examples: gain on sale of equipment (sold for more than book value), gain on sale of investments, insurance recoveries. Reported in the Non-Operating Section of the multi-step income statement, below operating income. Reason: separating gains from core operations allows investors to assess Recurring Operating Profitability separately from one-time non-operating items.

1-7.q: In a multi-step income statement, Selling, General And Administrative Expenses (Sga) are classified as:
o: Part of cost of goods sold
A non-operating expense
Operating Expenses — deducted from gross profit to arrive at operating income
A direct deduction from equity
1-7.e: Sga Expenses: Operating Expenses — the costs of Running the business (sales force, marketing, executive salaries, accounting, legal, rent, utilities for corporate offices). They are Not production costs (COGS) and Not financing/non-operating costs. On the multi-step IS: Gross Profit − Operating Expenses (including Sga) = Operating Income. Sga expenses are a key area of cost management — often targeted in restructuring programs.

1-7.q: Reclassification Adjustments on the Statement of Comprehensive Income serve to:
o: Correct errors from prior periods
Adjust retained earnings for dividend payments

Prevent Double-Counting — when an OCI item is realized and reclassified to Net Income, the reclassification adjustment removes it from OCI so the gain/loss is not counted in Both OCI and Net Income in the same period
Allocate income tax expense between current and deferred components
1-7.e: Reclassification Adjustment: when an AFS gain (previously in OCI) is Realized upon sale and moves to Net Income, the Total Comprehensive Income for the period must be adjusted to avoid double-counting. Without the adjustment, the realized gain would be in Both Net Income And OCI in the period of sale. The reclassification removes it from OCI — so it appears only Once in total comprehensive income (in Net Income). This is a technical but important presentation requirement.

1-8.q: A reclassification adjustment is needed when OCI items are realized because:
o: GAAP requires it for tax purposes
It prevents Double-Counting when gains/losses move from OCI to Net Income
It increases retained earnings
It eliminates foreign currency risk
1-8.e: Reclassification Adjustment: when an OCI item is realized (e.g., AFS security sold), it moves to Net Income. The reclassification adjustment simultaneously Removes it from OCI to prevent counting it twice in comprehensive income.

1-8.q: Under the two-statement approach for comprehensive income:
o: Only one combined statement is prepared
A separate income statement And a separate Statement of Comprehensive Income are prepared
OCI is shown within operating expenses
Net income is not reported separately
1-8.e: Two-statement approach: a Separate Income Statement (showing net income) followed by a Separate Statement of Comprehensive Income (beginning with net income, then adding OCI items to arrive at comprehensive income).

1-8.q: OCI items on the Statement of Comprehensive Income are shown:
o: At gross amounts
Net of tax effects
Only as positive amounts
In alphabetical order
1-8.e: OCI items are presented Net Of Their Tax Effects. Either individual items can be shown net of tax, or they can be shown gross with a single aggregate tax amount. The net-of-tax presentation is most common.

1-8.q: Foreign currency translation adjustments are reclassified from Aoci to Net Income when:
o: The exchange rate changes by more than 10%
The foreign subsidiary is completely liquidated or sold
At the end of each reporting period
When declared by management
1-8.e: Foreign currency Translation adjustments are reclassified from Aoci to Net Income Only when the foreign subsidiary is completely disposed of (sold or liquidated). Cumulative translation adjustments stay in Aoci until that complete disposition.

1-8.q: Comprehensive Income for a period = $200,000. Net Income = $180,000. OCI =
1-8.e: Comprehensive Income = Net Income + OCI. $200,000 = $180,000 + OCI. OCI = $200,000 − $180,000 = $20,000.

1-8.q: Under US GAAP, companies may present comprehensive income using which method(s)?
o: Only the one-statement approach (combining IS and OCI)
Only the two-statement approach (separate OCI statement)
Either the One-Statement Approach (single combined statement) Or the Two-Statement Approach (separate IS followed by a separate OCI statement) — both are acceptable
Only in the notes to financial statements
1-8.e: Presentation Options (ASC 220): (1) One-Statement: continues from Net Income directly into OCI items, ending with Total Comprehensive Income; (2) Two-Statement: first statement ends at Net Income; immediately following second statement starts with Net Income, adds OCI, shows Total Comprehensive Income. Both are acceptable under US GAAP. OCI cannot be presented Only in the statement of stockholders' equity — must be on a statement of its own or combined with the IS.

1-8.q: OCI items are presented on the Statement of Comprehensive Income:
o: At gross amounts with no tax effect shown
As part of operating expenses
Net Of Their Income Tax Effects — either individually netted or shown gross with a single aggregate tax line for all OCI
At historical cost amounts only
1-8.e: OCI Presentation — Net Of Tax: either (1) show each OCI item already net of its tax effect, Or (2) show each item at its gross amount with a combined tax expense/benefit line for all OCI items at the bottom. Both approaches result in the same net-of-tax OCI total. This tax netting is unique to OCI — the IS typically shows income tax expense separately as a line item, while OCI items embed the tax effect.

1-8.q: Items included in OCI under US GAAP include all of the following Except:
o: Unrealized gains/losses on AFS debt securities
Foreign currency translation adjustments
Pension actuarial gains and losses (remeasurements)
Unrealized Gains/Losses On Trading Securities — these flow directly through Net Income, not OCI
1-8.e: Trading Security FV changes → Net Income (not OCI). OCI Items (US GAAP): (1) Unrealized G/L on AFS Debt securities; (2) Foreign currency translation adjustment (consolidation); (3) Pension actuarial G/L and prior service cost; (4) Unrealized G/L on qualifying Cash Flow Hedges; (5) Gain/loss on post-retirement benefit plans. Trading securities and derivatives Not designated as hedges go through Net Income immediately.

1-8.q: Pension Remeasurement Gains And Losses in OCI are Different from AFS unrealized gains/losses because pension remeasurements:
o: Are taxed at a higher rate than investment gains
Never Get Reclassified To Net Income under GAAP — they are amortized from Aoci to pension expense via the corridor or immediate recognition approach, not through direct reclassification
Are only recognized when the employee retires
Are always material and require separate disclosure
1-8.e: OCI Reclassification Distinction: AFS unrealized G/L → OCI until sold → then Reclassified to Net Income (capital gain/loss). Pension remeasurement G/L → Aoci → Amortized to pension expense over time via the 'corridor' or 'immediate recognition' approach — Not a direct reclassification. Foreign currency translation → Aoci until subsidiary is disposed of. Understanding which OCI items reclassify (and when) is a key exam topic.

1-8.q: The Unrealized Gain on a qualifying Cash Flow Hedge is reported in OCI because:
o: It has not yet been realized in cash
The gain is too uncertain to include in Net Income
The Hedging Instrument'S Gain IS Offset Against The Hedged Item'S Loss In The Same Period — recording in OCI delays recognition until the hedged transaction affects earnings, creating proper matching
All derivative gains must go through OCI
1-8.e: Cash Flow Hedge (ASC 815): hedges Future Variable Cash Flows (e.g., forward contract to buy foreign currency for a future purchase). The hedge gain/loss is recorded in OCI and Reclassified to Net Income In The Same Period that the hedged item affects earnings (when the forecasted transaction occurs). This prevents a mismatch: without OCI parking, the derivative gain/loss would be in Net Income before the hedged item is recognized.

1-8.q: Foreign Currency Translation Adjustments (from consolidating foreign subsidiaries) are classified as OCI because:
o: They represent unrealized gains and losses on foreign investments
They Are Temporary Fluctuations that may reverse — driven by exchange rate movements beyond management's control — and reclassified to income only when the subsidiary is sold
They are always immaterial
They represent hedging instruments
1-8.e: Cta (Foreign Currency Translation Adjustment): when consolidating foreign operations, exchange rate changes create translation differences. In OCI because: (1) Temporary — rates fluctuate; the subsidiary is still in operation and earning in its functional currency; (2) Not Realized until the subsidiary is disposed of. When the foreign subsidiary is Sold or liquidated, the cumulative Cta is reclassified from Aoci to the income statement as part of the gain/loss on disposal.

1-8.q: Which OCI item would Never be reclassified to Net Income under GAAP?
o: Unrealized gain on AFS securities — reclassified when sold
Cash flow hedge gain — reclassified when hedged item affects earnings
Foreign Currency Translation Adjustment from a foreign subsidiary that management intends to hold indefinitely — may never reclassify if the subsidiary is not sold
Pension prior service cost — amortized to pension expense
1-8.e: Cta may Never Reclassify if the foreign subsidiary is held indefinitely and never sold. Other OCI items Do reclassify: AFS → realized when sold; cash flow hedge → when hedged transaction occurs; pension G/L → amortized over service periods. The question of 'which OCI items eventually leave Aoci and which might stay forever' is a nuanced but tested concept. Cta sits in Aoci potentially indefinitely.

1-8.q: When a company presents a One-Statement approach for comprehensive income, the statement begins with:
o: Net income from the prior period
Operating revenues
Revenue At The Top — it starts as a standard income statement and continues below Net Income to include OCI items, ending with Total Comprehensive Income
Beginning balance of retained earnings
1-8.e: One-Statement Approach: a single continuous statement starting with Revenue (top), flowing through to Net Income (same as a standard income statement), then continuing to OCI items (unrealized G/L, translation adjustments, etc.), and ending with Total Comprehensive Income. No separate OCI statement needed. This approach shows the full income picture from top revenue to total comprehensive income in one document.

1-8.q: Total Comprehensive Income equals:
o: Net income only — OCI is excluded from total comprehensive income
Gross profit plus OCI
Net Income Plus All Other Comprehensive Income Items — representing the total change in equity from non-owner sources during the period
Net income minus dividends paid
1-8.e: Total Comprehensive Income = Net Income + Other Comprehensive Income. This represents All changes in equity Except transactions with owners (issuing/repurchasing stock, paying dividends, owner contributions). It is the 'complete' income measure. Significance: a company can have positive Net Income but negative OCI (e.g., large pension loss or adverse Fx translation), resulting in Total Comprehensive Income less than Net Income or even negative.

1-8.q: Under GAAP, Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (Aoci) on the balance sheet represents:
o: Current year OCI only
Retained earnings accumulated over multiple years
The Cumulative Total Of All Prior OCI Items that have not yet been reclassified to net income — a running balance of all OCI
Dividends declared but not yet paid
1-8.e: Aoci is a Cumulative balance: (Beginning Aoci) + (Current Year OCI) − (Reclassifications to Net Income) = (Ending Aoci). It is a Balance Sheet component of equity reflecting the total unrealized effects of OCI items accumulated over multiple periods. Example: if AFS unrealized gains were $5M last year and $3M this year, Aoci for AFS = $8M cumulative (before any sales/reclassifications). Aoci can be positive or negative.

1-8.q: A company has Net Income of $100M and OCI of negative $30M (due to pension losses). Total Comprehensive Income is:
o: $100M — OCI losses do not affect comprehensive income
$130M — add back the loss
$70M — Net Income plus the negative OCI
Negative $30M — only OCI is included in comprehensive income
1-8.e: Total Comprehensive Income = Net Income + OCI = $100M + (−$30M) = $70M. Even though the company earned $100M in Net Income, the $30M pension loss in OCI reduces Total equity by a net $70M from operations. The pension loss, while not in Net Income, is a real economic decrease in equity that eventually will be recognized through pension expense as it amortizes out of Aoci.

1-8.q: The Primary purpose of the Statement of Comprehensive Income is to:
o: Replace the income statement with a more comprehensive view
Present All Changes In Equity from non-owner transactions — both Net Income and OCI items — giving users a complete picture of economic changes beyond Net Income alone
Show only items that affect cash flows
Reconcile the beginning and ending cash balances
1-8.e: Purpose: shows the Total Picture of value created/destroyed from non-owner activities. Net Income alone excludes significant economic events (pension remeasurements, Fx translation, AFS security FV changes) that are real changes in equity value. Comprehensive income provides investors with: (1) current period OCI items; (2) total economic change; (3) insight into future income (as OCI items reclassify). Used in: ratio analysis adjusting for non-recurring items, assessing quality of earnings.

1-8.q: Under the two-statement approach, the second statement (the separate OCI statement) begins with:
o: Revenue from operations
Beginning balance of retained earnings
Net Income — the second statement starts where the first (income statement) ended, then adds OCI items to arrive at Total Comprehensive Income
Total Assets minus Total Liabilities
1-8.e: Two-Statement Approach: (1) First statement = income statement ending at Net Income; (2) Second statement = Statement of Comprehensive Income starting with Net Income then adding all OCI items ending with Total Comprehensive Income. The second statement Picks Up where the first left off — starting with the same Net Income figure. This approach separates traditional IS performance from OCI while still showing the complete comprehensive income picture.

1-8.q: Reclassification Adjustments are presented in the Statement of Comprehensive Income to:
o: Correct accounting errors from prior periods
Adjust Net Income for cash items
Prevent Double-Counting when OCI items that were previously recognized are reclassified to Net Income in the current period
Allocate tax expense between jurisdictions
1-8.e: Reclassification Adjustment: when an AFS gain (sitting in Aoci from prior periods) is realized by selling the security, it moves to Net Income. Without an adjustment, that gain would appear Twice in Total Comprehensive Income: once in prior period OCI And once in current period Net Income. The reclassification adjustment (negative OCI item) offsets the prior OCI, so the gain appears Only Once in cumulative comprehensive income totals.

1-8.q: Which of the following items is Included in Other Comprehensive Income (OCI) under US GAAP?
o: Gain on sale of investments classified as trading securities
Gain on sale of equipment (above book value)
Unrealized Holding Gain on an AFS debt security that management has not yet sold
Loss from discontinued operations
1-8.e: OCI Items under US GAAP: (1) Unrealized G/L on AFS Debt securities → OCI until sold; (2) Foreign currency translation adjustment; (3) Pension remeasurement G/L and prior service cost; (4) Unrealized G/L on qualifying cash flow hedges. Not OCI: trading security G/L → Net Income; gains on asset sales → Net Income; discontinued operations → Net Income (below continuing operations line). The AFS unrealized gain is the prototypical OCI example.

1-9.q: The First note in financial statements is always:
o: Related party transactions
Contingencies
Summary of significant accounting policies
Earnings per share
1-9.e: The Summary Of Significant Accounting Policies is always the First note. It describes key accounting choices: inventory costing method, depreciation method, revenue recognition policy, consolidation policy, etc. — essential for interpreting all other financial data.

1-9.q: A fire destroys a warehouse two weeks after the Balance Sheet date but before the FS are issued. This is a:
o: Recognized subsequent event — adjust the FS
Non-recognized subsequent event — disclose in notes only
Not required to be disclosed
Extraordinary item
1-9.e: The fire occurred After the Balance Sheet date — the condition did Not exist at BS date. This is a Non-Recognized subsequent event: Disclose in notes but do Not adjust the FS. The opposite (adjust) applies if the condition existed at the BS date.

1-9.q: A lawsuit was filed before year-end. After year-end but before FS issuance, the court rules and orders payment of $500,000. This is a:
o: Non-recognized event — disclose only
Recognized subsequent event — Adjust the FS to record the liability
Not required to be disclosed
Treated as a prior period adjustment
1-9.e: The lawsuit Existed at the BS date — only the final amount became known after. This is a Recognized subsequent event: Adjust the FS to record the $500,000 liability. The condition (the lawsuit) existed; the post-BS event just clarified the amount.

1-9.q: The Statement of Changes in Stockholders' Equity shows which of the following?
o: Only retained earnings changes
Changes in All equity components: common stock, Apic, Re, Aoci, treasury stock
Cash flow information
Income tax calculations
1-9.e: The Statement of Changes in Equity shows All Changes in Every equity component: common stock (par), Apic, retained earnings (net income minus dividends), accumulated OCI (other comprehensive income), and treasury stock. It bridges beginning and ending equity balances.

1-9.q: Fair value Level 1 inputs are Best described as:
o: Management's internal estimates
Unobservable inputs
Quoted prices in active markets for identical assets
Prices from similar but not identical assets
1-9.e: Level 1 = Quoted Prices in Active Markets for Identical assets or liabilities. Most reliable — directly observable market prices (e.g., stock prices). Level 2 = observable but indirect. Level 3 = unobservable (management estimates) — least reliable.

1-9.q: The Statement Of Changes In Stockholders' Equity shows:
o: Only the change in retained earnings during the period
Only net income and dividends declared
The Beginning And Ending Balances Of All Equity Components and All Changes during the period — including net income, OCI, dividends, stock issuances, and treasury stock transactions
Only transactions that affect cash
1-9.e: Statement Of Changes In Stockholders' Equity: a rollforward of Each equity component (Common Stock, Apic, Retained Earnings, Aoci, Treasury Stock). Shows: beginning balance + all additions (NI, OCI, stock issuances) − all deductions (dividends, treasury stock purchases) = ending balance. Required under both GAAP and IFRS. Provides: complete history of equity changes; reconciliation that the BS equity totals are correct; details on dividend policy.

1-9.q: The First Note to financial statements is Always:
o: Contingencies and legal proceedings
Subsequent events disclosure
The Summary Of Significant Accounting Policies — always presented as Note 1, disclosing the key choices (revenue recognition, inventory method, depreciation method, consolidation policy, etc.)
Earnings per share calculation
1-9.e: First Note = Summary Of Significant Accounting Policies: always presented first. Content: (1) Basis of presentation (GAAP or IFRS); (2) Consolidation principles; (3) Revenue recognition; (4) Inventory valuation method (FIFO, LIFO, weighted avg); (5) Depreciation methods and useful lives; (6) Any other significant accounting policy choices. This note allows users to understand How the numbers were prepared — essential context for all other notes.

1-9.q: A Subsequent Event that provides additional evidence about Conditions That Existed at the balance sheet date should be:
o: Disclosed in notes but not adjusted in the financial statements
Ignored — only events during the reporting period matter
Recorded As An Adjustment to the financial statements — because the condition existed at the balance sheet date, the financial statements should reflect this information
Reported to the SEC within 4 business days
1-9.e: Adjusting Subsequent Events (Type I): events occurring After the balance sheet date but Before the financial statements are issued that provide Evidence about conditions that Existed at the balance sheet date → Adjust the financial statements. Examples: settling a lawsuit that existed at year-end for a different amount than estimated; determining a customer that owed Ar at year-end has filed for bankruptcy. The condition (lawsuit, credit risk) Existed at year-end — new information just changes the measurement.

1-9.q: A Subsequent Event that provides evidence about Conditions That Arose after the balance sheet date should be:
o: Recorded as an adjustment to the financial statements
Ignored — it is not part of the reporting period
Disclosed In The Notes only (not adjusted) — the condition did not exist at the balance sheet date, so financial statements should not be changed
Included in next period's financial statements only
1-9.e: Non-Adjusting Subsequent Events (Type Ii): events occurring After the balance sheet date that provide evidence about conditions that Arose After the balance sheet date → Disclose In Notes Only — do not adjust the statements. Examples: major natural disaster (flood destroys plant after year-end), major acquisition announced after year-end, significant new debt issuance. The condition Did Not Exist at year-end — no adjustment appropriate, but disclosure needed for complete picture.

1-9.q: The Fair Value Hierarchy under ASC 820 ranks inputs into Three levels. Level 1 inputs are:
o: Management's own assumptions and models
Observable inputs from comparable but not identical assets
Quoted Prices In Active Markets For Identical Assets Or Liabilities — the most reliable evidence of fair value
Historical cost of the asset
1-9.e: Fair Value Hierarchy (ASC 820): Level 1 (most reliable): quoted prices in Active Markets for Identical assets/liabilities — e.g., share price of Apple stock on Nasdaq. Level 2: observable inputs (not Level 1) — similar assets, market corroborated data, yield curves. Level 3 (least reliable): Unobservable inputs — management's own assumptions, discounted cash flow models. Notes must disclose: level of FV hierarchy used for each class of asset/liability, and reconciliation of Level 3 inputs.

1-9.q: Level 3 Fair Value Measurements are considered Least reliable because:
o: They are based on complex mathematical formulas
They require external appraiser involvement
They Use Unobservable Inputs — management's assumptions and models, not verifiable market data
They are calculated only at year-end
1-9.e: Level 3 FV: unobservable inputs — management cannot find observable market data and must use their own Internal Assumptions (DCF models with management's estimates of future cash flows and discount rates). Less reliable because: (1) management may be biased; (2) cannot be verified against market; (3) small changes in assumptions produce large FV changes. Required disclosures: description of valuation technique, significant unobservable inputs used, sensitivity of FV to changes in unobservable inputs.

1-9.q: Related Party Transactions must be disclosed in the notes because:
o: They are always fraudulent or illegal
They are always material to the financial statements
Related Party Transactions May Not Be On Arm'S Length Terms — they could be structured to benefit insiders at the expense of the company or other shareholders
They require SEC approval before completion
1-9.e: Related Party Disclosures (ASC 850): transactions between the reporting entity and parties that have a Special Relationship (parent-subsidiary, officer, director, significant shareholder, family members of officers). Required disclosures: nature of the relationship, description of transactions, amounts, any amounts still outstanding. Why: related party transactions may Not reflect arm's length pricing — management could manipulate earnings by pricing inter-company transactions favorably. Users need this context to evaluate the financial statements.

1-9.q: Contingent Liabilities must be disclosed in the Notes when the loss is classified as:
o: Remote — disclose all possible losses
Reasonably Possible — possible but not probable, requiring note disclosure without balance sheet recognition
Probable and estimable — these are recorded on the balance sheet instead
Both probable and reasonably possible — all contingent liabilities require notes
1-9.e: Contingency Disclosure: (1) Probable + Estimable → Accrue on balance sheet (And disclose in notes); (2) Reasonably Possible → Disclose In Notes Only (not accrued); (3) Remote → generally no disclosure. Note disclosure for reasonably possible/accrued: nature of contingency, estimate of possible loss range (or statement that estimate cannot be made). The notes fill in the detail that users need to understand the potential financial impact.

1-9.q: A company discovers a Material Subsequent Event before the financial statements are issued (but after the balance sheet date). The period during which subsequent events must be considered is:
o: Only the 5 business days after fiscal year-end
Between The Balance Sheet Date And The Date The Financial Statements Are Issued Or Available To Be Issued
Unlimited — all future events must be considered
Only events occurring in the same fiscal year
1-9.e: Subsequent Events Period: from the Balance Sheet Date to the date the financial statements are Issued (public companies) or Available To Be Issued (non-public companies). Management must actively search for events in this window. Examples: if fiscal year ends Dec 31 and FS issued Feb 28 — events occurring Jan 1–Feb 28 are potential subsequent events. The period can be weeks to months depending on how quickly the FS are completed.

1-9.q: Segment Information in the notes (ASC 280) is required for public companies to disclose:
o: Segment information only if segments are unprofitable
Information about each country of operation only
Information About Operating Segments that the Chief Operating Decision Maker (Codm) uses internally — revenue, profit/loss, and assets for each reportable segment
Only the most profitable segment's details
1-9.e: ASC 280 Segment Reporting: public companies must disclose information about Reportable Operating Segments based on How Management Internally Reports (the 'management approach'). Reportable if: revenue ≥ 10% of total revenue, Or profit/loss ≥ 10% of combined segment profit, Or assets ≥ 10% of total assets. Required disclosures per segment: revenues, profit/loss, assets, and reconciliation to consolidated totals. Enables users to assess which parts of the business drive performance.

1-9.q: Accounting Policy Disclosures in the notes include information about:
o: Only changes to accounting policies during the current period
Estimates only — not policies
Significant Policies That Management Has Chosen where alternatives exist — such as inventory method (FIFO vs. weighted avg), depreciation method (Sl vs. Ddb), and revenue recognition approach
All policies from the company's founding date
1-9.e: Accounting Policy Disclosures: Note 1 discloses the Significant Accounting Policies — the choices management made from acceptable alternatives under GAAP/IFRS. Why Important: two companies can report very different numbers for identical economic events if they use different (both acceptable) accounting methods. Knowing FIFO vs. LIFO, straight-line vs. accelerated depreciation, or specific revenue recognition methods is Essential for meaningful comparison.

1-9.q: When a company changes an Accounting Policy (e.g., switching from LIFO to FIFO), the change is generally accounted for:
o: Prospectively — applied only going forward
As a prior period error correction
Retrospectively — all prior periods presented are restated as if the new policy had always been applied, with cumulative effect to beginning retained earnings
As a special item on the current year income statement
1-9.e: Accounting Policy Change (ASC 250): Retrospective Treatment — restate All prior period financial statements to reflect the new policy as if always used. Cumulative effect of the change on All periods before the earliest presented = adjustment to Beginning Retained Earnings. Exception: if impracticable to determine retrospective effect → prospective treatment. Rationale: comparability across periods — financial statements should show what they Would Have Shown under the new policy all along.

1-9.q: The Notes section on Subsequent Events must disclose the:
o: Date of the auditors' report only
Total dollar value of all events occurring after year-end
Date Through Which Management Evaluated Subsequent Events — this tells users the boundary of the subsequent events review period
Names of all management personnel who reviewed events
1-9.e: Date Of Evaluation Disclosure: the notes must disclose the Date Through Which subsequent events were evaluated. For public companies: typically the date financial statements are issued. For non-public: date they are 'available to be issued.' This disclosure tells users: events after this date have Not been evaluated — they could be financially significant. Users can assess whether the evaluation period was sufficiently thorough.

1-9.q: Earnings Per Share (EPS) disclosures are presented:
o: Only in the notes — not on the face of the income statement for public companies
On The Face Of The Income Statement for public companies — both Basic and Diluted EPS must be shown as part of the primary financial statements
On the balance sheet as a supplementary statistic
Only in proxy statements to shareholders
1-9.e: EPS Presentation (ASC 260): public companies must present Basic and Diluted EPS on the Face Of The Income Statement. Not just notes — they are on the IS itself. Required for: income from continuing operations And net income (minimum). Private companies are Not required to present EPS. EPS is a key metric investors use to compare profitability across companies and assess the value of each share.

1-9.q: Commitments And Contingencies disclosed in the notes include all of the following Except:
o: Operating lease commitments (minimum future lease payments for certain leases)
Long-term purchase commitments to suppliers
Pending or threatened litigation
Payment Of Regular Quarterly Dividends — these are normal owner transactions, not contingencies or commitments requiring special disclosure
1-9.e: Commitments And Contingencies: disclose Obligations and Potential Obligations not fully recognized on the balance sheet. Examples: (1) Operating Lease future minimum payments; (2) Purchase Commitments (contracted to buy inventory at fixed prices — executory contracts); (3) Litigation (probable/reasonably possible losses); (4) Guarantees (of third-party debt); (5) Letters Of Credit. Regular dividends are a normal capital distribution decision, not a contingent or committed obligation requiring this type of disclosure.

1-10.q: Under US GAAP, interest Paid is classified in the Scf as:
o: Investing activity
Financing activity
Operating activity
Non-cash disclosure
1-10.e: Under US GAAP, interest Paid is classified as an Operating activity. Under IFRS, it can be classified as operating Or financing. This GAAP vs IFRS difference on interest and dividends classification is frequently tested.

1-10.q: Dividends Paid to shareholders are classified as which activity under US GAAP?
o: Operating
Investing
Financing
Non-cash
1-10.e: Under US GAAP, dividends Paid are Financing activities (returning cash to equity investors). Under IFRS, dividends paid can be classified as Operating or Financing. Dividends Received are operating under GAAP (investing or operating under IFRS).

1-10.q: Purchase of Pp&E for cash is classified as:
o: Operating activity
Investing activity — cash outflow
Financing activity
Non-cash disclosure
1-10.e: Purchasing Pp&E (long-term assets) is an Investing activity — a cash Outflow. Selling Pp&E produces an investing cash Inflow. All transactions involving long-term assets are investing activities.

1-10.q: A company buys equipment by signing a long-term note payable. In the Scf, this is reported as:
o: Both investing outflow and financing inflow
A non-cash investing and financing disclosure (no cash involved)
Operating activity
Investing outflow only
1-10.e: When equipment is acquired through a Note (no cash changes hands), it is a Non-Cash Investing And Financing transaction. GAAP requires it to be Disclosed separately (in a supplementary schedule) but it does Not appear in the three main sections of the Scf since no cash was paid.

1-10.q: The Indirect Method for Operating Activities starts with:
o: Cash received from customers
Net Income
Total revenues
Gross profit
1-10.e: The Indirect Method starts with Net Income and adjusts it to arrive at Cash From Operations by: adding back non-cash charges (depreciation, amortization), and adjusting for changes in working capital accounts (Ar, inventory, AP, etc.).

1-10.q: Taxes paid are classified in the Scf as:
o: Investing activity
Financing activity
Operating activity
Always a non-cash item
1-10.e: Income taxes paid are classified as Operating activities on the Scf — they relate to the core business operations. (Exception: if specifically identifiable with an investing or financing transaction, they can be classified accordingly — rare in practice.)

1-10.q: Under US GAAP, Interest Paid on debt is classified in the Scf as:
o: Investing activities — since interest relates to financing the company's assets
Financing activities — since interest is a cost of financing
Operating Activities — US GAAP requires interest paid to be classified as operating
Either operating or financing — management's choice under GAAP
1-10.e: Interest Paid — US GAAP: Operating Activities (required). Rationale: interest is an income statement item (expense) and operating cash flows correspond to IS items. IFRS: Flexible — interest paid can be classified as Operating or Financing. This GAAP/IFRS difference is frequently tested. Practical note: under GAAP, cash paid for interest is reconciled from interest expense using the beginning/ending accrued interest balance.

1-10.q: Under US GAAP, Dividends Paid to shareholders are classified in the Scf as:
o: Operating activities — since dividends relate to business performance
Financing Activities — dividends paid are a return of capital to owners, which is a financing activity
Investing activities — since dividends relate to investment returns
Either financing or operating — management's choice under GAAP
1-10.e: Dividends Paid — US GAAP: Financing Activities (required). Rationale: paying dividends is a Capital Distribution to owners — a financing decision (returning capital, not operating the business). IFRS: Flexible — dividends paid can be Financing or Operating. Note: Dividends Received are Operating under GAAP (IFRS: can be operating or investing). These classifications matter for the quality of operating cash flows analysis.

1-10.q: The Indirect Method for presenting operating activities in the Scf starts with:
o: Total revenues from the income statement
Total cash received from customers
Net Income — and then adjusts for non-cash items and changes in working capital to arrive at net cash from operating activities
Cash balance at the beginning of the period
1-10.e: Indirect Method (most common): Start with Net Income (accrual basis), then: Add Back non-cash expenses (depreciation, amortization, impairment). Add/Subtract changes in working capital: Ar increase → subtract (earned but not collected); Inventory decrease → add (sold without purchasing more); AP increase → add (expense incurred but not paid). Result = Net Cash From Operating Activities. The reconciliation explains Why operating cash flows differ from net income.

1-10.q: In the indirect method Scf, Depreciation Expense is Added Back to Net Income because:
o: Depreciation is a source of cash
Depreciation increases cash flows from investing activities
Depreciation IS A Non-Cash Expense that reduced Net Income but did Not require a cash outflow — adding it back converts accrual NI toward cash basis
Depreciation reduces taxes and therefore indirectly improves cash
1-10.e: Depreciation Add-Back: depreciation Reduced Net Income (deducted as an expense) but involved No Cash Payment in the current period (cash was paid when the asset was acquired — already captured in investing activities). Adding it back Reverses the non-cash deduction. Note: depreciation is Not a 'source of cash' — it is a non-cash item. The add-back is simply an unwinding of an accrual-basis reduction that never affected cash.

1-10.q: An Increase In Accounts Receivable during the period in the indirect method Scf is:
o: Added to Net Income — more Ar means more revenue recognized
Ignored — Ar is not a cash item
Subtracted from Net Income — revenue was Recognized (earned) but Cash Was Not Collected (Ar increased), so accrual-basis income overstates cash received from customers
Classified as an investing activity
1-10.e: Ar Increase → Subtract: Revenue is recognized when earned (increases NI) but if Ar increases, the cash Hasn'T been collected yet. Net Income overstates cash from customers by the Ar increase. Formula: Cash from Customers = Revenue − Increase in Ar (or + Decrease in Ar). In the indirect method, working capital changes: Asset Increases → subtract (cash not received or extra cash used); Asset Decreases → add; Liability Increases → add (cash not yet paid); Liability Decreases → subtract.

1-10.q: An Increase In Accounts Payable during the period in the indirect method Scf is:
o: Subtracted from Net Income — more AP means more expense recognized
Classified as a financing activity
Added to Net Income — expense was Incurred (reducing NI) but Cash Was Not Paid (AP increased), so accrual-basis income understates cash available
Ignored — AP is a long-term financing item
1-10.e: AP Increase → Add: Expenses are recognized when incurred (reduces NI) but if AP increases, the cash Hasn'T been paid yet. Net Income understates cash available because the expense was 'paid' by increasing a payable, not by using cash. Working capital: Liability Increases → Add to NI (not yet paid in cash, so NI understated cash); Liability Decreases → Subtract (cash paid beyond what expense was recognized).

1-10.q: Non-Cash Investing And Financing Activities are disclosed in the Scf:
o: As part of the investing section since they involve long-term assets
Within the financing section for all financing activities
As A Supplemental Disclosure — separate from the three main sections — because they represent significant activities that don't involve cash but affect the balance sheet
Not disclosed — only cash transactions are shown in the Scf
1-10.e: Non-Cash Supplemental Disclosure: significant investing/financing activities that Don'T involve cash must be separately disclosed (on the face or in notes to the Scf). Examples: issuing stock to acquire land (investing + financing, no cash); converting debt to equity; acquiring equipment through a finance lease; exchanging property. Why disclose: these transactions significantly affect the balance sheet and future cash flows — users need to see them even though they don't flow through the three Scf sections.

1-10.q: The Direct Method for presenting operating activities shows:
o: The same result as the indirect method — only the presentation differs, not the total amount
Net Income adjusted for non-cash items and working capital changes
Actual Cash Receipts (from customers) and Cash Payments (to suppliers, employees, taxes) — each major category listed explicitly
Cash flows from investing activities in a direct format
1-10.e: Direct Method: shows Specific Cash Flows directly — Cash received from customers, Cash paid to suppliers, Cash paid to employees, Cash paid for taxes, Cash paid for interest, etc. Each major operating cash flow is listed explicitly. Indirect Method: starts with Net Income and adjusts for non-cash items and working capital changes. Both methods produce the Same Total net cash from operating activities — only the presentation differs. GAAP recommends the direct method but most companies use the indirect method (simpler to prepare). If the direct method is used, a reconciliation of Net Income to operating cash flows must also be provided.

1-10.q: Purchase Of Equipment is classified in the Scf as:
o: Operating activities — because the equipment is used in operations
Financing activities — because equipment may be debt-financed
An Investing Activity — acquisition of long-term assets (Pp&E) represents an investment in productive capacity
A supplemental disclosure only
1-10.e: Investing Activities: include all cash flows related to Acquisition And Disposal of Long-Term Assets and Investments. Examples: purchasing/selling equipment (Pp&E), purchasing/selling investments (equity and debt securities), making/collecting loans to others, purchasing/disposing of entire businesses (acquisitions). Note: the Financing of the equipment (loan proceeds) is separately classified as financing activities — the two transactions are reported separately.

1-10.q: Dividends Received from investments are classified in the Scf under US GAAP as:
o: Financing activities — related to returns on investments
Investing activities — since they relate to investing in equity securities
Operating Activities — US GAAP requires dividends received to be operating activities
Either investing or operating — management's choice under GAAP
1-10.e: Dividends Received — US GAAP: Operating Activities (required). Rationale: dividends received are a return on the investment — they flow through the income statement as investment income and therefore are operating. IFRS: Flexible — dividends received can be classified as Operating or Investing. Note the GAAP difference: dividends Paid = financing; dividends Received = operating (under US GAAP).

1-10.q: A Decrease In Inventory during the period in the indirect method Scf is:
o: Subtracted from Net Income — more inventory sold means more COGS
Added to investing activities — inventory is a balance sheet asset
Added to Net Income — inventory decreased because goods were Sold without a corresponding New Purchase, providing more cash than COGS suggests
Classified as a financing activity
1-10.e: Inventory Decrease → Add: COGS reflected the cost of goods sold (reducing NI), but if Ending inventory < Beginning inventory, the company sold existing inventory Without buying new — getting cash benefit beyond what NI shows. Net Income Understates cash because inventory decreased (used up inventory to generate cash). Working capital rule: Asset Decrease → Add (provides cash); Asset Increase → Subtract (uses cash).

1-10.q: Proceeds From Issuing Long-Term Bonds are classified in the Scf as:
o: Operating activities — since bond proceeds fund operations
Investing activities — bonds represent a long-term financial investment
Financing Activities — borrowing from creditors is a financing activity
A supplemental non-cash disclosure
1-10.e: Financing Activities: include all cash flows related to the Entity'S Capital Structure — borrowing from and repaying creditors, and issuing equity to and returning capital to owners. Examples: proceeds from bonds/notes payable (inflow), repayment of debt principal (outflow), issuing stock (inflow), paying dividends (outflow), repurchasing treasury stock (outflow). Note: Interest Paid on the bonds = Operating (under US GAAP), but the Principal Borrowed = Financing.

1-10.q: The Three Sections of the Statement of Cash Flows are organized to show:
o: Short-term, medium-term, and long-term cash flows
Cash flows from domestic, international, and corporate activities
Operating (day-to-day business), Investing (long-term assets and investments), and Financing (capital structure) — matching the three main categories of business financial activity
Past, present, and future cash flows
1-10.e: Scf Three Sections: (1) Operating — cash generated from or used by the core business operations (selling goods/services, paying employees and suppliers) — most important for sustainability assessment; (2) Investing — buying/selling long-term assets and investments — reflects growth/maintenance spending; (3) Financing — borrowing, repaying, issuing equity, paying dividends — reflects capital structure management. Together: Operating generates cash; Investing uses it for growth; Financing shows how any shortfall or surplus is managed with external capital.

1-10.q: Cash Equivalents included in the Scf are defined as:
o: Any investment held by the company
All current assets that are not inventory
Short-Term, Highly Liquid Investments with original maturities of Three Months Or Less that are readily convertible to known amounts of cash and subject to insignificant risk of changes in value
All bank deposits regardless of maturity
1-10.e: Cash Equivalents definition: investments that are (1) Short-Term — original maturity of 3 Months Or Less at time of purchase; (2) Highly Liquid — readily convertible to a known cash amount; (3) Insignificant Value Risk — so close to maturity that interest rate changes have minimal impact. Examples: Treasury bills, money market funds, commercial paper maturing in 90 days. Not cash equivalents: stocks, bonds maturing in 6 months, or savings accounts with restrictions. The Scf explains the change in Cash And Cash Equivalents combined.

1-11.q: Depreciation expense is Added Back in the indirect method because:
o: It increases cash flow
It is a Non-Cash expense that reduced net income but required no cash payment
It is a financing activity
Depreciation always improves liquidity
1-11.e: Depreciation is a Non-Cash charge — it reduced Net Income but No Cash was paid. Starting from Net Income (which was reduced by depreciation), we Add IT Back to convert to cash basis. The cash was actually spent when the asset was purchased (investing activity).

1-11.q: Accounts Receivable increased by $30,000 during the year. In the indirect method, this is:
o: Added to Net Income — Ar increase = cash inflow
Subtracted from Net Income — earned revenue but cash not yet collected
Ignored — Ar is not a cash item
Added as a financing activity
1-11.e: Ar Increase means revenue was recognized (IS) but cash was Not collected. Net Income includes this revenue, but no cash was received. Subtract $30,000 from Net Income to convert to cash basis (cash collected < revenue earned).

1-11.q: Accounts Payable decreased by $20,000. In the indirect method, this is:
o: Added — AP decrease means less cash needed
Subtracted — AP decrease means More Cash was paid than current period expense
Ignored — AP is non-operating
Added as investing activity
1-11.e: AP Decrease means the company Paid Down its AP — paid More cash than it incurred as an expense in the current period. Net Income does not reflect this extra cash outflow. Subtract $20,000 from Net Income.

1-11.q: A gain on sale of equipment of $15,000 is Subtracted in the indirect method because:
o: Gains are unfavorable
The actual cash from the sale is reported in Investing Activities — reporting it in operations would double-count
Gains reduce taxes
Equipment sales are financing activities
1-11.e: The gain is already included in Net Income. However, the Actual Cash Received from the sale is reported in Investing Activities (in full). If we don't subtract the gain in Operations, the cash would be double-counted. Subtract the gain to remove it from Operations.

1-11.q: Inventory decreased by $40,000. In the indirect method, this adjustment is:
o: Subtracted — inventory decrease reduces assets
Added — using inventory generated a source of cash (sold more than bought)
Ignored — inventory is not cash
Subtracted as investing outflow
1-11.e: Inventory Decrease means the company Used more inventory than it purchased (sold more than bought). The cost of using this inventory was included in COGS (reducing Net Income), but no additional Cash was needed to buy it. Add $40,000 — it's a source of cash.

1-11.q: Net Income = $100,000. Depreciation = $20,000. Ar increased $10,000. AP increased $8,000. Cash from Operations =
1-11.e: Cash from Ops = $100,000 + $20,000 (add back dep) − $10,000 (Ar increase) + $8,000 (AP increase) = $118,000.

1-11.q: Which item is Subtracted in the indirect method for operating cash flows?
o: Depreciation expense
Decrease in accounts receivable
Increase in prepaid expenses
Increase in accrued liabilities
1-11.e: Increase in Prepaid Expenses = current asset Increase = Use Of Cash = Subtract from net income. The company paid cash that is not yet recognized as expense (income is overstated vs cash spent). All current asset increases are subtracted.

1-11.q: Prepaid expenses Increased by $5,000 during the year. In the indirect method, this is:
o: Added back — it is a non-cash item
Subtracted — cash was paid but expense not yet recognized
Ignored — it has no cash effect
Added back — it reduces operating expenses
1-11.e: An Increase in a prepaid asset means cash was paid but Not yet expensed. Cash outflow exceeded the income statement charge, so you Subtract the increase to reconcile net income to cash from operations.

1-11.q: Accrued liabilities Increased by $12,000. In the indirect method, this adjustment is:
o: Subtracted — it represents a future cash payment
Added back — expense was recognized but cash not yet paid
Ignored — accruals are non-cash
Subtracted — it represents an asset increase
1-11.e: An Increase in accrued liabilities means the company recognized an expense on the income statement but has Not yet paid cash. Net income was reduced by the expense, but no cash left — so you Add Back to reconcile.

1-11.q: Amortization of intangible assets (e.g., patents) appears in the indirect method as:
o: Subtracted — it is a cash outflow
Added back — it is a non-cash charge against income
Ignored — intangibles are not operating items
Classified under investing activities
1-11.e: Like depreciation, amortization reduces net income but involves No cash outflow. It is a non-cash expense and must be Added Back to net income in the indirect method.

1-11.q: A Loss on sale of investments of $8,000 appears in the indirect method as:
o: Subtracted — losses reduce cash
Added back — the loss was non-operating and reduced net income
Ignored — gains and losses are excluded
Subtracted — the proceeds go to investing activities
1-11.e: A loss on sale Reduced net income, but the full cash proceeds are reported in Investing Activities. To avoid double-counting, you Add Back the loss in the operating section. (Conversely, gains are subtracted.)

1-11.q: Deferred tax liability Increased by $4,000. In the indirect method, this is:
o: Subtracted — it is a future cash obligation
Added back — taxes were expensed but not yet paid in cash
Ignored — deferred taxes are non-operating
Classified as a financing activity
1-11.e: An increase in deferred tax liability means income tax expense exceeded cash taxes paid. Net income was reduced by the full expense, but less cash left — so the increase is Added Back.

1-11.q: Unearned revenue Increased by $9,000. In the indirect method, this is:
o: Subtracted — it is not yet earned
Added back — cash was received but not yet recognized as revenue
Ignored — it is a liability account
Subtracted — liabilities always reduce CFO
1-11.e: An increase in unearned revenue means cash was Received but revenue has Not been recognized on the income statement. Net income understates cash collected, so you Add Back the increase.

1-11.q: Net Income = $180,000. Depreciation = $25,000. Ar decreased $10,000. Inventory increased $15,000. AP increased $5,000. Cash from Operations =
1-11.e: Start: $180,000 + $25,000 (depreciation addback) + $10,000 (Ar decrease = cash inflow) − $15,000 (inventory increase = cash outflow) + $5,000 (AP increase = cash saved) = $205,000.

1-11.q: Under the indirect method, the starting point is:
o: Cash collected from customers
Gross profit
Net income
Total revenues
1-11.e: The indirect method Starts with net income (bottom of the income statement) and then makes adjustments for non-cash items and working capital changes to arrive at cash from operating activities.

1-11.q: Income tax payable Decreased by $3,000. In the indirect method, this is:
o: Added back — taxes reduced net income
Subtracted — more cash was paid for taxes than was expensed
Ignored — tax items are non-operating
Added back — it is a non-cash item
1-11.e: A Decrease in income tax payable means the company paid More cash in taxes than was recognized as expense. Net income overstates the cash retained, so you Subtract the decrease.

1-11.q: Writing off an uncollectible account reduces both Ar and the Allowance for Credit Losses. Its effect on Cash from Operations (indirect method) is:
o: Decreases CFO — it represents a cash loss
Increases CFO — it is a non-cash charge
No effect — both Ar and Allowance decrease equally
Decreases CFO — Ar decreased
1-11.e: A write-off Reduces both gross Ar and the allowance by equal amounts. Net Ar (and therefore the working capital adjustment) is Unchanged. There is no cash effect, so there is no adjustment needed.

1-11.q: Which of the following would Increase cash from operating activities in the indirect method?
o: An increase in accounts receivable
An increase in inventory
A decrease in accounts payable
A decrease in prepaid expenses
1-11.e: A Decrease in prepaid expenses means less cash was paid for prepayments than was expensed — cash outflow was lower than the income charge. This Adds to CFO. The others (Ar up, inventory up, AP down) all represent cash uses or cash reductions.

1-11.q: The Indirect method of presenting operating cash flows is preferred by most companies because:
o: It provides more detailed cash flow information than the direct method
It is required by IFRS but optional under US GAAP
It is easier to prepare since it begins with net income already reported
It shows actual cash receipts and payments from operations
1-11.e: The indirect method starts with net income from the income statement and makes adjustments — it requires no additional cash tracking. The Direct method requires detailed cash receipts/payments data, which is more work to compile. Both are allowed under GAAP and IFRS.

1-11.q: A $20,000 gain on sale of land is shown in the indirect method as:
o: Added back — gains are non-cash
Subtracted — to remove it from operating income
Ignored — land is not an operating asset
Added to investing activities only
1-11.e: The gain is Included in net income but belongs in Investing Activities (where the full proceeds appear). To prevent double-counting, the gain is Subtracted in the operating section. The full cash received from the sale is then shown as an investing cash inflow.

1-12.q: A company sells equipment with a book value of $50,000 for $65,000 cash. In the Scf, the Investing Activities section shows:
o: $50,000 inflow
$65,000 inflow
$15,000 inflow (the gain only)
$0 — it's in operating
1-12.e: The Full Cash Proceeds of $65,000 appear as an Investing inflow. The $15,000 gain is removed from Operating Activities (subtracted in indirect method). Never show just the book value or just the gain — always the Full Cash Received.

1-12.q: Repayment of long-term debt Principal is classified as:
o: Operating outflow
Investing outflow
Financing outflow
Non-cash disclosure
1-12.e: Repayment of debt Principal is a Financing outflow — it reduces the financing liability. Note: Interest Payments on the debt are Operating (under GAAP). The two components of a debt payment are classified differently.

1-12.q: A company acquires $100,000 of equipment by signing a note payable. On the Scf, this is reported:
o: $100,000 investing outflow and $100,000 financing inflow
As a $100,000 investing outflow only
As a non-cash investing and financing disclosure — no cash section
Not reported at all
1-12.e: No cash changed hands — the equipment was financed entirely by a note. This Non-Cash transaction is disclosed in a Supplementary Schedule or footnote to the Scf. It does Not appear in any of the three operating/investing/financing sections.

1-12.q: Issuance of common stock for cash is classified as:
o: Operating inflow
Investing inflow
Financing inflow
Non-cash transaction
1-12.e: Issuing stock for cash is a Financing inflow — it represents receiving cash from equity investors (a financing source). Stock repurchases (treasury stock) are financing outflows.

1-12.q: The Scf requires disclosure of cash paid for interest and income taxes when using:
o: The direct method only
The indirect method for operating activities
Either method
Only if amounts are material
1-12.e: When using the Indirect Method for operating activities, companies must disclose (supplementally) actual Cash Paid for Interest and Income Taxes. The indirect method doesn't directly show these amounts, so separate disclosure is required.

1-12.q: Which of the following is an Investing activity?
o: Paying dividends to shareholders
Borrowing from a bank
Purchasing equity securities as long-term investments
Paying interest on a bond
1-12.e: Purchasing equity securities as Long-Term Investments is an Investing activity (acquiring/disposing of long-term assets and investments). Paying dividends = Financing. Borrowing = Financing. Paying interest = Operating (GAAP).

1-12.q: Purchase of land for cash is classified in the Scf as:
o: Operating activity
Financing activity
Investing activity
Non-cash disclosure
1-12.e: Purchases and sales of Long-Term Assets (land, buildings, equipment, securities) are Investing activities. Cash paid to acquire land is an investing cash Outflow.

1-12.q: Dividends Paid to a company's own shareholders are classified as:
o: Operating activity under US GAAP
Financing activity under US GAAP
Investing activity under both GAAP and IFRS
Either operating or financing under US GAAP
1-12.e: Under US GAAP, dividends Paid are a Financing outflow (returning capital to owners). Under IFRS, companies may classify dividends paid as either financing Or operating. US GAAP = financing only.

1-12.q: A company collects the Principal on a loan it had made to another company. This is classified as:
o: Operating activity
Financing activity
Investing inflow
Non-cash activity
1-12.e: Making and collecting loans to Others is an Investing activity. Lending money = investing outflow. Collecting the principal back = investing Inflow. (Note: interest received = operating or investing depending on GAAP vs IFRS.)

1-12.q: Purchase of Held-to-Maturity bonds as an investment is classified as:
o: Operating activity — interest is an operating item
Financing activity — bonds involve debt
Investing activity — purchase of a financial asset
Non-cash activity
1-12.e: Buying investment securities (HTM, AFS, or trading in some interpretations) as long-term investments is an Investing activity. Cash paid to acquire the bonds = investing outflow.

1-12.q: Under US GAAP, dividends Received from investments are classified as:
o: Investing activity
Financing activity
Operating activity
Either operating or investing
1-12.e: Under US GAAP, dividends Received are classified as Operating. Under IFRS, dividends received may be classified as operating Or investing. This is a common exam distinction.

1-12.q: Proceeds from issuing bonds payable (long-term debt) is classified as:
o: Investing inflow
Operating inflow
Financing inflow
Non-cash activity
1-12.e: Issuing Debt is a Financing activity — it raises capital from lenders. Cash received from bond issuance = financing Inflow. Repaying the principal = financing Outflow.

1-12.q: A company repurchases its own common stock (treasury stock) for cash. This is classified as:
o: Investing outflow
Financing outflow
Operating outflow
Non-cash activity
1-12.e: Transactions with a company's Own shareholders (issuing stock, buying back stock, paying dividends) are Financing activities. Treasury stock repurchase = financing Outflow.

1-12.q: A company makes a $200,000 loan to a supplier. This is classified as:
o: Operating outflow — it is a supplier relationship
Financing outflow
Investing outflow
Non-cash disclosure
1-12.e: Making loans to Others is an Investing activity. Lending = investing outflow; collecting principal = investing inflow. Compare this to borrowing (which is financing).

1-12.q: A company sells an entire subsidiary for $500,000 cash. In the Scf, this appears as:
o: Operating inflow
Financing inflow
Investing inflow
Non-cash disclosure
1-12.e: Selling a subsidiary is the disposal of a long-term investment — an Investing activity. The cash proceeds = investing inflow. Any gain/loss on the sale is removed from operating activities (indirect method).

1-12.q: Under the Direct method, which item would appear in operating activities?
o: Proceeds from sale of equipment
Cash collected from customers
Purchase of investments
Repayment of bonds payable
1-12.e: The Direct method shows actual cash inflows and outflows from operations: cash Collected from customers, cash Paid to suppliers, cash paid for wages, taxes, etc. Equipment proceeds = investing; bond repayment = financing.

1-12.q: Significant non-cash investing and financing transactions (e.g., acquiring land by issuing stock) must be:
o: Included in investing activities at zero
Excluded from the Scf entirely
Disclosed in a supplemental schedule or notes
Included in operating activities
1-12.e: Non-cash transactions do not appear in the body of the Scf. However, they must be Disclosed in a supplemental schedule or notes to provide a complete picture of investing and financing activities.

1-12.q: Under US GAAP, interest Paid is classified as:
o: Financing activity
Investing activity
Operating activity
Either operating or financing
1-12.e: Under US GAAP, interest Paid = Operating. Under IFRS, interest paid may be classified as operating Or financing. This difference from IFRS is frequently tested.

1-12.q: The Direct method is preferred by the FASB and IASB because:
o: It is easier to prepare than the indirect method
It shows actual cash receipts and payments, giving more useful information
It does not require disclosure of non-cash activities
It reconciles net income to cash from operations
1-12.e: Standard setters Prefer the direct method because it shows actual cash flows (collected from customers, paid to suppliers, etc.) which is more informative. However, most companies use the indirect method because it is easier to prepare.

1-12.q: Which of the following is a Financing activity?
o: Purchase of a patent
Sale of manufacturing equipment
Payment of dividends to shareholders
Collection of a customer note receivable
1-12.e: Financing activities involve transactions with the company's capital providers: issuing/repaying debt, issuing/repurchasing stock, paying dividends. Payment of dividends = financing outflow. The others are investing activities.

1-13.q: The primary Purpose of Integrated Reporting is to:
o: Replace traditional financial statements
Communicate how an organization creates value over time across multiple capitals
Comply with SEC regulations
Report only environmental data
1-13.e: Integrated Reporting communicates how an organization creates Value over time by showing how it uses and affects All Six Capitals — not just financial. It is meant to complement, not replace, traditional financial statements.

1-13.q: How many 'capitals' does the International Ir Framework identify?
o: Four
Five
Six
Eight
1-13.e: The Ir Framework identifies Six Capitals: Financial, Manufactured, Intellectual, Human, Social & Relationship, and Natural. All six are used, affected, and transformed by an organization's activities in creating value.

1-13.q: Which of the following is Not one of the six capitals in the Ir Framework?
o: Natural capital
Intellectual capital
Regulatory capital
Social & Relationship capital
1-13.e: Regulatory capital is Not one of the six Ir capitals. The six are: Financial, Manufactured, Intellectual, Human, Social & Relationship, and Natural capital.

1-13.q: The guiding principle of Materiality in integrated reporting means:
o: Only financial information is reported
Disclosing all possible information
Focusing on matters that substantively affect the organization's ability to create value
Reporting only negative impacts
1-13.e: Materiality in Ir: focus on matters that Substantively Affect the organization's Ability To Create Value over the short, medium, and long term. This is broader than financial materiality — it includes non-financial factors that affect value creation.

1-13.q: Integrated Reporting was developed largely in response to:
o: SEC requirements
Recognition that traditional FS don't capture all value drivers and long-term risks
GAAP convergence with IFRS
The 2008 financial crisis only
1-13.e: Ir was developed because Traditional Financial Statements don't adequately capture All the factors that drive value — they focus on financial and manufactured capitals but miss intellectual, human, social, and natural capitals that are increasingly important to long-term performance.

1-13.q: The principle of Connectivity Of Information in Integrated Reporting means:
o: All financial statements must be audited
The report shows interdependencies between capitals, business activities, and value creation
The Ir must be filed with financial regulators
All subsidiaries must file separate integrated reports
1-13.e: Connectivity of information is a core Ir guiding principle — the integrated report must show How the various elements of the business model, strategy, performance, and capitals are interconnected and affect value creation over time.

1-13.q: The Integrated Reporting Framework focuses on value creation over:
o: The current reporting period only
Short, medium, and long-term time horizons
A 10-year strategic planning horizon
The short-term only, for investor decisions
1-13.e: Ir adopts a multi-horizon perspective — short, medium, and Long term. This distinguishes it from traditional financial reporting, which is largely backward-looking and focused on the current period.

1-13.q: The International Integrated Reporting Framework was developed by:
o: The FASB
The IASB
The International Integrated Reporting Council (Iirc)
The SEC
1-13.e: The Ir Framework was developed by the Iirc (International Integrated Reporting Council), which has since merged into the IFRS Foundation. The Iirc collaborated with businesses, investors, and regulators globally.

1-13.q: Manufactured capital in the Ir Framework refers to:
o: Financial assets and cash equivalents
