1-21.e: Switch to Sl when Sl amount (remaining Bv minus salvage ÷ remaining life) Exceeds Ddb. This ensures the full depreciable cost is allocated and the asset reaches salvage value.

1-21.q: A change in Estimated useful life is treated as:
o: Prior period error — restate
Cumulative-effect adjustment
Prospective change in accounting estimate
Change in principle — retrospective
1-21.e: Changes in useful life and salvage value are accounting Estimates — treated Prospectively. Spread remaining depreciable amount (Nbv minus new salvage) over new remaining life. No prior period restatement.

1-21.q: A change in Depreciation Method (Sl to Ddb) is treated as:
o: Change in estimate — prospective
Prior period error
Change in accounting Principle — retrospective restatement
Disclosed in notes only
1-21.e: Changing depreciation method = change in Accounting Principle. Requires Retrospective Restatement of all prior periods. Cumulative effect adjusts beginning retained earnings of earliest period presented.

1-21.q: Under IFRS (Ias 36), impairment test compares carrying amount to:
o: Undiscounted future cash flows
Recoverable amount = Higher of FV less disposal costs Or value in use
Replacement cost
Nrv only
1-21.e: IFRS: One-Step test — compare carrying amount to Recoverable Amount (higher of FV less disposal costs Or value in use). US GAAP: Two-Step test (recoverability then fair value).

1-21.q: Under IFRS, impairment losses on long-lived assets (not goodwill) Can be reversed if:
o: Never — IFRS prohibits reversals
Recoverable amount subsequently increases due to a change in estimates
Asset is revalued upward
Depreciation method changes
1-21.e: IFRS Allows reversal of impairment losses (except goodwill) when recoverable amount increases due to changed estimates. Capped at what carrying amount would have been without impairment. US GAAP does Not allow reversals.

1-21.q: When an asset is classified as Held For Sale (ASC 360), depreciation:
o: Continues at normal rate
Is accelerated
Ceases
Is reduced by 50%
1-21.e: Held-for-sale assets: depreciation Stops. Measured at lower of carrying amount or fair value less costs to sell. The asset will be sold, not consumed in operations.

1-21.q: Under IFRS, Value In Use for impairment testing is:
o: Current market value
Nrv minus selling costs
Present value of estimated future cash flows from use and disposal
Replacement cost
1-21.e: Value in use = PV of future cash inflows and outflows from continued use and ultimate disposal, discounted at a pre-tax rate reflecting time value and asset-specific risks.

1-21.q: Depletion of natural resources uses a method similar to:
o: Straight-line
Sum-of-years-digits
Units of production — based on actual extraction
Double declining balance
1-21.e: Depletion = Units Of Production: depletion per unit = (Cost − Residual Value) ÷ Estimated Total Units. Each period: units extracted × rate. Matches cost to actual resource consumed.

1-21.q: An impairment loss on a long-lived asset used in operations is reported in:
o: Other comprehensive income
Continuing operations on the income statement
Directly to retained earnings
Financing activities on Scf
1-21.e: Impairment losses on long-lived assets held and used are recognized in Income From Continuing Operations. They are Not in OCI.

1-21.q: Composite depreciation groups assets by:
o: Similar useful lives only
Similar asset Types, even if lives differ slightly
Individual asset — one rate per asset
Production output units
1-21.e: Composite depreciation groups Similar Asset Types (e.g., all furniture) with a weighted-average rate. Group depreciation groups assets with similar useful lives. Both simplify recordkeeping.

1-21.q: Half-year convention for GAAP depreciation: regardless of purchase month, Year 1 depreciation =
o: Full year
Three-quarters
Half of annual depreciation
One-quarter
1-21.e: Half-year convention treats all assets as acquired mid-year, recognizing Half a year in both Year 1 and the final year of the asset life.

1-21.q: Straight-Line: Cost=$120,000, Salvage=$20,000, Life=5 years. Nbv at end of Year 3=

1-22.q: Under US GAAP, Research & Development (R&D) costs are:
o: Always capitalized as intangible assets
Expensed as incurred — no capitalization allowed
Capitalized only if the project succeeds
Deferred until commercial production begins
1-22.e: Under GAAP (ASC 730), All R&D costs are Expensed as incurred — no capitalization is allowed except in specific situations (software development after technological feasibility, in-process R&D in a business combination). This is a key difference from IFRS.

1-22.q: Goodwill arising from a business combination is:
o: Amortized over 40 years
Amortized over its estimated useful life
Not amortized — tested for impairment Annually
Expensed immediately at acquisition
1-22.e: Goodwill has an Indefinite life and is Not amortized (public companies). Instead, it is tested for Impairment at least annually. Private companies may elect to amortize goodwill over 10 years (simplified method under ASU 2014-02).

1-22.q: A patent with a legal life of 20 years and estimated economic useful life of 8 years is amortized over:
o: 20 years
8 years
14 years (average)
Not amortized — indefinite life
1-22.e: A limited-life intangible is amortized over its Useful Life, which is the Shorter of its legal life and economic life. Patent: shorter of 20 years (legal) and 8 years (economic) = 8 years.

1-22.q: Internally developed trademarks and brand names are:
o: Capitalized at the amount spent developing them
Recognized at fair value annually
Not capitalized under GAAP — internally generated intangibles generally expensed
Amortized over 20 years
1-22.e: Internally Generated intangibles (brand names, mastheads, customer lists, internally developed goodwill) are Not recognized as assets under GAAP — the costs are expensed. Only Purchased intangibles and those acquired in business combinations are capitalized.

1-22.q: Under IFRS, development phase costs are:
o: Always expensed — same as GAAP
Capitalized when criteria for feasibility are met (technical feasibility, intent to complete, ability to sell/use, future economic benefits)
Capitalized only if government approved
Treated identically to research costs
1-22.e: IFRS: research phase = expensed. Development phase = Capitalized when specific criteria are met (ASC vs IFRS key difference). GAAP expenses all R&D. Under IFRS, once technical feasibility is established and other criteria met, development costs must be capitalized.

1-22.q: Goodwill impairment under ASU 2017-04 is measured as:
o: The excess of book value of the reporting unit over its fair value
The excess of goodwill over its replacement cost
Calculated only using a separate test for identifiable assets
Reversed in subsequent periods if value recovers
1-22.e: ASU 2017-04 (simplified goodwill impairment test): Impairment loss = carrying value of the reporting unit (including goodwill) Minus its fair value. If carrying value > FV → impairment loss = the difference (limited to carrying amount of goodwill). No reversal permitted.

1-22.q: An intangible with Indefinite useful life is:
o: Amortized over 40 years
Amortized over its legal life
Not amortized — tested for impairment annually
Amortized over 10 years
1-22.e: Indefinite-lived intangibles (goodwill, certain trademarks) are Not amortized. Tested for Impairment at least annually. Finite-lived intangibles are amortized over useful life.

1-22.q: Computer software for External Sale — costs after Technological Feasibility is established are:
o: Expensed as R&D
Capitalized and amortized
Same as internal-use software
Written off at product launch
1-22.e: Fas 86 (ASC 985-20): before technological feasibility = Expense. After feasibility until release = Capitalize. Amortize using the greater of straight-line or ratio of current to total expected revenue.

1-22.q: A Franchise Right with a 10-year contract life is:
o: Not amortized
Amortized over 10 years
Capitalized indefinitely
Expensed at purchase
1-22.e: Franchise right with specific contract period = finite-lived intangible. Amortize over the lesser of useful life or contract period (10 years). If indefinitely renewable at minimal cost, may be indefinite-lived.

1-22.q: A customer list acquired in a business combination is:
o: Expensed — customers are not owned
Capitalized as finite-lived intangible and amortized
Added to goodwill
Indefinite-lived
1-22.e: Acquired customer lists = separately identifiable intangibles under ASC 805. Capitalized at fair value as Finite-Lived and amortized over estimated useful life (expected customer retention period).

1-22.q: A copyright with 70-year legal life and 10-year economic life is amortized over:
o: 70 years
10 years
40 years
Longer of legal or economic life
1-22.e: Amortize over Useful Life = shorter of legal or economic life. Economic useful life (10 years) is shorter — amortize over 10 years.

1-22.q: An indefinite-lived intangible (other than goodwill) is impaired when:
o: Carrying value exceeds replacement cost
Carrying value exceeds Fair Value
Held for more than 10 years
Annual testing shows any decline
1-22.e: Compare Carrying Value to Fair Value directly. If Cv > FV → impairment loss = Cv − FV. Similar to simplified goodwill test.

1-22.q: Under US GAAP, Internally Generated customer lists and brand names are:
o: Capitalized at estimated fair value
Capitalized only if legally protected
Not recognized — expensed as incurred
Capitalized and impairment-tested annually
1-22.e: Internally generated intangibles Cannot be recognized under US GAAP or IFRS. Only Purchased or acquired (in a business combination) intangibles can be capitalized.

1-22.q: Under IFRS (Ias 38), Research phase costs are:
o: Capitalized if future benefits are probable
Expensed as incurred — same as US GAAP
Capitalized and impairment-tested
Capitalized if successful
1-22.e: IFRS: Research = Expense. Development = Capitalize when technical feasibility, intent, ability, and probable future benefits are demonstrated. US GAAP expenses All R&D.

1-22.q: Organization (start-up) costs under US GAAP are:
o: Capitalized and amortized over 5 years
Capitalized indefinitely
Expensed as incurred (ASC 720-15)
Charged to Apic
1-22.e: ASC 720-15: start-up and organization costs are Expensed As Incurred. No future benefit can be reliably measured.

1-22.q: Impairment of a Finite-Lived intangible: Step 1 tests whether:
o: Carrying value exceeds fair value
Undiscounted future cash flows Exceed carrying value (recoverability test)
Carrying value exceeds replacement cost
Amortized cost exceeds market value
1-22.e: Step 1 (recoverability): if undiscounted future cash flows Exceed carrying value → no impairment. If undiscounted cash flows < Cv → Step 2: loss = Cv − Fair Value.

1-22.q: A 3-year Non-Compete Agreement acquired in a business combination is:
o: Expensed immediately
Capitalized and amortized over 3 years
Added to goodwill
Not recognized
1-22.e: Non-compete agreements = separately identifiable contractual intangibles. Capitalized at fair value and amortized over the agreement term (3 years).

1-22.q: Advertising and promotional costs under US GAAP are:
o: Capitalized as brand equity
Capitalized when campaign effectiveness is proven
Expensed when incurred or when advertising first takes place
Deferred and amortized
1-22.e: Advertising costs are Expensed when incurred or when the advertising first runs (ASC 720-35). Brand-building campaigns cannot be capitalized.

1-22.q: The amortization method for a finite-lived intangible should:
o: Always be straight-line
Reflect the Pattern Of Consumption of economic benefits (straight-line if unknown)
Always be double-declining balance
Always be units-of-production
1-22.e: Method = best reflects consumption pattern of economic benefits. Straight-Line is default when pattern is unknown. Ias 38 is identical.

1-22.q: A 5-year license to use patented technology costs $100,000. Annual amortization =
o: Not amortized
$100,000 immediately
1-22.e: Finite-lived license with 5-year term: $100,000 ÷ 5 = $20,000/year straight-line.

1-23.q: Long-term debt is reclassified as current liability when:
o: Management decides to pay it early
It matures within One Year of the Balance Sheet date
Interest payments are past due
The credit rating declines
1-23.e: Long-term debt becomes a Current liability when it is Due Within One Year of the Balance Sheet date. The principal amount maturing within 12 months must be shown as current to properly reflect the company's near-term cash obligations.

1-23.q: A debt covenant is violated before year-end. The lender has not waived the acceleration clause. This debt should be classified as:
o: Non-current — covenant violations are normal
Current — the lender could demand immediate repayment
Only disclosed in notes, not reclassified
Current only if the lender actually demands payment
1-23.e: When a Covenant is violated and the lender can demand immediate repayment, the debt should be classified as Current. The acceleration risk means the debt could require cash within 12 months. Classification follows economic substance.

1-23.q: Under US GAAP, if long-term debt matures in 6 months but the company completes a refinancing agreement (3-year term) One Month After the BS date:
o: Can be classified as long-term because refinancing occurred
Must remain current — refinancing was Not completed before BS date
Can be long-term if disclosed in notes
Is classified based on management intent
1-23.e: Under GAAP: refinancing must be Completed (not just agreed upon) Before the BS date to classify debt as long-term. If refinancing occurs after the BS date — even before FS issuance — the debt remains Current under GAAP (IFRS differs here).

1-23.q: A lender grants a covenant waiver for 18 months from the Balance Sheet date. The violated long-term debt should be classified as:
o: Current — violations are always current
Non-Current — waiver covers more than 12 months
Current — waivers don't change classification
Non-current only if management intends to refinance
1-23.e: If the lender grants a Waiver covering at least 12 months from the BS date (here 18 months > 12 months), the acceleration risk is eliminated for that period. The debt can remain Non-Current because the lender has given up the right to demand payment for the waiver period.

1-23.q: Under IFRS, if a company refinances violating long-term debt Before the FS are authorized for issue (but after BS date), the debt:
o: Must always be current
May be classified as non-current since refinancing completed before FS authorized
Is always non-current
Must be disclosed but not reclassified
1-23.e: IFRS allows the refinancing to be considered if completed before the FS Are Authorized For Issue (even after BS date). This is a Key difference from GAAP, which only considers refinancing completed Before the BS date itself.

1-23.q: A Demand Note (callable by lender at any time) is classified as:
o: Long-term
Current — lender can demand payment within the year
Long-term if company does not expect demand
Non-current if secured by long-term assets
1-23.e: Demand notes callable At Lender'S Discretion = Current liabilities. The lender could legally demand full repayment anytime. Intent does not override the contractual right.

1-23.q: Reclassifying $500,000 of long-term debt to current due to covenant violation affects working capital by:
o: Decreasing working capital by $500,000
Increasing working capital
No effect
Decreasing current assets by $500,000
1-23.e: Working capital = Current Assets − Current Liabilities. Moving $500,000 to Current Liabilities increases current liabilities → working capital Decreases by $500,000.

1-23.q: Under US GAAP, short-term debt Can be classified as long-term if, before the FS are Issued, the company:
o: Signs a letter of intent
Obtains a Firm refinancing commitment (or actually refinances long-term)
Has board approval
Discloses intent in notes
1-23.e: ASC 470-10: must Complete refinancing or have Firm Commitment before FS are Issued (not just authorized). Intent alone is insufficient.

1-23.q: The Key difference between IFRS and US GAAP on covenant-violated debt is:
o: They are identical
Under IFRS, waiver before FS are Authorized For Issue (not just issued) keeps debt long-term
Under GAAP, waiver must be before FS authorization
IFRS always requires current classification
1-23.e: IFRS: waiver before FS Authorized For Issue = long-term. US GAAP: waiver before FS Issued. These dates can differ — IFRS allows a slightly longer window.

1-23.q: Current Portion Of Long-Term Debt =
o: 12 months of scheduled payments
Principal due within one year from BS date (or operating cycle if longer)
Callable portion
Annual interest payment
1-23.e: Current portion = debt principal due within One Year from the balance sheet date. Reclassified from long-term to current regardless of original issuance date.

1-23.q: A Subjective Acceleration Clause triggers when:
o: Borrower misses a payment
There is a Material Adverse Change in borrower financial condition
Lender needs liquidity
Market rates change
1-23.e: Subjective acceleration clauses use vague 'material adverse change' triggers. If acceleration probability is remote → debt may remain long-term. If reasonably possible or probable → reclassify to current.

1-23.q: For a waiver to keep covenant-violated debt as long-term, the waiver must cover:
o: At least 30 days
At least 6 months
More Than 12 Months from the balance sheet date
Indefinitely
1-23.e: Waiver must defer acceleration for More Than 12 Months from the BS date to maintain long-term classification.

1-23.q: A company violates a covenant September 30 (year-end). Lender grants waiver October 15 (before FS issued). Under US GAAP:
o: Current — violation existed at BS date
Long-term — waiver obtained before FS issued
Long-term only if waiver was before year-end
Current — waivers must be before year-end
1-23.e: US GAAP: waiver (for 12+ months) obtained before FS Issued = long-term, even if after the balance sheet date.

1-23.q: A $1,000,000 note maturing in 3 years has $200,000 due in next 12 months. Balance sheet shows:
o: $1,000,000 current
$200,000 current, $800,000 long-term
$1,000,000 long-term
All current — single note
1-23.e: $200,000 = Current Portion of long-term debt (due within 12 months). $800,000 = Long-Term. Split even for a single note.

1-23.q: Bridge Financing is classified as:
o: Long-term immediately — intended to be refinanced
Current unless a firm long-term commitment exists before FS issuance
Long-term with board approval
Current only if expiring within 30 days
1-23.e: Bridge financing = current until a Firm Commitment for permanent long-term financing exists (before FS issuance). Intent alone → current.

1-23.q: Minimum disclosure when short-term debt is excluded from current liabilities includes:
o: No disclosure needed
Description, terms of new arrangement, and amount excluded
Amount only
Auditor confirmation
1-23.e: Must disclose: (1) Description of refinancing, (2) Terms of new arrangement, (3) Amount excluded from current liabilities.

1-23.q: Under IFRS (Ias 1), covenant-violated long-term debt is classified as current Unless:
o: Company intends to cure
Lender grants waiver — at any time
Waiver granted Before FS authorized for issue (for 12+ months)
Violation is technical/immaterial
1-23.e: IFRS: must obtain waiver Before FS Authorized For Issue (for 12+ months post-BS). Under GAAP, cutoff is before FS Issued — potentially a later date.

1-23.q: Long-term debt due On Demand must be classified as:
o: Long-term if management expects to refinance
Current unless creditor has Written Waiver of demand right for 12+ months
Long-term if secured by fixed assets
Current only if demand has been made
1-23.e: Due-on-demand debt = Current unless written Waiver confirms demand right won't be exercised for more than 12 months from BS date.

1-23.q: A Revolving Credit Facility expiring in 14 months is classified as:
o: Current — expires within normal operating cycle
Long-Term — committed facility extends beyond 12 months
Current or long-term by management intent
Long-term only if currently drawn
1-23.e: Revolving credit with expiration beyond 12 months from BS date = Long-Term. The committed availability is long-term regardless of current borrowings.

1-23.q: Company intends to refinance short-term note but has No financing commitment. Classification:
o: Long-term — stated intent
Current — intent alone insufficient under GAAP
Long-term if disclosed in footnotes
Long-term if probable
1-23.e: Intent Alone is Insufficient. A Firm Commitment must exist before FS are issued. Without it — Current.

1-24.q: An Assurance-Type warranty is accounted for by:
o: Deferring all warranty revenue until claims are paid
Accruing a warranty expense and liability at the time of sale
Recording warranty costs only when claims are made
Treating it as a separate performance obligation
1-24.e: Assurance warranty: accrue Warranty Expense and Warranty Liability at the time of Sale, based on estimated future claims. This follows the Matching Principle — the expense is recognized in the same period as the related revenue.

1-24.q: A service-type warranty (extended warranty sold separately) is treated as:
o: An expense at time of sale — same as assurance warranty
A Separate Performance Obligation — revenue deferred and recognized over warranty period
Recognized entirely when the warranty is sold
A contingent liability
1-24.e: Service-Type warranty: treated as a Separate Performance Obligation under ASC 606. The transaction price is Allocated to the warranty. Revenue for the warranty is Deferred and recognized over the Warranty Period as the service is delivered.

1-24.q: Products sold: 500 units. Estimated 3% require warranty service at average cost $50. Warranty expense for the period =
1-24.e: Warranty expense = 500 × 3% × $50 = 500 × 0.03 × $50 = 15 × $50 = $750. This amount is both the warranty expense (IS) and the warranty liability (BS) accrued at time of sale.

1-24.q: When a Warranty Claim is honored (parts and labor provided), the journal entry is:
o: Dr. Warranty Expense / Cr. Cash
Dr. Warranty Liability / Cr. Cash or Inventory (parts used)
Dr. Cash / Cr. Warranty Revenue
Dr. Retained Earnings / Cr. Warranty Liability
1-24.e: When a claim is honored: Debit Warranty Liability (reducing the previously established liability) and Credit Cash or Inventory/Wages Payable (for parts and labor used). No new expense is recorded — it was expensed when the sale was made.

1-24.q: The Key distinction between assurance-type and service-type warranty is whether:
o: The warranty period exceeds one year
The warranty covers manufacturing defects vs. customer damage
The customer can Buy The Warranty Separately — indicating it provides additional value beyond product assurance
The warranty cost is material
1-24.e: Key question: Can the customer Buy the warranty separately or is it always bundled? If separate purchase is possible, it provides Value Beyond basic assurance → service-type → separate performance obligation. If always bundled and just assures basic functionality → assurance-type.

1-24.q: Beginning warranty liability $30,000. New accrual $40,000. Claims paid $35,000. Ending warranty liability =
Extended warranty sold separately for $200 covering 2 years. Year 1 revenue recognized =
o: $100 — straight-line over 2 years
$0 until claims made
$200 minus claims
1-24.e: Service-type warranty = separate performance obligation. Recognized Over Time — straight-line. Year 1 = $200 ÷ 2 = $100.

1-24.q: A product comes with a standard 1-year assurance warranty. An Optional 2-year extended warranty is sold for an extra fee. The extended warranty is:
o: Combined with assurance warranty
A Separate performance obligation — deferred over 2 years
Immediately expensed
Contingent liability only
1-24.e: Optional extended warranty at extra cost = Separate Performance Obligation under ASC 606. Allocate and defer; recognize over 2-year service period.

1-24.q: If estimated warranty costs Increase after initial accrual, the company should:
o: Restate prior periods
Record additional warranty expense Currently
Reduce liability and record gain
Disclose only
1-24.e: Warranty estimate changes = changes in Accounting Estimates — prospective. Record additional expense in current period. No restatement.

1-24.q: The journal entry to Accrue warranty expense is:
o: Dr Warranty Expense, Cr Cash
Dr Warranty Liability, Cr Warranty Expense
Dr Warranty Expense, Cr Warranty Liability
Dr Retained Earnings, Cr Warranty Liability
1-24.e: Accrual: Dr Warranty Expense, Cr Warranty Liability. When claims Paid: Dr Warranty Liability, Cr Cash/Inventory.

1-24.q: 500 units sold at $40 warranty repair cost each, 4% failure rate. Warranty expense =
Warranty Liability on the balance sheet is split into:
o: Long-term only
Current only
Current (next 12 months) and long-term (beyond 12 months)
Off-balance-sheet — notes only
1-24.e: Split: expected claims within 12 months = Current; beyond 12 months = Long-Term.

1-24.q: A Product Recall differs from a warranty claim because:
o: Recalls are never accrued
Recall is accrued when Probable and Estimable — often before individual claims
Warranties are always larger
Recalls are only expensed when paid
1-24.e: Product recalls = contingent liability (ASC 450). Accrue when Probable and Reasonably Estimable — often before specific claims are filed.

1-24.q: Under ASC 606, the key question for classifying a bundled warranty is whether:
o: Warranty period exceeds 12 months
Customer can purchase it separately
Warranty Provides Additional Service beyond assurance
Historical claim data exists
1-24.e: If warranty provides Additional Service beyond assuring product meets specifications → service-type (separate Po). If only assures compliance → assurance-type → ASC 460 accrual.

1-24.q: Service-type warranty revenue is recognized:
o: At point of sale
Over The Coverage Period (typically straight-line)
Only when claims are filed
When warranty expires
1-24.e: Service-type warranty revenue recognized Over Time — straight-line over coverage period unless another pattern better reflects service delivery.

1-24.q: Assurance-type warranty in a product sale: All revenue is recognized at delivery because:
o: Warranty is immaterial
Warranty is Not a separate performance obligation — accounted for under ASC 460
Customer chose not to pay separately
All warranties are point-in-time
1-24.e: Assurance-type warranties are Not separate POs under ASC 606. All revenue recognized at delivery. Warranty obligation then accrued as expense under ASC 460.

1-24.q: When a third party provides warranty coverage, the manufacturer:
o: Has no obligation — insurer takes all risk
May Still have net warranty obligation if it is the primary obligor
Records insurance premium as warranty expense
Records a warranty asset
1-24.e: If manufacturer is the Primary Obligor to customers, it still records the warranty liability. Substance of the arrangement determines accounting.

1-24.q: Warranty expense on the income statement is typically presented as:
o: Revenue deduction
Financing cost
Operating Expense (COGS or selling expense)
Extraordinary item
1-24.e: Warranty expense = Operating Expense — typically in COGS (manufacturers) or Selling Expense. It is a cost of the obligation created at the time of sale.

1-24.q: Changes in warranty Estimates affect:
o: Prior period retained earnings
Current And Future Periods — prospective treatment
Both current and prior periods proportionally
Only future periods — never current
1-24.e: Estimate changes = prospective. Adjust Current and Future periods for the revised expectation. No restatement of prior periods.

1-24.q: Warranty claims Paid are recorded as:
o: Dr Warranty Expense, Cr Cash
Dr Warranty Liability, Cr Cash (or Parts Inventory)
Dr Apic, Cr Cash
Dr Retained Earnings, Cr Cash
1-24.e: When claims are honored: Dr Warranty Liability (reduces the balance), Cr Cash (or Inventory/AP for parts). This settles the previously accrued obligation.

1-25.q: A Deferred Tax Liability arises when:
o: Taxable income exceeds book income (extra tax paid now)
Book income exceeds taxable income — More taxes will be paid in Future periods
A permanent difference exists
The company has operating losses
1-25.e: DTL arises when Book Income > Taxable Income in the current period — taxes are being Deferred to future periods. Example: accelerated depreciation deducts more in early years for tax → lower taxable income now → DTL because tax will be higher later when book depreciation catches up.

1-25.q: Warranty expense accrued for book purposes creates a:
o: Deferred Tax Liability
Permanent difference
Deferred Tax Asset — warranty deducted for tax only when Paid (future)
Valuation allowance
1-25.e: Warranty expense is Accrued for book at time of sale but deducted for Tax only when Paid. This means taxable income > book income currently (book gets the deduction earlier). The company paid More tax now than its book income requires → DTA (prepaid taxes → future tax savings).

1-25.q: Tax-exempt interest income (e.g., municipal bond interest) creates:
o: A deferred tax liability
A deferred tax asset
A Permanent Difference — no deferred tax effect
A valuation allowance
1-25.e: Tax-exempt interest is Never taxable (permanent difference). Book income includes it; taxable income never will. Since it never reverses, there is No deferred tax effect — only a Permanent Difference that causes the effective tax rate to differ from the statutory rate.

1-25.q: A Valuation Allowance for a DTA is recorded when:
o: Any uncertainty exists about future income
It is More Likely Than Not (>50%) that the DTA will Not be fully realized
The company has a profitable year
The DTA exceeds $1 million
1-25.e: Valuation Allowance: required when it is More Likely Than Not (>50% probability) that some or all of the DTA will Not be realized (company won't have sufficient future taxable income). Recorded as a contra-asset, reducing DTA's net value.

1-25.q: Income Tax Expense on the Income Statement =
o: Taxes actually paid during the period
Current Year Tax Payable Only
Current Tax Payable + Change in DTL − Change in DTA
Net Income × Statutory Tax Rate always
1-25.e: Income Tax Expense = Current Tax Payable (taxes owed this year) + Increase in DTL (or − Decrease in DTL) − Increase in DTA (or + Decrease in DTA). This reflects both the current period obligation And changes in deferred tax positions.

1-25.q: An NOL (Net Operating Loss) Carryforward creates:
o: A deferred tax liability
A permanent difference
A Deferred Tax Asset — future tax savings when offset against future income
A reduction in current tax expense only
1-25.e: NOL Carryforward: when a company has a tax loss, it can carry it forward to offset future taxable income (indefinitely under post-Tcja rules). This represents future Tax Savings → Deferred Tax Asset. Subject to valuation allowance if future income is uncertain.

1-25.q: Deferred Tax Assets and Liabilities on the Balance Sheet are classified as:
o: Current, separated as current and non-current
All presented as non-current (after ASU 2015-17 under GAAP)
Operating items on the income statement
Contra-equity accounts
1-25.e: Under ASU 2015-17, All deferred tax assets and liabilities are classified as Non-Current on the Balance Sheet. There is no longer a current/non-current split for deferred taxes under GAAP. (IFRS also does not distinguish current vs non-current for deferred taxes.)

1-25.q: Which is a Temporary difference (creates deferred taxes)?
o: Tax-exempt municipal bond interest
Non-deductible penalties
Macrs depreciation faster than book depreciation
Meals & entertainment 50% disallowed
1-25.e: Depreciation timing difference = Temporary (reverses when fully depreciated on both books). Tax-exempt interest and non-deductible items = Permanent — never reverse.

1-25.q: Deferred taxes are measured using:
o: Tax rate when temporary difference originated
Enacted rate expected when the difference Reverses
Management estimated future rate
Average of current and future rates
1-25.e: ASC 740: use Enacted tax rate expected to apply when the temporary difference Reverses. New enacted rates → remeasure immediately.

1-25.q: New tax law reduces rate from 28% to 21%. Effect on existing Deferred Tax Liabilities:
o: DTLs increase
DTLs Decrease — less tax owed when difference reverses
No effect
DTLs eliminated
1-25.e: DTLs remeasured at New Enacted Rate. Lower rate → less future tax → DTL Decreases. The reduction = benefit recognized in current income tax expense.

1-25.q: Rent received in advance — taxed immediately, recognized over service period for GAAP — creates:
o: DTL
DTA — taxable income > book income now; tax paid early
Permanent difference
No deferred tax
1-25.e: Taxable income > Book income in Year 1 (tax taxes now; book defers). Company pays tax Now on future book revenue → DTA (prepaid tax benefit).

1-25.q: Warranty expense accrued for GAAP but deducted for tax Only when paid — creates:
o: DTL
DTA — tax deduction comes later
Permanent difference
No deferred tax
1-25.e: GAAP expense > tax deduction currently. The company will get a tax deduction Later when claims are paid → DTA (future tax savings).

1-25.q: NOL carryforward of $300,000 at 21% tax rate creates a DTA of:
o: Depends on valuation allowance
1-25.e: DTA = $300,000 × 21% = $63,000. Represents future tax benefit from offsetting taxable income. Valuation allowance may reduce but is separate from the gross DTA.

1-25.q: Valuation allowance is required when it is More Likely Than Not (>50%) that:
o: Company will report a loss
Some or All of the DTA will Not be realized
DTL exceeds DTA
Tax rate will increase
1-25.e: Valuation allowance needed when > 50% likely some/all DTA is unrealizable. Evidence: history of income, future projections, DTL reversals, tax planning strategies.

1-25.q: Intraperiod tax allocation means:
o: Allocating taxes between quarters
Allocating total tax among continuing ops, discontinued ops, OCI, and equity items in Same period
Splitting between federal and state
Allocating between current and deferred
1-25.e: Intraperiod: total tax expense allocated among components — continuing operations, discontinued operations, OCI items, equity adjustments. Each section reported Net Of Tax.

1-25.q: Effective tax rate differs from statutory rate due to:
o: Temporary differences only
Permanent differences and tax credits
Deferred tax remeasurement only
Accounting method choices
1-25.e: Effective rate = Tax Expense ÷ Pre-tax Income. Differs from statutory rate due to Permanent Differences and Tax Credits. Temporary differences affect Timing, not the total lifetime rate.

1-25.q: Under ASC 740-10, uncertain tax position recognized only when:
o: Tax return is filed
More Likely Than Not (>50%) the position will be sustained upon examination
IRS has accepted it
Position is disclosed in footnotes
1-25.e: Recognize tax benefit only when > 50% likely the position will be Sustained upon examination. Amount = largest benefit > 50% likely to be realized.

1-25.q: Income Tax Expense includes:
o: Only current taxes payable
Only change in deferred balances
Both current tax And deferred tax (expense or benefit)
Only enacted future taxes
1-25.e: Income Tax Expense = Current tax (payable now) + Deferred tax expense/benefit (change in deferred balances). Both on income statement; often one line, detailed in notes.

1-25.q: Pre-tax income $200,000, $10,000 tax-exempt interest, tax rate 25%. Tax expense =
1-25.e: Taxable income = $200,000 − $10,000 = $190,000. Current tax = $190,000 × 25% = $47,500. Permanent differences don't create deferred taxes — they only affect current tax.

1-25.q: Under ASU 2015-17, deferred tax assets and liabilities are classified as:
o: Current and non-current
All current
All Non-Current (net within same jurisdiction)
Current DTA, non-current DTL
1-25.e: ASU 2015-17: All deferred taxes = Non-Current. Same-jurisdiction DTAs and DTLs are Netted. Eliminates prior current/non-current split.

1-26.q: Under ASC 842, All leases (with limited exceptions) are now:
o: Kept off-balance-sheet for operating leases
Recognized On The Balance Sheet as a Right-of-Use (Rou) asset and lease liability
Only recognized if they transfer ownership
Expensed immediately when signed
1-26.e: ASC 842 (effective 2019) Requires all leases (operating and finance, with exceptions for short-term leases < 12 months) to be Recognized On The Balance Sheet as a Rou Asset and Lease Liability. This was the major change — previously operating leases were off-balance-sheet.

1-26.q: A Finance Lease results in which income statement presentation?
o: A single straight-line lease expense
Separate amortization expense And interest expense — total expense front-loaded
Lease expense recognized only when cash is paid
No income statement effect
1-26.e: Finance Lease: income statement shows Amortization of the Rou asset (usually straight-line) And Interest on the lease liability (higher in early periods). Total expense is Front-Loaded — higher in early years of the lease compared to a straight-line operating lease.

1-26.q: The Lease Liability is measured at:
o: Total undiscounted lease payments
Present Value of future lease payments
Historical cost of the leased asset
Fair value of the asset at commencement
1-26.e: Lease liability = Present Value of future lease payments, discounted at the rate implicit in the lease (or if not determinable, the lessee's incremental borrowing rate). Future payments are discounted to reflect the time value of money.

1-26.q: An operating lease requires how many expense lines on the income statement?
o: Three — rent expense, interest, amortization
Two — amortization and interest
One — a single lease expense recognized on a straight-line basis
None — operating leases are off-balance-sheet
1-26.e: Operating Lease (ASC 842): a Single Lease Expense recognized on a Straight-Line basis over the lease term. Even though there is a Rou asset and liability on the balance sheet (like finance lease), the income statement shows only One straight-line expense — a key difference from finance lease treatment.

1-26.q: The Right-of-Use (Rou) Asset is initially measured at approximately:
o: Historical cost of the asset
Gross lease payments over the term
Present Value of lease payments + initial direct costs + prepaid rent
Fair value of the leased asset
1-26.e: Rou Asset initially = Lease Liability (PV of payments) + initial direct costs + any prepaid lease payments + initial estimate of Aro. Essentially, it approximates the PV of the lease payments. As payments are made, both the Rou asset and lease liability decrease.

1-26.q: A lease is classified as a Finance lease if:
o: It is for less than 12 months
The lessee always intends to purchase the asset
It meets Any One of five specified criteria (e.g., ownership transfer, purchase option, 75% life, 90% FV, specialized asset)
The lessor chooses to classify it as finance
1-26.e: Finance lease: if the lease meets Any One of five criteria: (1) title transfers, (2) purchase option likely to be exercised, (3) lease term ≥ 75% of economic life, (4) PV of payments ≥ 90% of asset FV, (5) specialized asset with no alternative use.

1-26.q: Short-Term Lease Exemption under ASC 842 applies to leases with term of:
o: Less than 24 months
12 months or less, no purchase option reasonably certain to exercise
Less than 36 months
Any lease under $10,000
1-26.e: Short-term exemption: term ≤ 12 months And no purchase option the lessee is reasonably certain to exercise. Payments expensed straight-line. No Rou asset or lease liability recognized.

1-26.q: Under IFRS 16, Low-Value Asset exemption applies when:
o: Annual payment below $1,000
Asset's fair value when New is low (e.g., ~$5,000 threshold)
Lease term under 6 months
Lessee has a purchase option
1-26.e: IFRS 16 low-value exemption based on the Underlying Asset'S Value when new (not lease term). IASB used ~$5,000 in basis for conclusions. E.g., tablets, small furniture.

1-26.q: Lease Modification that grants Additional right of use at standalone price is treated as:
o: Remeasurement of existing lease
Separate New Lease
Error correction
Operating expense
1-26.e: Modification = separate new lease when: (1) additional right of use Not in original lease, And (2) price increase reflects standalone price.

1-26.q: Lease Incentives paid by lessor to lessee Reduce the lessee's:
o: Lease liability
Lease expense immediately
Rou Asset initial measurement
Retained earnings
1-26.e: Lease incentives received reduce the initial Rou Asset measurement. Lower Rou asset → lower amortization → lower expense over lease term.

1-26.q: Initial Direct Costs incurred by the lessee to obtain a lease are:
o: Expensed immediately
Added to initial Rou Asset measurement
Deducted from lease liability
Recorded as separate prepaid
1-26.e: Initial direct costs (commissions, fees to obtain lease) added to the Initial Measurement of the Rou Asset. Amortized as part of lease expense or amortization expense.

1-26.q: In Sale-Leaseback where transfer qualifies as a Sale, the seller-lessee:
o: Recognizes no gain or loss
Recognizes gain/loss on sale And records leaseback as new lease
Records financing liability for full proceeds
Continues to carry asset on balance sheet
1-26.e: Qualifying sale-leaseback: derecognize asset, recognize Gain or Loss on sale (related to rights transferred), account for Leaseback as separate operating or finance lease.

1-26.q: Key income statement difference between Operating and Finance Lease (lessee):
o: Finance leases cost more over lifetime
Operating: single lease expense; Finance: amortization + interest (front-loaded)
Operating has higher interest
Finance shows no depreciation
1-26.e: Operating = One Line (straight-line expense). Finance = Two Lines (amortization + interest — higher in early years). Finance lease expense is front-loaded.

1-26.q: Variable Lease Payments based on usage are:
o: Included in lease liability at commencement
Excluded from liability — expensed when incurred
Capitalized in Rou asset
Contingent liabilities
1-26.e: Usage-based variable payments cannot be reliably estimated → Excluded from lease liability. Expensed as Incurred when usage occurs.

1-26.q: Lease Term includes:
o: Non-cancellable period only
Non-cancellable + optional periods the lessee is Reasonably Certain to exercise
Full legal term regardless of options
Only periods with fixed payments
1-26.e: Lease term = non-cancellable period + optional renewal periods where lessee is Reasonably Certain to exercise. High threshold — similar to 'reasonably assured' under old GAAP.

1-26.q: For a Finance Lease, interest expense each period =
o: Fixed payment ÷ remaining term
Lease Liability Balance × discount rate (effective interest method)
Straight-line over lease term
Fixed payment × implicit rate
1-26.e: Finance lease interest = Carrying Value of lease liability × discount rate. Effective interest method — higher in early periods (larger balance), decreasing over time.

1-26.q: For the Lessor, an Operating Lease means:
o: Derecognize asset, record receivable
Keep asset on balance sheet, recognize rental income straight-line
Record finance receivable at PV
Recognize profit at commencement
1-26.e: Lessor operating lease: Keeps asset (continues depreciation), recognizes Rental Income straight-line over lease term. Asset returned at end.

1-26.q: A Sales-Type Lease (lessor) results in:
o: Rental income straight-line
Derecognize asset; record net investment; recognize selling profit at commencement + interest income over term
Keep asset at original cost
No income until payments received
1-26.e: Sales-type: derecognize asset, record net investment (PV of future payments), recognize Selling Profit at commencement and Interest Income over the lease term.

1-26.q: For Lessee operating lease, the Year 1 journal entry is:
o: Dr Rou amortization, Cr Rou Asset + Dr Interest, Cr Liability
Dr Lease Expense (single line), Cr Cash/Lease Liability (adjust Rou and liability)
Dr Depreciation, Cr Acc Depr + Dr Interest, Cr Cash
Dr Rou Asset, Cr Lease Liability
1-26.e: Operating lease: Single Lease Expense line (straight-line). Debit Lease Expense, credit Cash (and adjust Rou asset and liability per amortization tables). One income statement line.

1-26.q: Finance lease Rou asset amortization period: if ownership transfers Or purchase option reasonably certain —
o: Lease term only
Useful Life of the underlying asset
Shorter of useful life or lease term
Longer of useful life or lease term
1-26.e: If ownership transfers/purchase option certain → amortize over Useful Life (same as owned asset). If neither → shorter of useful life or lease term.

1-27.q: When a cash dividend is Declared, the journal entry is:
o: Dr. Cash / Cr. Retained Earnings
Dr. Retained Earnings / Cr. Dividends Payable
Dr. Dividends Payable / Cr. Cash
Dr. Retained Earnings / Cr. Cash
1-27.e: Declaration creates the liability: Dr. Retained Earnings (or Dividends Declared), Cr. Dividends Payable. The Payment date entry is: Dr. Dividends Payable, Cr. Cash. No journal entry is required on the Record Date.

1-27.q: A Small stock dividend (<25%) is recorded at:
o: Par value per share
Zero — only memo entry
Fair Value per share — transferred from Re to Apic
Cost of treasury shares
1-27.e: Small stock dividend: recorded at Fair Value of the shares issued. Dr. Retained Earnings (FV), Cr. Common Stock (par value × new shares), Cr. Apic (excess above par). Total equity is Unchanged — just a reclassification within equity.

1-27.q: Total shareholders' equity is affected by which of the following?
o: Declaring a stock dividend
Issuing stock for cash (Increases equity)
Paying a previously declared cash dividend (Div Payable → Cash)
Converting treasury stock between equity accounts
1-27.e: Issuing Stock for cash Increases total equity (cash in, equity account up). Stock dividends just reclassify within equity (no total change). Paying a declared dividend reduces cash and removes the payable (net effect = zero on total equity since Re was reduced at declaration). Treasury stock conversions are reclassifications.

1-27.q: A material accounting error from a prior period is corrected as a:
o: Current period expense on the income statement
Prior Period Adjustment to beginning Retained Earnings — comparative FS are restated
Extraordinary item
Change in accounting estimate
1-27.e: Prior Period Error Correction: adjusted against the Beginning Retained Earnings balance for the earliest period presented. Prior period FS are Restated. Not reported on the current income statement (would distort current results). Disclosures required.

1-27.q: Accumulated OCI (Aoci) in equity includes:
o: Net income from the current period
Dividends paid
Unrealized gains on AFS securities, Fx translation adjustments, pension adjustments
Retained earnings balance
1-27.e: Aoci accumulates OCI items that bypass the income statement: unrealized gains/losses on AFS securities, foreign currency Translation adjustments, pension liability adjustments, and effective portion of cash flow hedges. Aoci is a separate equity component.

1-27.q: Retained Earnings can be Reduced by all of the following Except:
o: Cash dividends declared
Net loss for the period
Prior period error correction (overstatement of prior income)
Issuing new common stock
1-27.e: Issuing new common stock Increases equity (increases Common Stock and Apic). Retained Earnings is reduced by: dividends declared, net losses, and prior period adjustments for overstatement of prior income.

1-27.q: A Large stock dividend (25%+) is recorded at:
o: Fair market value
Par Value per share
Book value per share
No value — no entry
1-27.e: Large stock dividends (≥25%) recorded at Par Value. Dr Retained Earnings (par × new shares), Cr Common Stock. Small dividends (<25%) recorded at Fair Market Value.

1-27.q: A Property Dividend is recorded at:
o: Book value of property
Fair Value at declaration date — with gain/loss on revaluation
Cost minus depreciation
Par value equivalent
1-27.e: Property remeasured to Fair Value first (gain/loss recognized), then dividend declared at fair value. Dr Retained Earnings (FV), Cr Dividends Payable. On payment: Dr Dividends Payable, Cr Asset.

1-27.q: A Liquidating Dividend returns:
o: Company stock to shareholders
Paid-In Capital (not earnings) to shareholders
Excess cash beyond retained earnings
More than net income for the year
1-27.e: Liquidating dividend returns Invested Capital — debited to Apic, not Retained Earnings. Signals return of capital rather than distribution of profits.

1-27.q: A stock dividend's effect on Total Stockholders' Equity is:
o: Increases total equity
Decreases total equity
No Effect — reclassification within equity (Re to Common Stock/Apic)
Decreases equity by par value of new shares
1-27.e: Stock dividend = Reclassification: Re decreases; Common Stock (and Apic for small dividends) increases. Total equity Unchanged. No assets leave the company.

1-27.q: Appropriated (restricted) Retained Earnings means:
o: Re invested in fixed assets
Portion restricted for specific purpose — Unavailable for dividends
Re reduced by prior period errors
Re belonging to Nci
1-27.e: Appropriated Re = voluntary/required restriction unavailable for distribution. Signals a portion is set aside (e.g., loan covenant, expansion). Total equity amount unchanged.

1-27.q: A Prior Period Error correction is reported as:
o: Current period income adjustment
Adjustment to Beginning Retained Earnings of earliest period (restatement)
OCI component
Notes only
1-27.e: Prior period errors (mathematical mistakes, GAAP misapplication) = Restatement. Restate prior periods and adjust Beginning Re of earliest period presented.

1-27.q: A voluntary Change In Accounting Principle is reported:
o: Prospectively
Cumulative-effect in current income
Retrospectively — restate all prior periods presented
As OCI item
1-27.e: Accounting principle change = Retrospective Application. Restate prior periods as if new principle always used. Adjust beginning Re of earliest period.

1-27.q: Comprehensive Income equals:
o: Net income only
Net income Plus Other Comprehensive Income (OCI)
Operating income plus OCI
Total revenues minus expenses
1-27.e: Comprehensive Income = Net Income + OCI. OCI includes: unrealized AFS gains/losses, pension adjustments, foreign currency translation, effective cash flow hedge amounts.

1-27.q: Statement of Shareholders' Equity shows changes in all Except:
o: Retained earnings
Common stock and Apic
Accumulated OCI
Cash Flows From Operations
1-27.e: Statement of Se shows changes in all Equity components. Cash flows belong to the Statement Of Cash Flows, not the equity statement.

1-27.q: Book Value Per Common Share =
o: Total assets ÷ total outstanding
(Total equity − Preferred liquidation value) ÷ Common Shares Outstanding
Net income ÷ common shares
Total equity ÷ authorized shares
1-27.e: Book value per common share = (Total equity − Preferred liquidation value) ÷ Common shares outstanding.

1-27.q: Return On Equity (ROE) =
o: Net income ÷ total assets
Net income ÷ Average Total Stockholders' Equity
Operating income ÷ common equity
Gross profit ÷ average equity
1-27.e: ROE = Net Income ÷ Average Total Se. Measures efficiency in using shareholders' capital to generate profit.

1-27.q: Dividends on Non-Cumulative preferred stock Not declared in a year:
o: Accumulate for future years
Lost permanently
Convert to common dividends
Accrued as liability
1-27.e: Non-cumulative: skipped dividends are Lost permanently. Do not accumulate or carry forward.

1-27.q: Accumulated OCI (Aoci) includes:
o: Net income for the year
Cash dividends declared
Unrealized AFS debt gains/losses, pension adjustments, foreign currency translation
Treasury stock purchases
1-27.e: Aoci = OCI items that Bypass the income statement: unrealized AFS debt securities, pension liability adjustments, foreign currency translation adjustments, effective cash flow hedge amounts.

1-27.q: Total equity $500,000. Preferred liquidation value $100,000. Common shares outstanding 40,000. Book value per common share =

1-28.q: A company issues 1,000 shares of $1 par value stock for $15 per share. The journal entry Credit includes:
o: Common Stock $15,000
Common Stock $1,000 And Apic $14,000
Common Stock $1,000 only
Apic $15,000 only
1-28.e: Issue stock: Dr. Cash $15,000 / Cr. Common Stock $1,000 (1,000 shares × $1 par) / Cr. Apic $14,000 (excess: 1,000 × $14). Common Stock = par value only. Apic = amount Above par.

1-28.q: A 2-for-1 Stock Split: the effect on par value per share is:
o: Par value Doubles
Par value is Halved
Par value is unchanged
Par value is eliminated
1-28.e: 2-for-1 stock split: shares double → to keep total par value constant, par value per share is Halved. No journal entry is required — just a memo entry noting the new share count and par value. Total equity is completely unchanged.

1-28.q: 'Outstanding shares' equals:
o: Authorized shares
Issued shares
Issued shares Minus Treasury shares
Authorized minus unissued
1-28.e: Outstanding shares = Issued minus Treasury shares. Treasury shares are issued but repurchased and held by the company — they are Not outstanding (not held by external investors). EPS uses weighted average Outstanding shares.

1-28.q: Stock issued for non-cash consideration (e.g., services, equipment) is recorded at:
o: Par value of the stock
Book value of the asset received
Fair Value of consideration received, or FV of stock if more reliable
Historical cost of similar assets
1-28.e: Stock issued for non-cash consideration: record at the Fair Value of the Consideration Received (the asset/service), or if not reliably determinable, the Fair Value Of The Stock Issued. This ensures the exchange is recorded at economic value.

1-28.q: The difference between a stock Split and a stock Dividend is that a stock split:
o: Reduces retained earnings
Requires a formal journal entry
Has No journal entry — memo only; par value per share changes
Always has the same accounting treatment
1-28.e: Stock Split: No journal entry (only a memo to reflect new share count and adjusted par value). Par value per share Decreases. Stock Dividend: requires a formal journal entry (Dr. Re, Cr. Common Stock/Apic). Par value per share is Unchanged in stock dividend.

1-28.q: A 1-for-2 Reverse Stock Split: par value per share
o: Halves
Doubles
Unchanged
Eliminated
1-28.e: Reverse split halves shares → par value per share Doubles. Total par value in Common Stock account is Unchanged.

1-28.q: No-Par Value stock issued for $20/share. The full $20 is credited to:
o: Apic only
Common Stock account — full amount
Retained Earnings
Split equally
1-28.e: No-par stock: Entire proceeds → Common Stock account. No Apic separation since there is no par value.

1-28.q: Preemptive Rights give existing shareholders the right to:
o: Vote on major transactions
Purchase New Shares proportionally before public — maintain ownership %
Receive dividends first
Demand share repurchase
1-28.e: Preemptive rights: first right to buy new shares proportional to existing holdings. Protects ownership % from dilution.

1-28.q: Authorized shares =
o: Currently held by public
Issued and outstanding
Maximum approved by State Charter
Issued minus treasury
1-28.e: Authorized = maximum the company may issue, per the corporate Charter. Can be changed by shareholder vote. Issued ≤ Outstanding ≤ Authorized.

1-28.q: Stock Issuance Costs (underwriting, legal fees) are recorded as:
o: Deferred asset amortized over 5 years
Income statement expense
Deduction From Proceeds — reduces Apic
Charged to retained earnings
1-28.e: Stock issuance costs reduce Net Proceeds — deducted from Apic. Not expensed. These are costs of raising equity.

1-28.q: Contributed Capital (paid-in capital) consists of:
o: Retained earnings + par value
Common Stock + Apic + Preferred Stock contributions
Retained earnings only
Total equity minus treasury stock
1-28.e: Contributed capital = all shareholder contributions: Common Stock (par), Apic, Preferred Stock. Excludes retained earnings (earned capital) and treasury stock.

1-28.q: Legal Capital =
o: Amount available as dividends
Par Value of all issued shares (or stated value) — protects creditors
Total stockholders' equity
Liquid assets for legal obligations
1-28.e: Legal capital = Par Value of issued shares. Minimum that must remain; cannot be distributed. Protects creditors.

1-28.q: Under ASC 718, Employee Stock Options are expensed at:
o: Exercise price minus market price
Fair Value at Grant Date, over the vesting period
Fair value at exercise date
No expense — equity instrument
1-28.e: ASC 718: compensation = Fair Value at Grant Date (Black-Scholes model). Recognized over Vesting Period. At exercise: receive cash + issue shares — no additional expense.

1-28.q: Stock Rights (warrants) allow holder to:
o: Vote on corporate matters
Purchase Shares at specified exercise price during defined period
Receive guaranteed dividends
Force share repurchase
1-28.e: Warrants = options to Buy shares at exercise price during defined period. When issued with bonds: allocate proceeds by relative fair values.

1-28.q: Convertible Preferred To Common: journal entry
o: Records gain or loss
Dr Preferred Stock + Apic-Preferred, Cr Common Stock + Apic-Common (No Gain/Loss)
Requires cash payment
Reduces retained earnings
1-28.e: Conversion: Dr Preferred Stock + Apic-Preferred (carrying value), Cr Common Stock (par) + Apic-Common (plug). No Gain/Loss — reclassification within equity.

1-28.q: A Rights Offering allows existing shareholders to buy additional shares at:
o: Market price
Below Market (subscription price), proportional to holdings
Above market
Any negotiated price
1-28.e: Rights offering: existing holders buy at Subscription Price (below market) proportional to holdings. Maintains ownership % at a discount.

1-28.q: Stock Subscription Receivables under US GAAP are classified as:
o: Current assets
Long-term assets
Contra-Equity deduction
Revenue from issuance
1-28.e: Subscriptions receivable = Contra-Equity (not an asset). Shareholder pledge doesn't meet asset definition with certainty.

1-28.q: 3-for-1 stock split. Before: 100,000 shares, $3 par. After: par value per share =
1-28.e: Shares triple to 300,000; par per share = $3 ÷ 3 = $1.00. Total par = 300,000 × $1.00 = $300,000 — unchanged.

1-28.q: 2-for-1 stock split is recorded as:
o: Dr Re, Cr Common Stock
Memorandum Entry — reduce par per share, double shares (no dollar changes)
Dr Common Stock, Cr Apic
Dr Apic, Cr Common Stock
1-28.e: Stock Split = Memorandum Entry. No dollar amounts change. Contrast: stock Dividends require Dr Retained Earnings.

1-28.q: Treasury shares are:
o: Outstanding — can vote and receive dividends
Issued But Not Outstanding — no voting, no dividends
Authorized but unissued
Retired and removed from issued count
1-28.e: Treasury shares = Issued but Not Outstanding. No voting, no dividends, excluded from EPS denominator. Outstanding = Issued minus Treasury.

1-29.q: Cumulative preferred stock means:
o: Preferred shareholders get extra dividends above stated rate
Missed dividends Accumulate and must be paid before common dividends
Dividends grow each year
Preferred shareholders get voting rights
1-29.e: Cumulative: if a dividend is Not paid in a period, it Accumulates as 'dividends in arrears.' These arrears must be paid to preferred shareholders in full Before any dividends can be paid to common shareholders.

1-29.q: Dividends In Arrears on cumulative preferred stock are:
o: Recorded as a liability on the Balance Sheet
Not a liability until declared — but must be Disclosed in notes
Deducted from Retained Earnings immediately
Recognized as an expense on the income statement
1-29.e: Dividends in arrears are Not a liability — no declaration has been made. They are Not recorded on the balance sheet. However, they Must Be Disclosed in the notes to the financial statements, so users know the obligation before common dividends can be paid.

1-29.q: In liquidation, which is paid Last?
o: Secured creditors
Unsecured creditors
Preferred shareholders
Common shareholders
1-29.e: Liquidation priority: Secured creditors → Unsecured creditors → Preferred shareholders → Common Shareholders (last). Common shareholders bear the most risk and receive any residual value — which could be zero if prior claims consume all assets.

1-29.q: Mandatorily Redeemable preferred stock is classified as:
o: Equity — preferred stock
A Liability on the Balance Sheet — substance over form
Long-term investment
Other comprehensive income
1-29.e: Mandatorily redeemable preferred stock is classified as a Liability because the company is Obligated to redeem (buy back) it at a specified date or upon a specific event. Economic substance = debt obligation. This follows the Substance Over Form principle.

1-29.q: 1,000 shares of 8%, $50 par preferred stock. Dividends in arrears for 3 years. Total preferred dividends owed before common shareholders can receive anything =
1-29.e: Annual preferred dividend = 1,000 × $50 × 8% = $4,000. Dividends in arrears = 3 years × $4,000 = $12,000. Plus current year $4,000 = $16,000 total that preferred shareholders must receive before common gets anything.

1-29.q: Participating preferred: holders can receive:
o: Extra voting rights
Additional Dividends beyond stated rate after common gets base amount
Convert to common anytime
Demand redemption at par
1-29.e: Participating preferred can 'participate' in Extra dividends beyond stated rate, sharing excess dividends with common holders.

1-29.q: Convertible preferred: conversion is at:
o: Company's option only
Holder'S option at specified conversion ratio
Maturity only
Only when preferred dividend is in arrears
1-29.e: Convertible preferred: Holder has the right to convert at specified Conversion Ratio. Typically exercised when economically beneficial.

1-29.q: Callable preferred: the Company has the right to:
o: Force holder to convert
Redeem shares at specified call price after a set date
Vote on dividend amounts
Convert to debt
1-29.e: Callable: Issuer can Call (redeem) shares at specified Call Price (usually at or above par) after a certain date.

1-29.q: Non-Cumulative preferred: if dividends skipped Year 1, Year 2 requirement before common dividends:
o: Pay both Year 1 and Year 2
Pay Current Year (Year 2) only — Year 1 dividends lost
Pay common without any preferred
Pay 50% of skipped
1-29.e: Non-cumulative: skipped dividends Lost. Only current year preferred dividend must be paid before common.

1-29.q: Liquidation Preference means preferred holders receive:
o: Higher dividend rate
Their Liquidation Amount before common holders in bankruptcy/wind-down
Voting rights in liquidation
First access to operating assets
1-29.e: Liquidation priority: Creditors → Preferred (at liquidation preference: par + arrears) → Common (whatever remains, possibly nothing).

1-29.q: Preferred dividends are deducted in Basic EPS because:
o: They are tax-deductible
They reduce income Available To Common shareholders
They are fixed obligations
Preferred is classified as liability
1-29.e: Basic EPS = (Net Income − Preferred Dividends) ÷ Weighted Avg Common Shares. Deducted because EPS measures earnings attributable to Common shareholders.

1-29.q: $100 par, 7% cumulative preferred, 1,000 shares. Dividends skipped 2 years. Year 3 minimum preferred dividends =
1-29.e: Annual = 1,000 × $100 × 7% = $7,000. Arrears (2 years) + current = 3 × $7,000 = $21,000 before common receives anything.

1-29.q: Under IFRS, Mandatorily Redeemable preferred is classified as:
o: Permanent equity
Financial Liability — obligation to deliver cash
Mezzanine financing
Equity unless dividends are cumulative
1-29.e: IFRS (Ias 32) and US GAAP (ASC 480): mandatorily redeemable preferred = Liability. Dividends on such instruments = Interest Expense.

1-29.q: Preferred stock Issuance journal entry credits:
o: Retained earnings
Preferred Stock at par + Apic-Preferred at excess
Common Stock Apic
Bonds Payable if redeemable
1-29.e: Dr Cash, Cr Preferred Stock (par value × shares), Cr Apic-Preferred (excess). Mirrors common stock issuance.

1-29.q: Convertible preferred in Diluted EPS is Antidilutive when:
o: Preferred dividends exceed earnings
Including converted shares Increases (rather than decreases) diluted EPS
Preferred has been outstanding < 1 year
Conversion ratio is < 1:1
1-29.e: Antidilutive = inclusion would Increase diluted EPS (less negative effect). Exclude from calculation.

1-29.q: Participating preferred: 1,000 shares × $100 par, 6% base. Common: 5,000 shares × $10 par. Total dividends $20,000. After $6,000 base to preferred, remaining $14,000 shared per share among all 6,000 shares. Preferred Additional dividend =
1-29.e: Additional per share = $14,000 ÷ 6,000 = $2.333. Preferred additional = 1,000 × $2.333 = $2,333. Total preferred = $6,000 + $2,333 = $8,333.

1-29.q: Preferred redeemable at Holder'S option is classified under US GAAP as:
o: Permanent equity
Mezzanine Equity (temporary equity — between liabilities and permanent equity)
Financial liability
Common equity
1-29.e: US GAAP (Reg S-X): holder-redeemable preferred = Mezzanine Equity (outside permanent equity but not a liability). Under IFRS → may be classified as Liability.

1-29.q: Which preferred provides Most security to the holder?
o: Non-cumulative, non-participating
Cumulative, non-participating
Non-cumulative, participating
Cumulative, Participating
1-29.e: Cumulative: all missed dividends accumulate before common gets anything. Participating: shares in excess dividends. Combined = maximum protection.

1-29.q: Dividend priority order (from First to Last):
o: Common → Preferred → Creditors
Creditors → Common → Preferred
Creditors → Preferred (arrears + current) → Common
Preferred → Common → Creditors
1-29.e: Dividend/liquidation priority: (1) Creditors paid first, (2) Preferred stockholders (arrears then current), (3) Common stockholders receive whatever remains.

1-29.q: Beneficial Conversion Feature of convertible preferred is:
o: Conversion ratio advantage
Excess of common FV at issuance over effective conversion price — recognized as deemed dividend in EPS
Discount on preferred issue price
Premium company pays to call preferred
1-29.e: Bcf: when effective conversion price < FV of common at issuance → immediate economic benefit. Bcf = intrinsic value → Deemed Dividend → reduces income available to common in EPS numerator.

1-30.q: Treasury stock is classified on the Balance Sheet as:
o: An asset — the company owns its own shares
A Reduction from shareholders' equity (contra-equity)
A current liability
Other comprehensive income
1-30.e: Treasury stock is a Contra-Equity account — deducted from total shareholders' equity. It is Not an asset. A company cannot own itself. The repurchase reduces equity because cash left the company and shares were retired from circulation (though not formally cancelled).

1-30.q: When treasury stock is Reissued at a price Above its cost, the excess is:
o: Recognized as a gain in Net Income
Added to Retained Earnings
Credited to Apic-Treasury Stock
Credited to Common Stock
1-30.e: Reissue above cost: Dr. Cash (proceeds) / Cr. Treasury Stock (cost) / Cr. Apic-Treasury (excess). The Excess above cost goes to Apic — not income. There are No Gains or Losses on treasury stock transactions.

1-30.q: A company has: Authorized 500,000 / Issued 300,000 / Treasury 30,000. Outstanding shares =
1-30.e: Outstanding = Issued − Treasury = 300,000 − 30,000 = 270,000 shares. Outstanding shares represent shares currently held by External shareholders (not the company itself).

1-30.q: When treasury stock is reissued below its cost, the shortfall (cost > proceeds) is first charged to:
o: Net Income (loss)
Retained Earnings
Apic-Treasury Stock (if balance exists), then Retained Earnings
Common Stock account
1-30.e: Reissue below cost: Dr. Cash (proceeds) + Dr. Apic-Treasury (if available) + Dr. Re (if Apic-Treasury exhausted) / Cr. Treasury Stock (cost). The shortfall reduces Apic-Treasury First, then Retained Earnings if Apic-Treasury is insufficient.

1-30.q: A gain or loss on treasury stock transactions is recognized:
o: In Net Income as a gain/loss
In OCI
Never — treasury stock transactions are equity transactions, not income
In Retained Earnings as a direct adjustment
1-30.e: No Gain Or Loss is recognized on treasury stock transactions (purchase or reissuance). They are Equity Transactions — excess above cost goes to Apic; shortfall reduces Apic then Re. Reporting gains/losses on own-stock transactions would allow manipulation of earnings.

1-30.q: Under the Cost Method, treasury stock is recorded at:
o: Par value
Cost (price paid to repurchase) — most common under US GAAP
Book value
Average issued price
1-30.e: Cost method (most common): treasury stock = Cost Paid. Contra-equity at cost. No entry to individual equity components until resale or retirement.

1-30.q: When treasury stock is formally Retired (cost method), any excess of cost over par+Apic is charged to:
o: Apic then Retained Earnings
Operating expense
OCI
Investment loss
1-30.e: Retirement: Dr Common Stock (par), Dr Apic (per share), Dr Retained Earnings (if cost > par+Apic), Cr Treasury Stock (cost). If cost < par+Apic → credit Re. No gain on income statement.

1-30.q: Constructive Retirement of treasury stock means:
o: Stock physically destroyed
Accounted for As If retired using par value method — even if may be reissued
Reached end of useful life
Donated to charity
1-30.e: Par Value Method (constructive retirement): treasury stock immediately recorded as if retired. Less common than cost method.

1-30.q: Donated treasury stock received from a shareholder is recorded at:
o: Zero — no consideration
Fair Value at donation date
Par value
Book value per share
1-30.e: Donated shares → Fair Value at donation date. Dr Treasury Stock (FV), Cr Donated Capital (Apic component).

1-30.q: Purchasing treasury stock Reduces EPS denominator because:
o: Treasury shares earn no dividends
Treasury shares Not Outstanding — excluded from weighted average share count
Company net income decreases
Classified as liabilities
1-30.e: EPS denominator = outstanding shares. Treasury shares (issued but not outstanding) Excluded → fewer shares → EPS increases (assuming unchanged net income).

1-30.q: Share Repurchase journal entry:
o: Dr Investment in own shares, Cr Cash
Dr Treasury Stock (contra-equity), Cr Cash
Dr Retained Earnings, Cr Cash
No entry until cancellation
1-30.e: Repurchase: Dr Treasury Stock (contra-equity at cost paid), Cr Cash. Treasury stock reduces total equity.

1-30.q: Treasury stock on balance sheet is presented as:
o: Current asset
Long-term investment
Deduction From Total Stockholders' Equity
OCI component
1-30.e: Treasury stock = Contra-Equity. Appears in Se section as Deduction. Not an asset — company cannot hold itself.

1-30.q: If treasury stock repurchase price Exceeds book value per share, remaining shareholders' book value per share:
o: Increases — fewer shares
Decreases — paid more than book value
Unchanged
Increases equal to excess paid
1-30.e: Paying More than book value per share: total equity decreases more per share than each repurchased share's book value → remaining book value per share Decreases.

1-30.q: Company data: Authorized 1,000,000; Issued 600,000; Treasury 60,000. Outstanding =
1-30.e: Outstanding = Issued − Treasury = 600,000 − 60,000 = 540,000.

1-30.q: 2-for-1 stock split effect on Treasury Shares:
o: Treasury unaffected — only outstanding split
Treasury Also Doubles — split applies to All issued shares
Treasury cancelled
Converted to authorized-but-unissued
1-30.e: Stock split applies to All Issued shares including treasury. Treasury shares also double; par value per treasury share halves. Treasury Stock account dollar total unchanged.

1-30.q: Under IFRS, treasury shares are treated as:
o: Financial asset at fair value
Contra-Equity deduction — same concept as US GAAP
OCI component
Reduction of share premium only
1-30.e: IFRS (Ias 32): treasury shares deducted from Equity. No gain/loss on purchase, sale, reissuance, or cancellation. Consistent with US GAAP in concept.

1-30.q: Restricted Shares differ from treasury shares because restricted shares:
o: Have been repurchased
Issued To Employees — cannot be sold until vesting conditions met
Held indefinitely by company
Authorized but never issued
1-30.e: Restricted shares = issued to employees as compensation, subject to forfeiture until vesting. They Are outstanding (and dilutive in EPS). Treasury stock = repurchased by company.

1-30.q: Treasury stock purchase effect on Total Equity:
o: Increases equity
Decreases equity by amount paid
No effect — asset replaces cash
Increases equity per share proportionally
1-30.e: Treasury stock purchase: cash leaves (asset ↓) and contra-equity increases. Net effect: total Se Decreases by full purchase price.

1-30.q: When treasury stock is reissued at price Above cost, the excess is:
o: Gain on income statement
Credited To Apic — Treasury Stock transactions never affect income
Credited to retained earnings
Credited to common stock at par
1-30.e: Reissuance above cost: excess → Apic (Paid-In Capital from Treasury Stock). No Gain recognized on income statement. Treasury transactions always go through equity.

1-30.q: A gain or loss on treasury stock transactions is:
o: Always recognized in net income
Recognized only for gains, not losses
Never recognized in income — treasury transactions affect equity only
Recognized in OCI
1-30.e: No Gain or Loss is ever recognized on treasury stock transactions. All differences go through equity accounts (Apic or Retained Earnings), never the income statement.

1-31.q: The Core Principle of ASC 606 is to recognise revenue:
o: When cash is received
When an invoice is sent
When control of the good or service transfers to the customer in the amount of expected consideration
Only for long-term contracts
1-31.e: ASC 606 core principle: recognise revenue when (or as) Control of goods/services Transfers to the customer, in the amount of consideration the entity Expects To Receive. Control transfer (not risks and rewards) is the key trigger.

1-31.q: A good or service is a Distinct performance obligation when:
o: It has a high selling price
The customer can benefit from it on its own And it is separately identifiable from other promises in the contract
It is always delivered separately
Management decides to treat it separately
1-31.e: Distinct = Two criteria Both met: (1) Customer can benefit from it on its own (or with other available resources), And (2) Separately identifiable from other promises in the contract. Both criteria must be met simultaneously.

1-31.q: A company receives $24,000 for a 2-year software subscription on January 1. Under ASC 606, revenue recognised in Year 1 =
o: $0 — recognised at renewal
1-31.e: The subscription is a performance obligation satisfied Over Time (customer simultaneously receives and consumes the benefit). $24,000 / 24 months × 12 months = $12,000 recognised in Year 1. The remaining $12,000 is deferred (contract liability).

1-31.q: A contractor completes 40% of a long-term construction project. Under ASC 606 over-time recognition, revenue recognised =
o: 0% — recognised on completion
40% of transaction price
100% — recognised when contract signed
50% as an estimate
1-31.e: Long-term construction typically qualifies for Over Time recognition (entity performs and customer controls the asset as it is built, or entity has enforceable right to payment for work completed). Recognise based on progress: 40% complete = 40% of total transaction price.

1-31.q: Cash received Before a performance obligation is satisfied creates a:
o: Contract asset
Accounts receivable
Contract liability (deferred revenue)
Revenue immediately
1-31.e: Cash received Before performance = Contract Liability (Deferred Revenue). The company owes the customer a good or service. Revenue is recognised only When the performance obligation is satisfied (when control transfers).

1-31.q: A software bundle: Software Ssp $6,000, Training Ssp $2,000, Support Ssp $4,000. Total Ssp $12,000. Contract price $9,000. Revenue allocated to Software =
1-31.e: Relative Ssp: Software allocation = (6,000/12,000) × $9,000 = $4,500. The $3,000 discount is spread proportionally across all POs based on their standalone selling prices.

1-31.q: Variable consideration in Step 3 should be included in the transaction price Only when:
o: The amount is exactly known
It is highly probable that including it will Not result in a significant revenue reversal
The customer requests it
Management approves it
1-31.e: Variable consideration (bonuses, rebates, penalties, returns) is Constrained — include only to the extent it is Highly Probable that a significant revenue reversal will not occur when uncertainty resolves. This prevents recognition of revenue that might need to be reversed.

1-31.q: A company builds a custom machine for a client. The machine has No alternative use and the company has an enforceable right to payment for work completed. Revenue is recognised:
o: At a point in time when delivered
Over time as construction progresses
Only when full payment received
Only when client accepts the machine
1-31.e: Over-time recognition applies when: the entity's performance Does Not Create an asset with Alternative Use to the entity And the entity has an Enforceable Right To Payment for performance completed to date. Both conditions met here → recognise Over Time.

1-31.q: The Five-Step Model of ASC 606 in order:
o: Identify contract→Allocate→Identify POs→Determine price→Recognize
Identify contract→Identify POs→Determine price→Allocate to POs→Recognize when/as satisfied
Determine price→Identify POs→Allocate→Recognize→Disclose
Identify customer→Determine price→Identify POs→Recognize→Allocate
1-31.e: Step 1: Identify Contract. Step 2: Identify Performance Obligations. Step 3: Determine Transaction Price. Step 4: Allocate to POs by standalone selling price. Step 5: Recognize when/as each Po is satisfied.

1-31.q: Contract Modification adding distinct goods at standalone price is treated as:
o: Modification to original contract
Separate New Contract
Cumulative catch-up
Prospective change to original
1-31.e: New contract when: (1) goods are Distinct and (2) price reflects Standalone Selling Price. Both met → separate new contract.

1-31.q: Company acts as Agent when it:
o: Bears inventory risk
Controls goods before transfer
Arranges for another party to provide goods/services — earns commission
Is the manufacturer
1-31.e: Agent = arranges for another party to satisfy the Po. Revenue = Net (commission). Principal controls the good before transfer. Revenue = Gross.

1-31.q: Bill-And-Hold arrangement qualifies for revenue recognition only if:
o: Invoice has been issued
Customer requested it; goods separately identified; ready for transfer; seller cannot redirect them
Payment received
Delivery within 30 days
1-31.e: All four criteria must be met: (1) Customer Requested; (2) Goods Identified separately; (3) Ready for transfer; (4) Seller Cannot redirect. Then control has transferred.

1-31.q: Revenue on Consignment goods is recognized:
o: When goods shipped to consignee
When Consignee Sells to end customer
When consignor invoices consignee
Ratably over consignment period
1-31.e: Consignee does not control goods — consignor retains control. Revenue recognized only when Consignee Sells to end customer.
